content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Chest pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None.  History of Present Illness: The patient is a ___ year old man with a history of diabetes mellitus type 2, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (catheterization ___: LAD 40% stenosis, RCA 50% stenosis; Echo ___ LVEF>=60; RCA angioplasty ___ who presents with chest pain. . This AM the patient awoke with substernal chest pressure and pain (___) that radiated to his arms bilaterally. It was associated with light-headedness and a sensation of a pounding heartbeat. He notes it felt similar to previous episodes of chest pain except the pounding sensation was new. He took a shower, shaved and notified his daughter (who currently lives at home with him) who prompted him to take his SL nitroglycerin. Approximately 1 hour after his symptoms started, he took 3 SLNG separated by ___ minutes which decreased his symptoms. His daughter drove him to ___ where an EKG was reportedly at baseline and a set of cardiac enzymes were negative. Chest x-ray was read as concerning for left lower lobe pneumonia and he was given azithromycin and ceftriaxone. He was then transferred to ___ for futher care. During this episode, he is unsure if he had breathing difficulty. He had no n/v, diaphoresis. . He has a long history of exertional chest pain, which resolves with rest and/or SLNG. His last episode that he and his wife recalls was in ___. He is usually able to walk ~ ___ mile before having chest discomfort that resolves with sitting down; at times his chest discomfort requires SLNG. No orthopnea, PND, lower extremity swelling. . No recent cough, or fever/chills. He last had a cough in early ___ that was productive of mucous and associated with chills. His PCP prescribed him cough suppressants and his symptoms resolved after ~ 1 week. . In the ED, his vitals were 98.3, 60-70s, 128/56, 20, 96%RA. He was given aspirin. Second set of cardiac enzymes were drawn.  Past Medical History: - Coronary artery disease: status post right coronary artery angioplasty (___) Cardiac cath ___: left anterior descending artery 40% stenosis, right coronary artery 50% stenosis, neither flow limiting, LVEF=60, mild diastolic dysfuction. Echo ___: LVEF>60; mild LA, RA enlargement, mild-moderate TR, mild MR. ___ ___: No fixed/reversible perfusion defects. - Diabetes mellitus type 2: On oral agents. Was followed at ___ by Dr. ___ ___ daily. But in recent months has stopped checking. - Hypertension - hypercholesterolemia - nephrolithiasis - sciatica: MRI ___ right-sided extraforaminal protrusion of the L4-5 intervertebral disc. Currently asymptomatic - cervical spondylosis - status post cholecystectomy  Social History: ___ Family History: Mother: ___. Stroke age ___ Father: ___ MIs, first in his late ___, died of MI age ___ Brother: died of MI age ___  Physical Exam: T 98.7, HR 61, BP 148/66, RR 16, SaO2 99%RA, FSBG 214 General - Resting comfortably in bed, no acute distress HEENT - Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear Neck - Supple, No JVD, carotid 2+ bilat, no bruits CV - RRR, ___ holosystolic murmur at L lower sternal border, heart sounds faint at mitral region Pulm - CTA bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi Abdomen - Normoactive bowel sounds; soft, non-tender, non-distended Ext - Warm, well perfused, radial and DP pulses 2+; no clubbing, cyanosis or edema Neuro - Alert, oriented x3. Language fluent. Slightly frontal. CN2-12 intact. Good strength throughout. Sensation intact to light touch in distal extremities.  Pertinent Results: ___ 07:20AM BLOOD WBC-9.3 RBC-3.99* Hgb-12.0* Hct-35.8* MCV-90 MCH-30.1 MCHC-33.5 RDW-13.5 Plt ___ ___ 03:15PM BLOOD WBC-11.6* RBC-3.87* Hgb-11.8* Hct-35.1* MCV-91 MCH-30.4 MCHC-33.5 RDW-13.3 Plt ___ ___ 03:15PM BLOOD Neuts-74.7* ___ Monos-3.8 Eos-1.1 Baso-0.3 ___ 07:22AM BLOOD ___ PTT-47.8* ___ ___ 03:15PM BLOOD Glucose-232* UreaN-15 Creat-1.0 Na-138 K-4.3 Cl-105 HCO3-23 AnGap-14 ___ 03:15PM BLOOD CK(CPK)-114 ___ 11:55PM BLOOD CK(CPK)-122 ___ 07:20AM BLOOD CK(CPK)-110 ___ 03:15PM BLOOD CK-MB-4 ___ 03:15PM BLOOD cTropnT-<0.01 ___ 11:55PM BLOOD CK-MB-4 cTropnT-<0.01 ___ 07:20AM BLOOD CK-MB-3 cTropnT-<0.01 ___ 06:55AM BLOOD Mg-2.1 Transthoracic Echocardiography ___ The left atrium is mildly dilated. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size and regional/global systolic function are normal (LVEF >55%). Transmitral Doppler and tissue velocity imaging are consistent with Grade I (mild) LV diastolic dysfunction. Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The aortic arch is mildly dilated. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened. There is no valvular aortic stenosis. The increased transaortic velocity is likely related to high cardiac output. No aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. IMPRESSION: Normal global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild diastolic LV dysfunction. Moderate pulmonary hypertension. Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, the findings are similar. Exercise Stress Test ___ - Exercise data Exercise protocol: modified ___ protocol Resting heart rate: 58 Resting blood pressure: 126/60 Exercise Duration: 9.5 min Peak heart rate: 115 Percent maximum predicted heart rate obtained: 78% Peak blood pressure: 174/70 Symptoms during exercise: no anginal type symptoms. Reason exercise terminated: fatigue ECG findings: uninterpretable EKG findings due to LBBB. Good functional exercise capacity. No anginal type symptoms with uninterpretable ECG to achieved workload. Normal hemodynamic response to exercise. - Cardiac Perfusion The image quality is adequate. Left ventricular cavity size is normal (EDV=82ml). Resting and stress perfusion images reveal uniform tracer uptake throughout the left ventricular myocardium. Gated images reveal normal wall motion. The calculated left ventricular ejection fraction is 61%. IMPRESSION: 1. Normal myocardial perfusion study at the level of exercise achieved. 2. Left ventricle with normal size and normal systolic function. Chest X-ray ___: Gallbladder clips otherwise unremarkable.  Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old man with a history of diabetes mellitus type 2, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (catheterization ___: LAD 40% stenosis, RCA 50% stenosis; Echo ___ LVEF>=60; RCA angioplasty ___ who presents with chest pain. 1) Chest Pain Although the patient's symptoms resolved with sublingual nitroglycerin, his presentation was concerning for unstable angina as he had had symptoms at rest. EKG showed new T-wave inversions in the anterior leads. He was treated with aspirin and started on a heparin drip overnight. In the morning, the heparin drip was discontinued as he had 3 sets of negative cardiac enzymes and remained asymptomatic (at rest and with ambulation). Repeat EKG showed resolving EKG abnormalities. A modified ___ stress test was completed which showed good functional exercise capacity without anginal symptoms and normal hemodynamic response to exercise (EKG was uninterpretable in the setting of rate-related left bundle branch block); nuclear perfusion study showed normal myocardial perfusion with normal left ventricular size and systolic function. Transthoracic echocardiography showed: normal global and regional biventricular systolic function; mild diastolic LV dysfunction; and moderate pulmonary hypertension; compared with the prior study of ___, the findings are similar. During his stay, the patient remained asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable. Given the EKG changes, his symptoms were likely cardiac in origin. Cardiology consult felt no intervention or catherization was required at this time. The patient was schedule for a follow-up appointment was this cardiologist Dr. ___. 2) Question of pneumonia At the outside hospital, staff suspected the patient had a pneumonia. At ___, the patient had no fever, no cough, and a normal chest x-ray. Based on this evidence, he was not diagnosed with pneumonia. He did have a mild leukocytosis with mild neutrophilia, which could have been due to transient myocardial ischemia. 3) Coronary artery disease The patient was continued on metoprolol and ezetimibe. His dose of aspirin was increased from 81 mg daily to 325 mg daily. His dose of atorvastatin was increased from 40 mg daily to 80 mg daily. As described above, an exercise tolerance test was normal and an echo showed similar findings to those in ___. 4) ___ mellitus, type II The patient's HbA1c was 8.4 in ___. He was seen by ___ staff to optimize his diabetes regimen. His glyburide dose was increased from 5 mg twice a day to 10 mg twice a day. His metformin dose was increased from 500 mg once a day to 1000 mg twice a day. He was discharged home with visiting nursing to teach him to check his blood glucose levels on his own. He was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with Dr. ___, who the patient had seen in the past for diabetes.  Medications on Admission: Donepezil 5 mg PO HS ___ View Quinapril 40 mg PO DAILY Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID ___ View Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg PO DAILY Atorvastatin 40 mg PO DAILY ___ View Amlodipine 10 mg PO DAILY Ezetimibe 10 mg PO DAILY ___ View Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO DAILY Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY ___ View Toprol XL 100 mg PO DAILY Nitroglycerin SL 0.4 mg SL PRN chest pain Glyburide 5mg BID Januvia 100mg QD Metformin 500 mg QD  Discharge Medications: 1. Donepezil 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime).  2. Quinapril 20 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 3. Omeprazole 20 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO BID (2 times a day). 4. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO DAILY (Daily). 5. Atorvastatin 80 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 6. Amlodipine 5 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 7. Ezetimibe 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 8. Polyethylene Glycol 3350 100 % Powder Sig: One (1) PO Q24H PRN as needed for constipation. 9. Aspirin 325 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 10. Metoprolol Succinate 100 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr PO once a day. 11. Nitroglycerin 0.3 mg Tablet, Sublingual Sig: One (1) Tablet, Sublingual Sublingual PRN (as needed) as needed for chest pain: For chest pain, take every 5 minutes for maximum of 3 doses in 15 minutes. 12. Glyburide 5 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO twice a day. Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 13. Metformin 1,000 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 14. Sitagliptin 100 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.  15. Diabetic Blood Glucose Testing Supplies 1) Lancets - One Touch Ultra - Quantity 300 - Refills 3 2) Strips - One Touch Ultra - Quantity 300 - Refills 3 3) Glucometer - One touch Ultra - Quantity 1 - Refills ___ substitute brand.  Discharge Disposition: Home With Service  Facility: ___  Discharge Diagnosis: Primary diagnosis 1. Coronary artery disease 2. Chest pain Secondary diagnosis 2. Diabetes mellitus type 2  Discharge Condition: Ambulatory. Hemodynamically stable. Maintaining normal oxygen saturation on room air. Chest-pain free.  Discharge Instructions: You were admitting for chest pain. The chest pain was most likely due to your underlying heart disease, but it was found that you did not have a heart attack. You were treated with aspirin and heparin to thin your blood. You did not have any chest pain while you were here. Your medication regimen has changed. Your aspirin dose has increased to 325 mg each day. Your atorvastatin (Lipitor) dose has increased to 80 mg each day. Your glyburide dose has increased to 10 mg twice a day. Your metformin dose has increased to 1000 mg twice a day. Please follow up with your health care providers as listed below. Please return to the ED or call your primary care physician for chest pain, shortness of breath, blood glucose>300, or for any other symptoms which are concerning to you.  Followup Instructions: ___

As described above, an exercise tolerance test was normal and an echo showed similar findings to those in ___. 4) ___ mellitus, type II The patient's HbA1c was 8.4 in ___. His metformin dose was increased from 500 mg once a day to 1000 mg twice a day. He was discharged home with visiting nursing to teach him to check his blood glucose levels on his own. He was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with Dr. ___, who the patient had seen in the past for diabetes.

Discharge Instructions: You were admitting for chest pain. The chest pain was most likely due to your underlying heart disease, but it was found that you did not have a heart attack. You were treated with aspirin and heparin to thin your blood. You did not have any chest pain while you were here. Your medication regimen has changed. Your aspirin dose has increased to 325 mg each day. Your atorvastatin (Lipitor) dose has increased to 80 mg each day. Your glyburide dose has increased to 10 mg twice a day. Your metformin dose has increased to 1000 mg twice a day. Please follow up with your health care providers as listed below. Please return to the ED or call your primary care physician for chest pain, shortness of breath, blood glucose>300, or for any other symptoms which are concerning to you.

Her weight had increased by 30 lbs by the end of ___ and was told to increase her torsemide to twice a day, but her weight continued to increase to 360 lbs by ___ and she started to become more SOB and wheezy. In addition, her BP was noted to be 80/60 and her O2 sat on RA was 92%. She was reluctant to come to the hospital and so the plan was to increase torsemide to 80mg bid if her SBP was >90. However, her SOB continued to get worse and she presented to the ED.  In the ED her VS were 97, 73, 102/59, 19, 100% on 4L NC (on 2L NC at home at baseline). A Foley catheter was placed. She was given 80mg Lasix IV and put out 1400cc. EKG was unchanged from prior. CXR showed mild fluid overload. Bilateral LENIs were negative for DVT. She had no chest pain and was breathing and speaking comfortably. She was admitted for decompensated CHF. Slightly limited study secondary to patient body habitus. No evidence of lower extremity DVT. CHEST (PA & LAT) ___ 9:11 ___ IMPRESSION: A mild congestive failure with stable marked cardiomegaly. Right Upper Ext ultrasound ___: No DVT in the right upper extremity. CXR ___: Marked cardiomegaly is stable. haziness of the perihilar regions is new consistent with mild interstitial pulmonary edema. Right PICC tip is in the proximal SVC. There is no pneumothorax. If any, there is a small left pleural effusion. TTE ___: The left atrium is markedly dilated. The right atrium is markedly dilated. Left ventricular wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity is severely dilated. There is moderate global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = ___ %). There is no ventricular septal defect. The right ventricular free wall is hypertrophied. The right ventricular cavity is dilated with severe global free wall hypokinesis. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. Trace aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is partial mitral leaflet flail (anterior leaflet). At least moderate (2+) mitral regurgitation is seen. Due to the highly eccentric (posterior) trajectory of the regurgitant flow, the severity of mitral regurgitation may be significantly underestimated (Coanda effect). The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly thickened. At least moderate [2+] tricuspid regurgitation is seen. There is severe pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. Compared with the findings of the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, partial flail anterior mitral leaflet is now present. The mitral regurgitation may be increased, and may be significantly underestimated on this study due to the Coanda effect. The left ventricular ejection fraction is increased on the current study. The pulmonary artery pressure is now markedly increased. CXR ___: The tip of the PICC line lies in the mid to upper SVC. The heart remains markedly enlarged but failure is not currently seen.  Brief Hospital Course: ___ with chronic systolic CHF (EF 15%) from non-ischemic cardiomyopathy thought to be due to cocaine abuse, HTN, and morbid obesity admitted with decompensated heart failure and acute renal failure.  # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Patient triggered for hypotension on day of admission with SBP 80 requiring ICU stay, during which time PICC line was placed for access and pt was initiated on furosemide drip at 10mg/hr. Baseline SBP 80-100's during ICU stay for 1 day. Carvedilol was held given hypotension. Lisinopril was restarted at low dose 2.5mg po daily and was tolerating well. Pt aggressively diuresed with lasix drip, uptitrated to 12mg/hr, and spironolactone was added. Diuril 500mg IV was intermittently added for goal neg ___. We repleted lytes aggressively, and pt was maintained on 1.5L fluid restriction as well as a low sodium diet. After diuresing 20kg on the lasix gtt, the patient's creatinine began to climb. She was switched to oral diuretics, but creatinine continued to increase, and all diuretics as well as her lisinopril were held. When creatinine continued to rise, nephrology was consulted and recommended CVVH to help with removing fluid as she was still significantly volume overloaded. She had a catheter placed by ___ and was transferred to the CCU to undergo CVVH. She underwent CVVH and 30kg of fluid was removed. She went down to 273lbs (dry weight felt to be 300lbs per documentation). Her Cr however continued to rise and thus UF was discontinued and further diuresis was held. Lasix was not resumed prior to transfer back to floor. In addition, no afterload reducing agents could be given as patient's BP would not tolerate it; thus isordil and hydralazine were held. Upon return to the floor, further diuretics were held, and patient's Creatinine decreased to 1.7 on the day of discharge. At the time of discharge, she was on torsemide 40mg BID as well as carvedilol 3.125mg BID. Her lisinopril and spirinolactone were being held in the setting of relatively low blood pressures; these agents will likely need to be restarted as an outpatient. # h/o AVNRT & NSVT: Remained in NSR during admission with rare episodes of significant NSVT. Patient had been evaluated by EP for ___ placement, but she was not considered a candidate for pacemaker given her comorbidities & non compliance with medications and appointments. Her electrolytes were repleted aggressively. Although her carvedilol has been held in the setting of hypotension and acute heart failure, it was restarted during CCU course. She was placed on 3.125mg BID. She was given follow-up with EP as an outpatient in the hopes that she might be able to better comply with appointments after her significant diuresis.  # Acute renal failure: Likely hemodynamic due to poor forward flow given her depressed ejection fraction and continued diuresis. Although her creatinine initially improved with diuresis, it began to climb as discussed above. Whe renal was consulted, it was recommended that she undergo CVVH for continued volume removal. She was transferred the CCU and underwent aggressive fluid removal wtih CVVH. However her Cr continued to stay elevated. She did have a hypontensive episode into the ___ and there was concern that she may have ATN ___ hypotension; however urine sediment did not show muddy brown casts. UF was stopped and Lasix held given renal function and monitored. At the time of discharge, her Cr was 1.7 and she was restarted on torsemide 40mg BID. Her weight was 123kg (272-275lbs). She had follow-up with nephrology. # UTI: Patient was found to have a pan-sensitive E coli UTI during her hospital stay. Her Foley catheter was changed and she was started on bactrim on ___. Because bactrim can cause the creatinine to be increased (without actually causing true renal failure), her bactrim was switched to keflex on ___. She completed the abx course. # Asthma/COPD: Continued albuterol, atrovent, and advair.  # Gout: continued home allopurinol  # GERD: Patient was continued on a PPI per her home regimen, but when her creatinine worsened, the PPI was stopped. On the day of discharge, she complained of gastritis pain that improved with pepto bismol. She was discharged on omeprazole, sucralfate, and maalox.  # Depression/anxiety: continued home regimen Celexa # Hyponatremia: Patient had hypovolemic hyponatremia in the setting of significant diuresis. Her sodium improved as her fluid shifted into the vascular space. FULL CODE # Access: Although she had a PICC during her stay, this was removed prior to discharge (especially given her h/o drug abuse). Discharge Instructions: Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight > 3 lbs. Adhere to 2 gm sodium diet. Avoid food from restaurants and frozen foods. Information regarding heart failure management and low sodium diet was reviewed and discussed with you. Fluid Restriction: 1.5 liters You were admitted with congestive heart failure with fluid overload. You were aggressively diuresed with lasix and underwent CVVH to remove several kilograms of fluid. MEDICATION CHANGES: Your torsemide was decreased to 40mg twice a day Your carvedilol was decreased wot 3.125mg twice a day. Your lisinopril was stopped for the time being. It should be restarted some time in the future. We also stopped your spironolactone. We gave you prescriptions for sucralfate and maalox, which can help for your gastritis. Slightly limited study secondary to patient body habitus. No evidence of lower extremity DVT. CHEST (PA & LAT) ___ 9:11 ___ IMPRESSION: A mild congestive failure with stable marked cardiomegaly. Right Upper Ext ultrasound ___: No DVT in the right upper extremity. CXR ___: Marked cardiomegaly is stable. haziness of the perihilar regions is new consistent with mild interstitial pulmonary edema. Right PICC tip is in the proximal SVC. There is no pneumothorax. If any, there is a small left pleural effusion. TTE ___: The left atrium is markedly dilated. The right atrium is markedly dilated. Left ventricular wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity is severely dilated. There is moderate global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = ___ %). There is no ventricular septal defect. The right ventricular free wall is hypertrophied. The right ventricular cavity is dilated with severe global free wall hypokinesis. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. Trace aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is partial mitral leaflet flail (anterior leaflet). At least moderate (2+) mitral regurgitation is seen. Due to the highly eccentric (posterior) trajectory of the regurgitant flow, the severity of mitral regurgitation may be significantly underestimated (Coanda effect). The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly thickened. At least moderate [2+] tricuspid regurgitation is seen. There is severe pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. Compared with the findings of the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, partial flail anterior mitral leaflet is now present. The mitral regurgitation may be increased, and may be significantly underestimated on this study due to the Coanda effect. The left ventricular ejection fraction is increased on the current study. The pulmonary artery pressure is now markedly increased. CXR ___: The tip of the PICC line lies in the mid to upper SVC. The heart remains markedly enlarged but failure is not currently seen.  Brief Hospital Course: ___ with chronic systolic CHF (EF 15%) from non-ischemic cardiomyopathy thought to be due to cocaine abuse, HTN, and morbid obesity admitted with decompensated heart failure and acute renal failure.  # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Patient triggered for hypotension on day of admission with SBP 80 requiring ICU stay, during which time PICC line was placed for access and pt was initiated on furosemide drip at 10mg/hr. Baseline SBP 80-100's during ICU stay for 1 day. Carvedilol was held given hypotension. Lisinopril was restarted at low dose 2.5mg po daily and was tolerating well. Pt aggressively diuresed with lasix drip, uptitrated to 12mg/hr, and spironolactone was added. Diuril 500mg IV was intermittently added for goal neg ___. We repleted lytes aggressively, and pt was maintained on 1.5L fluid restriction as well as a low sodium diet. After diuresing 20kg on the lasix gtt, the patient's creatinine began to climb. She was switched to oral diuretics, but creatinine continued to increase, and all diuretics as well as her lisinopril were held. When creatinine continued to rise, nephrology was consulted and recommended CVVH to help with removing fluid as she was still significantly volume overloaded. She had a catheter placed by ___ and was transferred to the CCU to undergo CVVH. She underwent CVVH and 30kg of fluid was removed. She went down to 273lbs (dry weight felt to be 300lbs per documentation). Her Cr however continued to rise and thus UF was discontinued and further diuresis was held. Lasix was not resumed prior to transfer back to floor. In addition, no afterload reducing agents could be given as patient's BP would not tolerate it; thus isordil and hydralazine were held. Upon return to the floor, further diuretics were held, and patient's Creatinine decreased to 1.7 on the day of discharge. At the time of discharge, she was on torsemide 40mg BID as well as carvedilol 3.125mg BID. Her lisinopril and spirinolactone were being held in the setting of relatively low blood pressures; these agents will likely need to be restarted as an outpatient. # h/o AVNRT & NSVT: Remained in NSR during admission with rare episodes of significant NSVT. Patient had been evaluated by EP for ___ placement, but she was not considered a candidate for pacemaker given her comorbidities & non compliance with medications and appointments. Her electrolytes were repleted aggressively. Although her carvedilol has been held in the setting of hypotension and acute heart failure, it was restarted during CCU course. She was placed on 3.125mg BID. She was given follow-up with EP as an outpatient in the hopes that she might be able to better comply with appointments after her significant diuresis.  # Acute renal failure: Likely hemodynamic due to poor forward flow given her depressed ejection fraction and continued diuresis. Although her creatinine initially improved with diuresis, it began to climb as discussed above. Whe renal was consulted, it was recommended that she undergo CVVH for continued volume removal. She was transferred the CCU and underwent aggressive fluid removal wtih CVVH. However her Cr continued to stay elevated. She did have a hypontensive episode into the ___ and there was concern that she may have ATN ___ hypotension; however urine sediment did not show muddy brown casts. UF was stopped and Lasix held given renal function and monitored. At the time of discharge, her Cr was 1.7 and she was restarted on torsemide 40mg BID. Her weight was 123kg (272-275lbs). She had follow-up with nephrology. # UTI: Patient was found to have a pan-sensitive E coli UTI during her hospital stay. Her Foley catheter was changed and she was started on bactrim on ___. Because bactrim can cause the creatinine to be increased (without actually causing true renal failure), her bactrim was switched to keflex on ___. She completed the abx course. # Asthma/COPD: Continued albuterol, atrovent, and advair.  # Gout: continued home allopurinol  # GERD: Patient was continued on a PPI per her home regimen, but when her creatinine worsened, the PPI was stopped. On the day of discharge, she complained of gastritis pain that improved with pepto bismol. She was discharged on omeprazole, sucralfate, and maalox.  # Depression/anxiety: continued home regimen Celexa # Hyponatremia: Patient had hypovolemic hyponatremia in the setting of significant diuresis. Her sodium improved as her fluid shifted into the vascular space. FULL CODE # Access: Although she had a PICC during her stay, this was removed prior to discharge (especially given her h/o drug abuse).  Medications on Admission: albuterol inhaler Advair 250/50 1 puff bid ASA 325mg qd carvedilol 12.5mg bid torsemide 60mg bid citalopram 30mg qhs simethicone 80mg qid prn Protonix 40mg bid allopurinol ___ qd Zofran 4mg q8h prn Ambien 5mg qhs  Discharge Medications: 1. Discharge Instructions: Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight > 3 lbs. Adhere to 2 gm sodium diet. Avoid food from restaurants and frozen foods. Information regarding heart failure management and low sodium diet was reviewed and discussed with you. Fluid Restriction: 1.5 liters You were admitted with congestive heart failure with fluid overload. You were aggressively diuresed with lasix and underwent CVVH to remove several kilograms of fluid. MEDICATION CHANGES: Your torsemide was decreased to 40mg twice a day Your carvedilol was decreased wot 3.125mg twice a day. Your lisinopril was stopped for the time being. It should be restarted some time in the future. We also stopped your spironolactone. We gave you prescriptions for sucralfate and maalox, which can help for your gastritis. Please take all medications as prescribed. Please call your primary doctor or come to the ED if you develop chest pain, shortness of breath or any other worrisome symptoms. sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: No Drug Allergy Information on File  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Abdominal Pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: EGD  History of Present Illness: ___ y/o hispanic female visiting from ___ who presents with complaints of abdominal pain of of four days duration. Patient lives in the country side of ___, having contact with chickens and cows. She drinks water from a well. She complains of having frequent watery stools over the last year, not noting blood in her stool, but has noted mucous. Her son, who currently lives in ___, has been treated for similar symptoms in the past for amebomas. Patient endorses a 30 pound weight loss of the last year, but says it was intentional to combat her diabetes. She does not describe, however, any restricted diet or exercise to explain the weight. Four days prior to presentation, the patient developed severe ___ epigastric pain. She has noted black stools since that time and has experienced some light headedness. She denies any fever/chills. She has not had any shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitatons. To treat her abdominal pain she was taking NSAIDS.  Past Medical History: Hysterectomy ___ to fibroids Diabetes  Social History: ___ Family History: No history of GI malignancy or other disorders. Son has had ___.  Physical Exam: Vitals: T: 98.1 BP:120/84 HR:71 RR: 12 O2Sat: 98% on RA GEN: Well-appearing, well-nourished, no acute distress HEENT: EOMI, PERRL, sclera anicteric, no epistaxis or rhinorrhea, MMM, OP Clear NECK: No JVD, carotid pulses brisk, no bruits, no cervical lymphadenopathy, trachea midline COR: RRR, no M/G/R, normal S1 S2 PULM: Lungs CTAB, no W/R/R ABD: Soft, mild tenderness in epigastrum, ND, +BS, no HSM, no masses EXT: No C/C/E, no palpable cords NEURO: alert, oriented to person, place, and time. CN II  XII grossly intact. Moves all 4 extremities. Strength ___ in upper and lower extremities. Patellar DTR +1. Plantar reflex downgoing. No gait disturbance. No cerebellar dysfunction. SKIN: No jaundice, cyanosis, or gross dermatitis. No ecchymoses.  Pertinent Results: ___ 06:55PM BLOOD WBC-10.1 RBC-4.53 Hgb-13.1 Hct-38.2 MCV-84 MCH-28.9 MCHC-34.3 RDW-13.2 Plt ___ ___ 06:55PM BLOOD ___ PTT-20.3* ___ ___ 06:55PM BLOOD Glucose-287* UreaN-16 Creat-0.7 Na-138 K-4.4 Cl-100 HCO3-27 AnGap-15 ___ 06:55PM BLOOD ALT-45* AST-30 LD(LDH)-177 AlkPhos-70 TotBili-0.5 ___ 06:55PM BLOOD Albumin-4.6 ___ 06:55PM URINE Blood-NEG Nitrite-NEG Protein-NEG Glucose-1000 Ketone-NEG Bilirub-NEG Urobiln-0.2 pH-5.0 Leuks-NEG 1. Echogenic liver consistent with fatty infiltration. Other forms of liver disease and more serious liver disease such as cirrhosis or fibrosis cannot be excluded. 2. Status post cholecystectomy. No biliary ductal abnormalities. EGD: Findings: Esophagus: Mucosa: Normal mucosa was noted in the whole esophagus. Stomach: Mucosa: Erythema and congestion of the mucosa were noted in the fundus, stomach body and antrum. These findings are compatible with mild gastritis. Cold forceps biopsies were performed for histology at the stomach antrum. Excavated Lesions A few erosions were noted in the antrum. Duodenum: Mucosa: Normal mucosa was noted in the second part of the duodenum. Cold forceps biopsies were performed for histology at the second part of the duodenum. Excavated Lesions A single superficial non-bleeding 1cm ulcer was found in the duodenal bulb. Impression: Normal mucosa in the whole esophagus Erosions in the antrum Erythema and congestion in the fundus, stomach body and antrum compatible with mild gastritis (biopsy) Ulcer in the duodenal bulb Normal mucosa in the second part of the duodenum (biopsy) Otherwise normal EGD to second part of the duodenum  Brief Hospital Course: #) Melena: Description of stools concerning for UGIB. Given use of NSAIDS and epigastric pain, cocern for PUD vs. GU vs. gastritis. Patient's hct fell from 38 to 31 after 2L IVF with guiac + stool. Follow up hcts remained stable. Patient underwent an EGD which revelaled a non-bleeding ulcer of the duodenom and gastritis. Patient was started on PPI BID for 6 weeks. Biopsy results were taken and should be followed up. Patient is scheduled for an outpatinet screening colonoscopy. . #) Abd Pain: Most likely secondary to observed duodenal ulcer. Patient remained free during admissio no evidence of fevers, no abnormality besides fatty liver on ultrasound. Patient had prior ___. LFTs unremakrable. . #) Diahrea: Given chronicity of symptoms, social history, and son with similar symptoms, concern for parasitic source vs IBS vs metformin induced (although she claims not temporally linked.) No elevation of WBC and no eosinophilia. Stool cultures collected prior to discharge for culture, DFA for giardia/cryptosporidia, and O+P. Patient schedueld for follow up with colonoscopy and evaluation by GI. . #) DM: Patient with history of diabetes, taking metformin/glipizde at home. No glucometer and little insight into condition. ___ running btw 200-300 off agents while NPO. Patient was restarted on home agents following EGD. Have arranged follow up with a new PCP for proper education and outpatient manegment.  Medications on Admission: Metformin 500mg BID Glipizide 5mg BID  Discharge Medications: 1. Metformin 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). 2. Glipizide 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). 3. Pantoprazole 40 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO twice a day for 6 weeks. Disp:*90 Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.)(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Primary Diagnosis: Duodenol Ulcer Secondary Diagnosis: Chronic Diahrea  Discharge Condition: Stable  Discharge Instructions: You are being discharged from the hospital after admission for abdominal pain. You were found to be loosing blood in your stool. You underwent a visulaization procedure which showed an ulcer in your small intestine. You are being started on a medication which decreases your acid producetion to help this ulcer heal, which you should take for 6 weeks. Biopsies were taken, and you should follow up the results. YOU SHOULD AVOID TAKING NSAIDS, INCLUDING MOTRIN. Stool samples are being sent for you complaints of chronic diahrea, and you should follow up as outpatient in order to fully evaluate.  Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Epigastric Pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: Ms ___ is a ___ with a medical history significant for chronic abdominal pain and recurrent UTI who presents with severe ___ epigastric pain which she says is more severe than normal and radiates to the epigastrum from the back. Pain began last night after having bowl of noodles. Pain is sharp and radiates to back. Associated with nausea and sensation of bloating.She had one episode of yellow emesis. Last BM and flatus within 24h. She began to notice this pain, though less in severity, just before ___, first noting back pain after ingesting sweet foods. The pain was sudden in onset and prevented her from sleeping. Vomiting began after ___. She would feel nauseous just after ingesting a large meal.  She presented to ___ ___ complaining of epigastric and back pain that comes on most often after meals. Diagnosis was unclear at that time and an US was ordered that showed Prominent CBD measuring 8 mm with no intrahepatic biliary dilatation. She denies any fatigue or additional constitutional symptoms. She does not currently believe she has a UTI (takes prophylactic cipro after sex). Denies recent travel or abnormal foods.  In the ED, initial vs were: 97.4 72 100/74 16 97% ra. Labs were remarkable for WBC 6.8, lactate 0.9, normal LFTs, lipase. CT abdomen showed massive gastric distention. Surgery was consulted who recommended NGT for decompression. Patient was given morphine and ondansetron. Vitals on Transfer: 98 76 98/54 16 98% NGt drained 250 cc and then patient self-d/c'ed.  Review of sytems: (+) Per HPI + weight loss of a couple pounds (-) Denies fever, chills, night sweats. Denies headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denies cough, shortness of breath. Denies chest pain or tightness, palpitations. No recent change in bladder habits. No dysuria. Denies arthralgias or myalgias. Ten point review of systems is otherwise negative.   Past Medical History: recurrent UTI, chronic abdominal pain, mild mitral regurg, No past surgical history  Social History: ___ Family History: negative for GI malignancy  Physical Exam: ON ADMISSION Vitals: T:98.3 BP:102/64 P:67 R:16 O2:99RA General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, ronchi CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops Abdomen: +BS, soft, non-tender, mild distension with no guarding in epigastrium, bowel sounds present, no rebound tenderness or guarding, no organomegaly Ext: Warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or edema Neuro: CN II-XII intact, moves all 4 extrem, 2+ patellar and chilles reflexes b/l ON DISCHARGE Vitals: T:98.5 BP:96/58 P:66 R:18 O2:99RA General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, ronchi CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops Abdomen: +BS, soft, non-tender, no distension, no tenderness, bowel sounds present, no rebound tenderness or guarding, no organomegaly Ext: Warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or edema Neuro: CN II-XII intact, moves all 4 extrem, 2+ patellar and chilles reflexes b/l  Pertinent Results: ON ADMISSION ___ 09:00AM BLOOD WBC-6.4 RBC-4.59 Hgb-14.2 Hct-41.3 MCV-90 MCH-31.0 MCHC-34.4 RDW-12.6 Plt ___ ___ 09:00AM BLOOD Glucose-103* UreaN-16 Creat-0.8 Na-143 K-3.9 Cl-108 HCO3-26 AnGap-13 ___ 09:00AM BLOOD ALT-14 AST-17 AlkPhos-36 TotBili-0.6 ___ 09:00AM BLOOD Albumin-4.7 Calcium-9.4 Phos-3.6 Mg-2.2 ___ 05:30AM BLOOD TSH-1.4 ___ 05:30AM BLOOD %HbA1c-PND ___ 09:18AM BLOOD Lactate-0.8 ON DISCHARGE ___ 05:30AM BLOOD WBC-5.5 RBC-3.92* Hgb-12.2 Hct-35.6* MCV-91 MCH-31.2 MCHC-34.4 RDW-12.3 Plt ___ ___ 01:00PM BLOOD Hct-36.6 ___ 05:30AM BLOOD Glucose-85 UreaN-11 Creat-0.8 Na-138 K-4.0 Cl-111* HCO3-20* AnGap-11 ___ 05:30AM BLOOD ALT-11 AST-14 LD(LDH)-122 AlkPhos-31* TotBili-0.8 ___ 05:30AM BLOOD Albumin-3.8 Mg-2.1 US GALLBLADDER/LIVER IMPRESSION: Normal gallbladder and intra- and extra-hepatic biliary ducts. Marked gastric distention, new since the prior exam. Consider radiograph or CT if there is concern for bowel obstruction. CT Abdomen IMPRESSION: Marked gastric distention and dilation concerning for gastric outlet obstruction. No cause identified. Correlation with endoscopy is recommended.  Brief Hospital Course: ___ with no significant past medical history presents with worsening epigastric pain- found to have massive gastric dilation of US and CT scan. #Epigastric Pain with Gastric Dilation NG Tube was placed and air and 250cc fluid removed from the stomach. Patient felt much better afterwards, and then asked for tube to be removed. Patient was observed overnight where she received IVF. Her diet was advanced as tolerated and before discharge she was able to tolerate solid foods. Labs remained unremarkable. Cause of this distension remains unclear. She may have a dysmotility disorder or a structural abnormality. Causes could include tumor or gallstone ileus. She has ___ with her Primary Care Physician on ___. Transitional Issues -___ with Primary Care Physician ___ -Will need EGD as an outpatient -___ HgA1c -___ Final CT Abdomen Read  Medications on Admission: None  Discharge Medications: None  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Gastric Distension  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: You came to the hospital with belly pain and nausea. You were found to have a very distended stomach. After removing fluid with a nasogastic tube and giving you bowel rest, you improved. The cause of the stomach distension is unclear. You will see a Gastroenterologist as an outpatient to further evaluate. They will likely need to perform more tests/procedures to determine the ultimate cause of your symptoms. When you see your Primary care physician on ___, she should refer you to a Gastroenterologist. It was a pleasure taking care of you, Ms ___.  Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: Anticholinergics,Other / Reglan  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: Patient is a ___ yo woman with history of chronic pancreatitis s/p cholecystectomy and sphincterotomy who presents with 1wk of worsening abdominal pain. As per patient, the pain is intermittent, sharp and ___ in quality. It localizes to her mid/righ upper abdomen and radiates up the chest wall and to the back. Patient finds this pain to be very similar to her prior pancreatitis flare-ups. Denies precipitants including alcohol use, abd. trauma, infections or h/o gallstones. She notes that she is on a restricted diet (no caffeine/fatty food/fried food/dairy) as part of her pancreatitis management. Also notes that pain worsens with meals; and that it is alleviated when NPO, or with NSAIDs and dilaudid. . Patient was initally seen by her PCP ___ ___ for worsening pain. She was treated with PO Zofran and NSAIDs. As pain failed to improve, she presented to ___ ED on ___. In the ED vitals were T 99.6, HR 77, BP 104/56, RR 16, O2 sat 98% on RA. Patient received 8 mg IV morphine and zofran. She was admitted to medicine for pain control.  Past Medical History: 1. Chronic pancreatitis - (First episode ___. Pt has had been laparoscopic cholecystectomy (___), dietary modifications and ERCP w sphincterotomy (___). Most recent flare on ___ pt responded to IV hydration and tylenol). 2. Cholecystectomy (___) 3. Ampulary stricture s/p sphincterectomy ___ 4. s/p R ovary removal 5. Pseudocholinesterase deficiency 6. MODERATE GROWTH.  ___ 4:29 pm Mini-BAL BRONCHIAL LAVAGE. **FINAL REPORT ___ GRAM STAIN (Final ___: 1+ (<1 per 1000X FIELD): POLYMORPHONUCLEAR LEUKOCYTES. NO MICROORGANISMS SEEN. RESPIRATORY CULTURE (Final ___: Commensal Respiratory Flora Absent. YEAST. ~1000/ML. IMAGING ___ CXR: Continued low lung volumes with substantial volume loss in the left lower lobe. Cardiac silhouette remains enlarged though there is little if any vascular congestion. Probable small bilateral pleural effusions. ___ Abd/Pelvic CT: Manifestations of pancreatic and peripancreatic necrosis with acute neccrotic collections. 8.7 cm fluid collection in the left paracolic gutter. 14.8 cm fluid collection in the anterior pelvis. Moderate abdominal free fluid tracking from the upper abdomen to the pelvis with no discrete loculation. New splenic vein thrombosis with interval splenomegaly measures 14.5 cm. Nonocclusive thrombus in the proximal portal vein. Anasarca. Bilateral atelectasis. Left-sided pleural effusion.  Brief Hospital Course: ICU course: Mr. ___ was admitted ___ as a transfer from OSH with septic shock secondary to acute pancreatitis and ischemic bowel. He received rapid and liberal resuscitation and appropriate empiric broad spectrum antibiotics, then underwent exploratory laparotomy, right colectomy and temporary abdominal wall closure soon after arrival. During the initial postoperative period, he continued to receive liberal IVF resuscitation and his fluid status was monitored closely. He returned to the operating room POD1 for a second look, and no further bowel was resected. An end ileostomy was created, and lysis of adhesions done to free up the bowel for this. His abdomen was left open. He was started on enteric feeds via a post-pyloric dobhoff tube early as well as anticipatory Precedex drip for ETOH withdrawal. His sedation was managed by the SICU, and it was a challenge. He required high doses of benzodiazepines for sedation and fentanyl for pain control. By ___, his abdominal wall remained open, and he underwent another exploratory surgery. Mr. ___ was started on lasix for diuresis ___ and the ___ patch was advanced at the bedside in stages, which the patient tolerated well without any worrisome post-procedure increase in peak airway or bladder pressures. ___ he became febrile and repeat CT abdomen revealed evidence of severe pancreatitis and multiple rim-enhancing fluid collections. His intraabodminal drain amylase level was >7000. Our hepatobiliary surgery team was consulted regarding his severe pancreatitis, and likely necrotizing pancreatitis, and they recommended holding off on necrosectomy at that time, as the pancreas did not have foci of air within it and did not appear to be infected. A ___ Patch was placed to enable staged abdominal wall closure and a drain was left in the ___ space. On ___, Mr. ___ returned to the operating rooom and a large intraabodminal abscess was drained. The ___ patch was removed and a biologic mesh placed instead. Several intraabdominal and subcutaneous drains were placed to prevent further abscess development and a wound vac was placed over the mesh to assist with wound healing. The intent was to encourage epithelialization of the wound. By system, here is the remainder of Mr. ___ ICU course: His postoperative course was complicated by persistent hypoxic respiratory failure in setting of pulmonary edema now s/p bedside tracheostomy ___ and high ventilator requirement, septic shock, now on CRRT with improving urine output. Neurologic: Per family, patient at baseline has very limited coping skills and almost no insight into his disease process. Will likely need psychiatric evaluation once stable. Analgesia with tylenol, dilaudid ___ q3h prn. He required precidex and weaned off more than once. Clonidine 0.3 patch weekly, can consider po via post pyloric dobhoff. Ativan given prn anxiety.  Cardiovascular: Septic Shock, resolved. Tachycardic possibly from pain or agitation. Minimal improvement with fluids. Now with reflex tachycardia ___ patch removal and weaning of precidex. Pressors required for septic shock which were weaned off throughout his hospital course. Pulmonary: s/p bedside percutaneous tracheostomy (Perc-fit 8.0, 1 cm above carina). Placed back on the vent on ___ with a RR in the ___. Moderate ARDS improving. CXR stable. Tolerating trach Mask. Passe Muir valve assessment: failed, ___ to trach size. Albuterol given prn.  GI/Abdomen: s/p right colectomy, ileostomy, Dobhoff post-pyloric, biologic mesh overlay sewn to skin edge, multiple drains (likely intra-abdominal source of sepsis). S/p CTAP significant for splenic infarct. Malecot placed over fistula ___. Ostomy appliance placed over on ___ with ostomy nursing. Famotidine 20 IV BID given. ___ drains placed x2 in fluid collection L abd and pelvis. Strict NPO with TPN for nutrition.  Renal: Anion gap metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis) resolved, anuric when in septic shock requiring CRRT, urine o/p improving. Baseline renal function: 0.7. Initially fluid resuscitated and when developed sepsis IVF resuscitated. Overall fluid positive after but had significant fluid drawn off with CRRT. By time of transfer, HLIV.  Infectious Disease: s/p colectomy; some spillage. Continuing to spike fevers, fungal growth in peritoneal fluid. s/p pelvic fluid collection drainage ___, gram stain shows ___, on Micafungin 100 mg IV Q24H and cefepime 2gr IV Q8H, flagyl 500 mg IV Q8H on ___ when spiked. Infectious disease team followed him throughout his ICU stay. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Please ___ your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the Emergency Department for any of the following: *You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or tightness. *New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze. *If you are vomiting and cannot keep down your medications. *You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, increased ostomy output, or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. *You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or in the Ostomy. *You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your urine, or experience a discharge. *Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone within 24 hours. ___ or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or back. *You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius. *Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take any new medications as prescribed. Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times per day. JP Drain Care: *Please look at the site every day for signs of infection (increased redness or pain, swelling, odor, yellow or bloody discharge, warm to touch, fever). *Maintain suction of the bulb. *Note color, consistency, and amount of fluid in the drain. ___ the doctor, ___, or ___ nurse if the amount increases significantly or changes in character. *Be sure to empty the drain frequently. Record the output, if instructed to do so. *You may shower; wash the area gently with warm, soapy water. *Keep the insertion site clean and dry otherwise. *Avoid swimming, baths, hot tubs; do not submerge yourself in water. *Make sure to keep the drain attached securely to your body to prevent pulling or dislocation. PICC Line: *Please monitor the site regularly, and ___ your MD, nurse practitioner, or ___ Nurse if you notice redness, swelling, tenderness or pain, drainage or bleeding at the insertion site. * ___ your MD or proceed to the Emergency Room immediately if the PICC Line tubing becomes damaged or punctured, or if the line is pulled out partially or completely. DO NOT USE THE PICC LINE IN THESE CIRCUMSTANCES. Please keep the dressing clean and dry. Contact your ___ Nurse if the dressing comes undone or is significantly soiled for further instructions. Monitoring Ostomy output/ Prevention of Dehydration: *Keep well hydrated. *Replace fluid loss from ostomy daily. *Avoid only drinking plain water. Include Gatorade and/or other vitamin drinks to replace fluid. *Try to maintain ostomy output between 1000mL to 1500mL per day.  *If Ostomy output >1 liter, take 4mg of Imodium, repeat 2mg with each episode of loose stool. Do not exceed 16mg/24 hours. Wound Vac CARE: .While VAC is in place, please clean around the VAC site and monitor for air leaks of the VAC .A written record of the daily output from the VAC drain should be brought to every follow-up appointment. Your VAC drain will be removed as soon as possible when the daily output tapers off to an acceptable amount and the wound is no longer concerning for ongoing infection .You may shower daily with assistance as needed. .Okay to shower, but no baths until after directed by your surgeon  Followup Instructions: ___

Brief Hospital Course: ICU course: Mr. ___ was admitted ___ as a transfer from OSH with septic shock secondary to acute pancreatitis and ischemic bowel. He received rapid and liberal resuscitation and appropriate empiric broad spectrum antibiotics, then underwent exploratory laparotomy, right colectomy and temporary abdominal wall closure soon after arrival. During the initial postoperative period, he continued to receive liberal IVF resuscitation and his fluid status was monitored closely. He returned to the operating room POD1 for a second look, and no further bowel was resected. An end ileostomy was created, and lysis of adhesions done to free up the bowel for this. His abdomen was left open. He was started on enteric feeds via a post-pyloric dobhoff tube early as well as anticipatory Precedex drip for ETOH withdrawal. His sedation was managed by the SICU, and it was a challenge. He required high doses of benzodiazepines for sedation and fentanyl for pain control. By ___, his abdominal wall remained open, and he underwent another exploratory surgery. Mr. ___ was started on lasix for diuresis ___ and the ___ patch was advanced at the bedside in stages, which the patient tolerated well without any worrisome post-procedure increase in peak airway or bladder pressures. ___ he became febrile and repeat CT abdomen revealed evidence of severe pancreatitis and multiple rim-enhancing fluid collections. His intraabodminal drain amylase level was >7000. Our hepatobiliary surgery team was consulted regarding his severe pancreatitis, and likely necrotizing pancreatitis, and they recommended holding off on necrosectomy at that time, as the pancreas did not have foci of air within it and did not appear to be infected. A ___ Patch was placed to enable staged abdominal wall closure and a drain was left in the ___ space. On ___, Mr. ___ returned to the operating rooom and a large intraabodminal abscess was drained. The ___ patch was removed and a biologic mesh placed instead. Several intraabdominal and subcutaneous drains were placed to prevent further abscess development and a wound vac was placed over the mesh to assist with wound healing. The intent was to encourage epithelialization of the wound. Neurologic: Per family, patient at baseline has very limited coping skills and almost no insight into his disease process. Will likely need psychiatric evaluation once stable. Analgesia with tylenol, dilaudid ___ q3h prn. He required precidex and weaned off more than once. Clonidine 0.3 patch weekly, can consider po via post pyloric dobhoff. Ativan given prn anxiety.  Cardiovascular: Septic Shock, resolved. Tachycardic possibly from pain or agitation. Minimal improvement with fluids. Now with reflex tachycardia ___ patch removal and weaning of precidex. Pressors required for septic shock which were weaned off throughout his hospital course. Pulmonary: s/p bedside percutaneous tracheostomy (Perc-fit 8.0, 1 cm above carina). Placed back on the vent on ___ with a RR in the ___. Moderate ARDS improving. CXR stable. Tolerating trach Mask. Passe Muir valve assessment: failed, ___ to trach size. Albuterol given prn. HEENT: (-)LAD, mucous membranes moist, trachea midline, EOMI, PERRL. CHEST: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, (-) cyanosis. ABDOMEN: (+) BS x 4 quadrants, anterior midline abdominal wound with wound vac on it. EXTREMITIES: Warm, well perfused, pulses palpable, (-) edema
fields.诊疗经过: Brief Hospital Course: ICU course: Mr. ___ was admitted ___ as a transfer from OSH with septic shock secondary to acute pancreatitis and ischemic bowel. He received rapid and liberal resuscitation and appropriate empiric broad spectrum antibiotics, then underwent exploratory laparotomy, right colectomy and temporary abdominal wall closure soon after arrival. During the initial postoperative period, he continued to receive liberal IVF resuscitation and his fluid status was monitored closely. He returned to the operating room POD1 for a second look, and no further bowel was resected. An end ileostomy was created, and lysis of adhesions done to free up the bowel for this. His abdomen was left open. He was started on enteric feeds via a post-pyloric dobhoff tube early as well as anticipatory Precedex drip for ETOH withdrawal. His sedation was managed by the SICU, and it was a challenge. He required high doses of benzodiazepines for sedation and fentanyl for pain control. By ___, his abdominal wall remained open, and he underwent another exploratory surgery. Mr. ___ was started on lasix for diuresis ___ and the ___ patch was advanced at the bedside in stages, which the patient tolerated well without any worrisome post-procedure increase in peak airway or bladder pressures. ___ he became febrile and repeat CT abdomen revealed evidence of severe pancreatitis and multiple rim-enhancing fluid collections. His intraabodminal drain amylase level was >7000. Our hepatobiliary surgery team was consulted regarding his severe pancreatitis, and likely necrotizing pancreatitis, and they recommended holding off on necrosectomy at that time, as the pancreas did not have foci of air within it and did not appear to be infected. A ___ Patch was placed to enable staged abdominal wall closure and a drain was left in the ___ space. On ___, Mr. ___ returned to the operating rooom and a large intraabodminal abscess was drained. The ___ patch was removed and a biologic mesh placed instead. Several intraabdominal and subcutaneous drains were placed to prevent further abscess development and a wound vac was placed over the mesh to assist with wound healing. The intent was to encourage epithelialization of the wound. By system, here is the remainder of Mr. ___ ICU course: His postoperative course was complicated by persistent hypoxic respiratory failure in setting of pulmonary edema now s/p bedside tracheostomy ___ and high ventilator requirement, septic shock, now on CRRT with improving urine output. Neurologic: Per family, patient at baseline has very limited coping skills and almost no insight into his disease process. Will likely need psychiatric evaluation once stable. Analgesia with tylenol, dilaudid ___ q3h prn. He required precidex and weaned off more than once. Clonidine 0.3 patch weekly, can consider po via post pyloric dobhoff. Ativan given prn anxiety.  Cardiovascular: Septic Shock, resolved. Tachycardic possibly from pain or agitation. Minimal improvement with fluids. Now with reflex tachycardia ___ patch removal and weaning of precidex. Pressors required for septic shock which were weaned off throughout his hospital course. Pulmonary: s/p bedside percutaneous tracheostomy (Perc-fit 8.0, 1 cm above carina). Placed back on the vent on ___ with a RR in the ___. Moderate ARDS improving. CXR stable. Tolerating trach Mask. Passe Muir valve assessment: failed, ___ to trach size. Albuterol given prn.  GI/Abdomen: s/p right colectomy, ileostomy, Dobhoff post-pyloric, biologic mesh overlay sewn to skin edge, multiple drains (likely intra-abdominal source of sepsis). S/p CTAP significant for splenic infarct. Malecot placed over fistula ___. Ostomy appliance placed over on ___ with ostomy nursing. Famotidine 20 IV BID given. ___ drains placed x2 in fluid collection L abd and pelvis. Strict NPO with TPN for nutrition.  Renal: Anion gap metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis) resolved, anuric when in septic shock requiring CRRT, urine o/p improving. Baseline renal function: 0.7. Initially fluid resuscitated and when developed sepsis IVF resuscitated. Overall fluid positive after but had significant fluid drawn off with CRRT. By time of transfer, HLIV.  Infectious Disease: s/p colectomy; some spillage. Continuing to spike fevers, fungal growth in peritoneal fluid. s/p pelvic fluid collection drainage ___, gram stain shows ___, on Micafungin 100 mg IV Q24H and cefepime 2gr IV Q8H, flagyl 500 mg IV Q8H on ___ when spiked. Infectious disease team followed him throughout his ICU stay. Culture Data includes: ___: BCx x 2: NG; ___: UCx: Negative; ___ Catheter tip:Ng; ___ BCx x2: NG; ___ UCx: No Growth; ___ Peritoneal Fluid Cx: ___ ___ blood cx x2 PIV/CVL - negative; ___ urine - no growth; ___ sputum - commensal; ___ peritoneal fluid - yeast (prelim: both ___ and non-candidat albicans) & PMNs; ___ blood cx x2 - negative; ___ urine cx - no growth; ___ blood cx x2 - no growth; ___ urine - negative; ___ miniBAL - yeast; ___ blood cx x2 - no growth; ___ urine cx - no growth; ___ urine cx - no growth; ___ Blood cx x2 - no growth; ___ Sputum - yeast; ___ blood cx x2 PIV/PICC - pending; ___ blood, urine, sputum - Urine has yeast; sputum contaminated; blood pending; ___ Peritoneal Fluid - NGTD; ___ Blood culture - pending; ___ blcx, ucx - pending. His antibiotic history includes Micafungin (___), Cefepime (___) (___-), Flagyl (___-), unasyn (___), Meropenem (___), Vancomycin (___), Zosyn ___  ___, Fluconazole ___ - ___, Linezolid (___), Amikacin (___).  Hematology: DVT PPx given with SQH TID, Boots. Hct trended. Vitamin K 5mg given for elevated INR (___). Splenic vein thrombosis. MSK: No issues. Endocrine: ISS; tightened scale ___. Lantus 13 U BID. Then Patient was transferred to the floor on ___ until ___. By system, here is the remainder of Mr. ___ regular floor course: General: He has been active, conscious, cooperative and oriented. Neuro: He has been up and walking without any assistance, pain was initially managed by dPCA and IV break through dilaudid, then, switched to oral oxycodone which controlled to the pain very well. Cardio/respiratory: he has been hemodynamically stable with Vital signs being monitored periodically and were within normal range. He had one episode of chest pain that's been worked out by sending Cardiac enzymes, EKG and chest x-ray and were negative for MI. on ___, Tracheostomy tube has been taken out and patient has had any problem with breathing or speaking. GI/GU: Patient was receiving feeding through TPN and Dobhoff. ___ got pulled out on ___, then diet has been advanced to clears and VAC out put (where the fistulas are) was monitored daily for any changes. The output were high, then patient placed back on strict NPO. multiple trial of advancing diet attempted but unfortunately failed. He has been voiding well without assistance with adequate urine output. He has been having 2 enterocutaneous fistula that being covered by VAC in which has been changing every 3 days. on ___, 2 stitches of vicryl 2.0 has been placed on the lower fistula. Ostomy output was monitored during hospitalization and has been productive. Infectious: he has been on antibiotic all the period of staying until ___, complete blood count and chem 10 have been rottenly monitored. WBC elevated from 16 to 28 on ___, with elevation of temperature. work up for fever has been done as well as CT abdomen and pelvis, PICC has been replaced and the tip has been sent for culture. Antibiotic at this point has been started again. all the cultures came back to be negative for any growth. In the setting of decreasing of WBCs and patient being afebrile decision has been made to discontinue the Antibiotics which was on ___. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was NPO on TPN, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. Wound VAC on mid abdominal wound, 2 drains and T tube were in place. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.
fields.出院情况: Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.
fields.出院医嘱: Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H 2. Artificial Tears ___ DROP BOTH EYES PRN dry eyes 3. Clonidine Patch 0.3 mg/24 hr 1 PTCH TD QWED 4. DiphenhydrAMINE 50 mg IV QHS:PRN insomnia 5. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg PO Q6H 6. Neomycin-Polymyxin-Bacitracin 1 Appl TP PRN with all dressing changes 7. HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain RX *hydromorphone [Dilaudid] 2 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every four (4) hours Disp #*60 Tablet Refills:*0  Discharge Instructions: Please ___ your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the Emergency Department for any of the following: *You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or tightness. *New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze. *If you are vomiting and cannot keep down your medications. *You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, increased ostomy output, or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. *You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or in the Ostomy. *You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your urine, or experience a discharge. *Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone within 24 hours. ___ or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or back. *You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius. *Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take any new medications as prescribed. Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times per day. JP Drain Care: *Please look at the site every day for signs of infection (increased redness or pain, swelling, odor, yellow or bloody discharge, warm to touch, fever). *Maintain suction of the bulb. *Note color, consistency, and amount of fluid in the drain. ___ the doctor, ___, or ___ nurse if the amount increases significantly or changes in character. *Be sure to empty the drain frequently. Record the output, if instructed to do so. *You may shower; wash the area gently with warm, soapy water. *Keep the insertion site clean and dry otherwise. *Avoid swimming, baths, hot tubs; do not submerge yourself in water. *Make sure to keep the drain attached securely to your body to prevent pulling or dislocation. PICC Line: *Please monitor the site regularly, and ___ your MD, nurse practitioner, or ___ Nurse if you notice redness, swelling, tenderness or pain, drainage or bleeding at the insertion site. * ___ your MD or proceed to the Emergency Room immediately if the PICC Line tubing becomes damaged or punctured, or if the line is pulled out partially or completely. DO NOT USE THE PICC LINE IN THESE CIRCUMSTANCES. Please keep the dressing clean and dry. Contact your ___ Nurse if the dressing comes undone or is significantly soiled for further instructions. Monitoring Ostomy output/ Prevention of Dehydration: *Keep well hydrated. *Replace fluid loss from ostomy daily. *Avoid only drinking plain water. Include Gatorade and/or other vitamin drinks to replace fluid. *Try to maintain ostomy output between 1000mL to 1500mL per day.  *If Ostomy output >1 liter, take 4mg of Imodium, repeat 2mg with each episode of loose stool. Do not exceed 16mg/24 hours. Wound Vac CARE: .While VAC is in place, please clean around the VAC site and monitor for air leaks of the VAC .A written record of the daily output from the VAC drain should be brought to every follow-up appointment. Your VAC drain will be removed as soon as possible when the daily output tapers off to an acceptable amount and the wound is no longer concerning for ongoing infection .You may shower daily with assistance as needed. .Okay to shower, but no baths until after directed by your surgeon

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: seasonal  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Infiltrating ductal carcinoma, right breast.  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Right total mastectomy.  Past Medical History: Hypothyroid Depression Insomnia Allergic rhinitis Osteoporosis Left nares cyst/polyp, B12 deficiency Rectocele (___) PSH: Right total mastectomy ___.  Social History: ___ Family History: non-contributory  Physical Exam: Vitals:98.3,68,118/66, 20, 95% room air General:no acute distress, alert and oriented x3, Cardiac:regular rate rhythm, no mumur, rubs or gallops Pulmonary: clear to auscultate bilaterally,no wheeze,no rhonchi Abdomen:soft nontender, nondistended, no rebound guarding,normoactive bowel sounds Wound:mastectomy incision steri-strips clean, dry, intact, no erythema, no hematoma.JP drain intact to bulb suction with serosanguineous drainage Extremities:+2 dorsalis pedis, no edema  Pertinent Results: none  Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with right breast infiltrating ductal carcinoma,who is status post right total mastectomy under general anesthesia. The procedure was completed with minimal blood loss and no complications. After surgery, the patient was brought to the recovery room in good condition and admitted to inpatient general surgery unit and was hemodynamically stable. The diet was advanced from clears to regular which was tolerated well. The patient's pain was well-controlled with oral pain medication. The JP drain was intact to bulb suction with serosanguineous drainage. The mastectomy site appeared clean, dry and intact with steri-strips in place. There was no evidence of erythema, hematoma or induration. The patient was evaluated by ___ and cleared for discharge. Of note on postoperative day 2 had episode of epistaxis and the heparin Sc was discontinued. The patient was discharged home with ___ for drain care.  Medications on Admission: Proair 90 Levothyroxine 75 mg PO QD Mirtazapine 15 mg Po QD  Discharge Medications: 1. levothyroxine 88 mcg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 2. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/Actuation HFA Aerosol Inhaler Sig: Two (2) Puff Inhalation Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for wheezing. 3. mirtazapine 15 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime). 4. acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO TID (3 times a day). 5. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain. Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 6. Colace 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO twice a day.  Discharge Disposition: Extended Care  Facility: ___  Discharge Diagnosis: Infiltrating ductal carcinoma,right breast.   Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker or cane).  Discharge Instructions: Personal Care: 1. Leave your mastectomy/chest incision open to air. You may cover with a clean, dry dressing daily if this is more comfortable 2. Clean around the drain site(s), where the tubing exits the skin, with soap and water. 3. Strip drain tubing, empty bulb(s), and record output(s) ___ times per day. 4. A written record of the daily output from your drain should be brought to every follow-up appointment. Your drain will be removed as soon as possible when the daily output tapers off to an acceptable amount. 5. You may shower daily. No baths until instructed to do so by ___ 6. You may wear a soft, non-restrictive camisole but no tight or underwire bras. Activity: 1. You may resume your regular diet. 2. DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in strenuous activity until instructed by ___. Medications: 1. Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise and take any new meds as ordered. 2. You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol for mild pain as directed on the packaging. Please note that Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient so do not take these meds with additional Tylenol. 4. Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by tylenol. 5. Take your antibiotic as prescribed. 6. Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking the prescription pain medication. You may use a different over-the-counter stool softener if you wish. 7. Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that are high in fiber. Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the following: 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, welling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual drainage from the incision(s). 2. A large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s). 3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF 4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication. Return to the ER if: * If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your medications. * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or anything else that is troubling you. * Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. DRAIN DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS You are being discharged with drains in place. Drain care is a clean procedure. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before performing drain care. Perform drainage care twice a day. Try to empty the drain at the same time each day. Pull the stopper out of the drainage bottle and empty the drainage fluid into the measuring cup. Record the amount of drainage fluid on the record sheet. Reestablish drain suction.   Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: NEUROLOGY  Allergies: Codeine / Demerol / Plavix / Percocet / Omeprazole / Metoprolol / Morphine  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Gait instability  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: HPI: ___ is a ___ old woman with longstanding diabetes (which has been poorly controlled), hypertension, coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, lumbar radiculopathy and a long history of smoking who presented to the ED after one week of progressive gait problems and frequent falls. Patient is poor historian, and she described progressive worsening gait for the past 10 days, characterized by tremor (high amplitude proximal body tremor), wide based gait and tendency to lean forward. Patient feels off balance and she denied weakness or numbness. Patient denied vertigo or dizziness. Patient has presented frequent falls and today she fell twice. She has well established diabetic neuropathy and she saw Dr ___ in clinic in ___ and according to previous documentation patient has severe neuropathy with abnormal physical exam. In addition she has been followed by pain clinic due to L5 radiculopathy with steroid injections. ROS: The pt denied headache, loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus or hearing difficulty. Denied difficulties producing or comprehending speech. No bowel or bladder incontinence or retention. Patient lost 10 Lbs snce ___. Denied cough, shortness of breath. Denied chest pain or tightness, palpitations. Denied arthralgias or myalgias. Denied rash.   Past Medical History: 1. Diabetes Type II (last A1C 10%) 2. HTN 3. Hypothyroidism 4. Major depressive disorder 5. Anxiety with agoraphobia 6. CAD s/p stent ___ mid LCx, mid and distal RCA) 7. CHF (EF 40%) 8. Asthma 9. Hypothyroidism 10. Hyperlipidemia  Social History: ___ Family History: Mother had ___. Father died of MI (age of death uncertain).  Physical Exam: Vitals: afebrile, BP120\80mmHg Hr 88 General: Awake, cooperative, NAD. Mal-nourished woman HEENT: NC/AT, no scleral icterus noted, MMM, no lesions noted in oropharynx Neck: Supple, no carotid bruits appreciated. No nuchal rigidity Pulmonary: Lungs CTA bilaterally without R/R/W Cardiac: RRR, nl. S1S2, no M/R/G noted Abdomen: soft, NT/ND, normoactive bowel sounds, no masses or organomegaly noted. Neurologic: -Mental Status: Alert, oriented x 3. Able to relate history without difficulty. Language is fluent with intact repetition and comprehension. Normal prosody. There were no paraphasic errors. Speech was not dysarthric. Able to follow both midline and appendicular commands. There was no evidence of apraxia or neglect. CN I: not tested II,III: VFF to confrontation, pupils 4mm->2mm bilaterally, fundi normal III,IV,VI: EOMI, no ptosis.
