content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: Vaginal prolapse and urinary incontinence  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: TVH/I- STOP Sling, AP colphorrhaphy, cysto, IUD removal  History of Present Illness: Ms. ___ is a ___, gravida 5, para 5, who was referred to me by Dr. ___ for vaginal prolapse. The patient was examined and was found to have a second degree uterine prolapse, second degree vault prolapse and third degree rectocele. She was referred for multichannel urodynamics testing which showed that she had stress urinary incontinence with urethral hypermobility with a low pressure urethra. The decision was made to proceed with surgical management. The risks, benefits and alternatives to surgery were explained to the patient.  Past Medical History: PAST OB HISTORY G5P___ Vaginal: 5 1 Forceps Largest Vaginal delivery: 9.5 lbs. PAST GYN HISTORY She denies ever having Chlamydia, Gonorrhea, Syphilis, Genital Herpes, Trichomonas, Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) or HIV. She denies having an abnormal Pap test and her last one was on ___ She denies having an abnormal Mammogram and her last one was on ___. Her last colonoscopy was in ___ She is Perimenopausal and has an IUD She denies using vaginal estrogen cream. She denies post-menopausal bleeding. PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: 1.Anxiety/Depression 2.Arthritis 3.Hypercholesterolemia 4.Low back pain PAST SURGICAL HISTORY 1.Tubal ligation  Social History: ___ Family History: non-contributory  Physical Exam: On arrival to floor: VS 96.1 ___ 16 99% RA I/O including OR/PACU: sips/crackers PO + 2175cc IVF / 525 UOP + 250 EBL NAD, visiting comfortable Lungs: CTAB Cardiac: +S1, +S2, no M/R/G Abdomen: obese, sort, nondistrended, nontender, +BS Incision: mons C/D ___: Foley in place, clear of bloody urine Extremitites: no erythema, no edema  Pertinent Results: ___ 05:55AM BLOOD WBC-8.1 RBC-3.31* Hgb-10.2* Hct-30.0* MCV-91 MCH-30.7 MCHC-33.9 RDW-12.5 Plt ___ ___ 05:55AM BLOOD Glucose-104* UreaN-8 Creat-0.8 Na-140 K-3.8 Cl-103 HCO3-31 AnGap-10 ___ 05:55AM BLOOD Calcium-8.4 Phos-3.5 Mg-1.7  Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ underwent an uncomplicated total vaginal hysterectomy, I-stop sling, AP colporrhaphy, cystoscopy, and IUD removal for urinary incontinence and prolapse; see operative report for details. She had a brief episode of anxiety in the PACU which responded to intravenous Ativan. She was unable to void spontaneously so the foley was replaced on postoperative day 1 and she was given training to go home with a foley and leg bag in place. Otherwise, she had an uncomplicated recovery and was discharged home on postoperative day #1 in good condition: ambulating without difficulty, tolerating a regular diet, with adequate pain control using PO medication, and with instructions to follow up with Dr. ___ for foley removal.  Medications on Admission: BUPROPION HCL 300mg daily, BUSPIRONE 5mg twice daily, CITALOPRAM 40mg daily, FLUTICASONE 50 mcg - ___ sprays(s) in each nostril every day LORAZEPAM - 1 mg Tablet - 1 Tablet(s) by mouth at bedtime ___ add ___ to 1 during daytime as needed for anxiety  Discharge Medications: 1. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2* 2. oxycodone-acetaminophen ___ mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain: maximum daily Tylenol (acetaminophen) is 4000mg, each Percocet contains 325mg Tylenol (acetaminophen). . Disp:*50 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 3. ibuprofen 600 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for pain. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 4. bupropion HCl 150 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: Two (2) Tablet Sustained Release PO QAM (once a day (in the morning)). 5. buspirone 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). 6. fluticasone 50 mcg/Actuation Spray, Suspension Sig: Two (2) Spray Nasal DAILY (Daily). 7. citalopram 20 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).  8. nitrofurantoin (macrocryst25%) 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day) for 14 days. Disp:*28 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0* 9. Pyridium 200 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO three times a day as needed for dysuria for 3 days. Disp:*9 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: pelvic organ prolapse stress urinary incontinence intrinsic sphincter deficiency  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Call your doctor for: * fever > 100.4 * severe abdominal pain * difficulty urinating * vaginal bleeding requiring >1 pad/hr * abnormal vaginal discharge * redness or drainage from incision * nausea/vomiting where you are unable to keep down fluids/food or your medication General instructions: * Take your medications as prescribed. * Do not drive while taking narcotics. * No strenuous activity, nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no douching, no sex), no heavy lifting of objects >10lbs for 6 weeks or until follow-up w your doctor. * You may eat a regular diet. . Incision care: * You may shower and allow soapy water to run over incision; no scrubbing of incision. No bath tubs for 6 weeks.  Followup Instructions: ___  出院情况：Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

She had a brief episode of anxiety in the PACU which responded to intravenous Ativan. She was unable to void spontaneously so the foley was replaced on postoperative day 1 and she was given training to go home with a foley and leg bag in place. Otherwise, she had an uncomplicated recovery and was discharged home on postoperative day #1 in good condition: ambulating without difficulty, tolerating a regular diet, with adequate pain control using PO medication, and with instructions to follow up with Dr. ___ for foley removal. Disp:*50 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 3. ibuprofen 600 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for pain. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 4. bupropion HCl 150 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: Two (2) Tablet Sustained Release PO QAM (once a day (in the morning)). 5. buspirone 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). 6. fluticasone 50 mcg/Actuation Spray, Suspension Sig: Two (2) Spray Nasal DAILY (Daily). 7. citalopram 20 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).  8. nitrofurantoin (macrocryst25%) 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day) for 14 days. Disp:*28 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0* 9. Pyridium 200 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO three times a day as needed for dysuria for 3 days. Disp:*9 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: pelvic organ prolapse stress urinary incontinence intrinsic sphincter deficiency  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Call your doctor for: * fever > 100.4 * severe abdominal pain * difficulty urinating * vaginal bleeding requiring >1 pad/hr * abnormal vaginal discharge * redness or drainage from incision * nausea/vomiting where you are unable to keep down fluids/food or your medication General instructions: * Take your medications as prescribed. * Do not drive while taking narcotics. * No strenuous activity, nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no douching, no sex), no heavy lifting of objects >10lbs for 6 weeks or until follow-up w your doctor. * You may eat a regular diet. . Incision care: * You may shower and allow soapy water to run over incision; no scrubbing of incision. No bath tubs for 6 weeks. content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: seasonal  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Bright red blood per rectum  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None.  History of Present Illness: This is a ___ female with h/o multiple diverticular bleeds, s/p partial sigmoidectomy with ___ takedown (___), subtotal colectomy with side-to-end ileosigmoid anastomosis (___) and two polyps s/p removal, who presented with one cup of bright red blood per rectum around 3 am (___). Ms. ___ was at her baseline state of health until developing some nausea yesterday evening about three hours after dinner. She vomited food remnants, non-bilious, non-bloody, fell asleep, and then woke at 3AM with an urge to void and defecate. She noticed about 1 cup of bright red blood in the bowl, no clots, no stool. She had no pain at that time, denies associated symptoms like lightheadedness, dizziness, palpitations, feeling weak/fainting, changes in vision. She has not had any diarrhea or changes in stool habits. She denies any reflux symtoms. She has not used alcohol, tobacco, NSAIDs, coffee. She drinks an occasional cup of tea, sometimes peppermint flavored, and some chocolates. Of note, she has been hospitalized 10 times since ___, including with BRBPR from ___ for diverticular bleed, when she required 8 units PRBCs, 2 units FFP, 1 unit platelets, and one related to PUD in ___. Many of the others have been in relation to revision of a AAA (6.7 cm) graft with persistent endoleak. She was scheduled for an open revision/replacement of her aortic graft this coming week. She has had dark stools over the past few weeks that she attributes to taking iron. She has not felt weak/fatigued - feels full of energy. Denies recent travel, antibiotics, sick contacts, or changes in diet.  In the ED, initial VS were 98.0, 71, 113/73, 16, 100 RA. Two large-bore IVs placed: 16G in R wrist, 18G in R hand. Typed and screened. Hct was 36.3 and BUN was slightly elevated at 23. CTA abd/pelvis showed no evidence of aorto-enteric fistula, but did show multiple diverticuli in leftover colon, and continuing type 2 endoleak. Vascular surgery saw her in the ED and felt her GIB was highly unlikely to be related to her AAA history; GI saw her and felt that since her crit was stable and was not actively bleeding, she could be admitted for non-urgent scope. On arrival to the floor, patient reports feeling at her baseline except for a funny taste in her mouth from the oral contrast. Denies lightheadedness, weakness. She denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, fever, and chills.  Past Medical History: - Known diverticulosis-related bleeds s/p sigmoidectomy with colostomy and s/p takedown in ___ ___ last hospitalized at ___ ___ for diverticular bleed, when she required 8 units PRBCs, 2 units FFP, 1 unit platelets. - Diverticulitis - all hospitalizations at ___ - H/o ascending colonic polyp (2.5 cm) on colonoscopy, ___, not amenable to endoscopic removal, s/p removal via subtotal colectomy - S/p subtotal colectomy with ileosigmoid anastomosis, ___, ___, complicated by superficial seroma and possible anastamotic leak (fluid/inflammation seen on CT ___. - H/o rectal polyp seen on colonoscopy ___, s/p polypectomy - + H Pylori - ___, s/p antibiotic tx; per pt, has since tested negative - AAA measuring 6.7 cm - S/p EVAR ___, ___ - S/p EVAR revision for type 1 endoleak and type 2 endoleak with enlarging sac, ___, ___, with persistence of type 2 leak - S/p percutaneous stent graft repair for type 1 leak of the left common iliac artery, ___, ___ - Attempted coil embolization for persistent type 2 endoleak without success (___) - still present. - HTN - Pancreatitis several years ago - Small bowel ventral hernia, s/p reduction ___, ___  Social History: ___ Family History: No history of malignancy, IBD; brother has diverticulosis; multiple family members with HTN and DM.  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM: VS: 97.5, 110/79 (90-130/54-88), 54-75, 20, 100% RA GEN: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress HEENT: Hair of average texture, scalp without lesions. Sclera anicteric, conjunctivae pink. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharyngeal mucosa normal in appearance, without inflammation, exudates, or lesions. Neck: Supple. No anterior cervical, submental, submandibular, or supraclavicular LAD appreciated. Thorax and lungs: Breathing comfortably on RA, with no retractions. Lungs CTAB, with no wheezes/rhonchi/rales. Cardiovascular: RRR, normal S1/S2, no M/R/G. No JVD appreciated. Abdomen: Central bruit appreciated. Multiple surgical scars. Soft, obese, ND, and mostly NT abdomen, except to deep palpation in LLQ (per baseline, says pt). Normoactive bowel sounds. No masses or organomegaly were appreciated. Rectal: No external hemorrhoids. No masses or hard stool on digital rectal exam. Stool guiaic negative. Extremities: WWP, with 2+ radial and DP pulses. No ___ edema appreciated. DISCHARGE PHYSICAL EXAM: VS: 98.6, 113/75 (101-132/62-84), 85 (58-99), 18, 98% RA Orthostatics: 124/79 and 80 lying, 132/84 and 99 sitting, 113/75 and 85 standing. I/O: NPO/BR GEN: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress HEENT: Hair of average texture, scalp without lesions. Sclera anicteric, conjunctivae pink. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharyngeal mucosa normal in appearance, without inflammation, exudates, or lesions. Neck: Supple. No anterior cervical, submental, submandibular, or supraclavicular LAD appreciated. Thorax and lungs: Breathing comfortably on RA, with no retractions. Lungs CTAB, with no wheezes/rhonchi/rales. Cardiovascular: RRR, normal S1/S2, no M/R/G. No JVD appreciated. Abdomen: Central bruit appreciated. Multiple surgical scars. Soft, obese, ND, and mostly NT abdomen, except to deep palpation in LLQ (per baseline, says pt). Normoactive bowel sounds. No masses or organomegaly were appreciated. Rectal: No external hemorrhoids. No masses or hard stool on digital rectal exam. Stool guiaic negative. Extremities: WWP, with 2+ radial and DP pulses. No ___ edema appreciated.  Pertinent Results: ___ 04:15AM BLOOD WBC-7.9 RBC-4.89 Hgb-12.7 Hct-36.3 MCV-74* MCH-26.0* MCHC-35.0 RDW-16.4* Plt ___ ___ 11:35AM BLOOD Hct-35.3* ___ 08:55PM BLOOD Hct-33.3* ___ 06:40AM BLOOD WBC-4.7 RBC-4.49 Hgb-11.7* Hct-33.5* MCV-75* MCH-26.0* MCHC-34.8 RDW-16.1* Plt ___ ___ 04:15AM BLOOD Glucose-118* UreaN-23* Creat-0.9 Na-141 K-4.4 Cl-102 HCO3-29 AnGap-14 ___ 06:40AM BLOOD Glucose-80 UreaN-16 Creat-0.7 Na-144 K-3.4 Cl-107 HCO3-28 AnGap-12 CT abd and pelvis (___): IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of aortoenteric fistula. 2. Type 2 endoleak is again demonstrated with stable native aortic aneurysmal sac compared to ___. 3. Postoperative changes consistent with ileosigmoid anastomosis. 4. Multiple diverticula, but no diverticulosis. 5. Soft tissue density within the anterior abdominal wall likely expected post-surgical change. 6. Right lower quadrant omental infarct, stable from the prior examination. 7. Bilateral hypodense renal lesions representing simple cyst, cyst with clacification and some too small to characterize. Cortical thinning in bilateral kidneys.  COLONOSCOPY (___): Sessile polyp at 60cm (injection) Previous end to end left hemi-colectomy at 20cm Diverticulosis of the whole colon Otherwise normal colonoscopy to cecum I. Colon specimen, subtotal colectomy (A-Q), ___: 1. Adenomas, two, 1.7 and 0.1 cm, respectively. 2. Diverticular disease. 3. Resection margins free of adenoma. 4. Unremarkable appendix. 5. Sixteen unremarkable lymph nodes (___). EGD (___): Mild gastritis COLONOSCOPY (___): Previous ileo-colonic anastomosis of the colon Diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon Polyp in the rectum (polypectomy) Rectal polyp, polypectomy (___): Hyperplastic polyp.  Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ female with history of multiple diverticular bleeds, three suspicious colonic polyps in the last year (two of which one were adenomas), now s/p subtotal colectomy with side-to-end ileosigmoid anastomosis (___), who presented with 1 cup of blood per rectum on the morning of admission. # Hematochezia: In a woman whose anatomy is so altered (and colonic surface area and transit time is substantially reduced), BRBPR could represent either an upper or a lower GI source. Given that she has a known history of diverticulosis, PUD, and colonic polyposis, these were foremost on the differential. She has had significant trouble with diverticulosis in the past, requiring surgical removal of most of her colon in ___ and ___. Diverticuli were still present diffusely on the remaining colon she has by CT; per report, this bleeding was painless and similar in appearance to her bleeding episodes with diverticulosis in the past. This was the leading item on our differential. She has three known polyps (two were found to be adenomatous) that have been removed (two by subtotal colectomy, other via polypectomy). She developed both within a short period of time -- the two colonoscopies were conducted within the past year. This could have potentially represented another polyp or adenoma, though less likely, given known diverticular burden. With respect to PUD, she had few risk factors by history (including no NSAIDs, coffee, tobacco, alcohol use) -- she only drinks a moderate amount of tea. She is also now s/p antibiotic treatment for H. pylori (___), and has reportedly tested negative for it since then. This was low on our differential. Aorto-enteric fistula in a patient with a history of an abdominal aortic aneurysm or an aortic graft should always be considered. However, this was not seen on the CT. Angiodysplasia is much rarer, and would be a diagnosis of exclusion here, since Ms. ___ has many more pertinent pathological entities that could account for this. Ischemic, ulcerative colitis not likely per history; no evidence of anorectal source. Did not bled again while in the hospital; reassured by stability of Hct here (see results). Since she did not rebleed while in-patient, neither GI (who was following the pt) nor vascular surgery felt that a colonoscopy with potential intervention would be productive, as the culprit diverticulum had likely spontaneously stopped bleeding. While here, we maintain access on the pt with two large-bore PIVs (R hand, R wrist). She was typed and screened. We monitored Hct TID. We held her home aspirin, lisinopril, and amlodipine. She was maintained NPO for 15 hours; once we felt secure that she was hemodynamically stable, we advanced her to a regular diet on the morning of discharge. She tolerated this well, without nausea, vomiting, abd pain, or diarrhea. # AAA revision: Planned surgery on ___: EXPLANT STENT GRAFT; REPAIR AORTIC ANEURYSM; RETROPERITONEAL Pt contacted anesthesia dept regarding hospitalization; they conducted her scheduled pre-surgical evaluation at the bedside on ___ instead of in the clinic. Still planning for AAA graft revision next week; vascular surgery aware and following. Vascular surgery aware and following. # HCM: - Held aspirin. - Continued iron, vit D, B, multivit. # HTN: - Held lisinopril, amlodipine in the setting of a GIB in order to maintain ability to increase HR in case of massive bleed. Restarted on discharge. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Planned surgery on ___: EXPLANT STENT GRAFT; REPAIR AORTIC ANEURYSM; RETROPERITONEAL  Medications on Admission: Preadmission medications listed are correct and complete. Information was obtained from PatientFamily/CaregiverwebOMR. 1. Amlodipine 5 mg PO DAILY 2. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY 3. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO DAILY 4. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 5. Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY 6. Vitamin B Complex 1 CAP PO DAILY 7. Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY 8. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY  Discharge Medications: 1. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO DAILY 2. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 3. Vitamin B Complex 1 CAP PO DAILY 4. Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY 5. Amlodipine 5 mg PO DAILY 6. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY 7. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 8. Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Diverticulosis Hematochezia  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Dear Ms. ___, You were admitted to the BI after passing a cup of blood into your toilet bowl during the night on ___. You were monitored here, and were found to have a stable blood count (hematocrit) over time, and were not seen to pass further blood through your rectum while you were here. You were initially maintained without food or drink by mouth, so as not to aggravate the bleeding. However, on the morning of ___, you were able to advance your diet without trouble. We think the primary reason for your bleed was diverticulosis, from which you have experienced bleeding before. We saw extensive evidence of diverticuli in your remaining colon on the CT scan of your abdomen. We think the bleed has resolved on its own. We touched base with vascular surgery as well as the gastrointestinal team, and in coordination with them, we decided that a colonoscopy would not help very much at this stage, because we would attempt to do anything (such as clipping) to the diverticuli that you are known to have. We would not be able to identify which diverticulum the bleed was coming from since it is no longer bleeding. At this time, we are fairly comfortable that the bleed has stopped. This morning, you will proceed with the anesthesia evaluation for the surgery you have scheduled for next week, and then will have this procedure as planned. We made no changes in your medications. It was a pleasure taking part in your care.  Followup Instructions: ___

Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ female with history of multiple diverticular bleeds, three suspicious colonic polyps in the last year (two of which one were adenomas), now s/p subtotal colectomy with side-to-end ileosigmoid anastomosis (___), who presented with 1 cup of blood per rectum on the morning of admission. # Hematochezia: In a woman whose anatomy is so altered (and colonic surface area and transit time is substantially reduced), BRBPR could represent either an upper or a lower GI source. Given that she has a known history of diverticulosis, PUD, and colonic polyposis, these were foremost on the differential. She has had significant trouble with diverticulosis in the past, requiring surgical removal of most of her colon in ___ and ___. Diverticuli were still present diffusely on the remaining colon she has by CT; per report, this bleeding was painless and similar in appearance to her bleeding episodes with diverticulosis in the past. This was the leading item on our differential. She has three known polyps (two were found to be adenomatous) that have been removed (two by subtotal colectomy, other via polypectomy). She developed both within a short period of time -- the two colonoscopies were conducted within the past year. This could have potentially represented another polyp or adenoma, though less likely, given known diverticular burden. With respect to PUD, she had few risk factors by history (including no NSAIDs, coffee, tobacco, alcohol use) -- she only drinks a moderate amount of tea. She is also now s/p antibiotic treatment for H. pylori (___), and has reportedly tested negative for it since then. This was low on our differential. Aorto-enteric fistula in a patient with a history of an abdominal aortic aneurysm or an aortic graft should always be considered. However, this was not seen on the CT. Angiodysplasia is much rarer, and would be a diagnosis of exclusion here, since Ms. ___ has many more pertinent pathological entities that could account for this. Ischemic, ulcerative colitis not likely per history; no evidence of anorectal source. Did not bled again while in the hospital; reassured by stability of Hct here (see results). Since she did not rebleed while in-patient, neither GI (who was following the pt) nor vascular surgery felt that a colonoscopy with potential intervention would be productive, as the culprit diverticulum had likely spontaneously stopped bleeding. While here, we maintain access on the pt with two large-bore PIVs (R hand, R wrist). She was typed and screened. We monitored Hct TID. We held her home aspirin, lisinopril, and amlodipine. She was maintained NPO for 15 hours; once we felt secure that she was hemodynamically stable, we advanced her to a regular diet on the morning of discharge. Restarted on discharge. Discharge Instructions: Dear Ms. ___, You were admitted to the BI after passing a cup of blood into your toilet bowl during the night on ___. You were monitored here, and were found to have a stable blood count (hematocrit) over time, and were not seen to pass further blood through your rectum while you were here. You were initially maintained without food or drink by mouth, so as not to aggravate the bleeding. However, on the morning of ___, you were able to advance your diet without trouble. We think the primary reason for your bleed was diverticulosis, from which you have experienced bleeding before. We saw extensive evidence of diverticuli in your remaining colon on the CT scan of your abdomen. We think the bleed has resolved on its own. We touched base with vascular surgery as well as the gastrointestinal team, and in coordination with them, we decided that a colonoscopy would not help very much at this stage, because we would attempt to do anything (such as clipping) to the diverticuli that you are known to have. We would not be able to identify which diverticulum the bleed was coming from since it is no longer bleeding. At this time, we are fairly comfortable that the bleed has stopped. This morning, you will proceed with the anesthesia evaluation for the surgery you have scheduled for next week, and then will have this procedure as planned. We made no changes in your medications. It was a pleasure taking part in your care.  Followup Instructions: ___

fields.入院情况: History of Present Illness: This is a ___ female with h/o multiple diverticular bleeds, s/p partial sigmoidectomy with ___ takedown (___), subtotal colectomy with side-to-end ileosigmoid anastomosis (___) and two polyps s/p removal, who presented with one cup of bright red blood per rectum around 3 am (___). Ms. ___ was at her baseline state of health until developing some nausea yesterday evening about three hours after dinner. She vomited food remnants, non-bilious, non-bloody, fell asleep, and then woke at 3AM with an urge to void and defecate. She noticed about 1 cup of bright red blood in the bowl, no clots, no stool. She had no pain at that time, denies associated symptoms like lightheadedness, dizziness, palpitations, feeling weak/fainting, changes in vision. She has not had any diarrhea or changes in stool habits. She denies any reflux symtoms. She has not used alcohol, tobacco, NSAIDs, coffee. She drinks an occasional cup of tea, sometimes peppermint flavored, and some chocolates. Of note, she has been hospitalized 10 times since ___, including with BRBPR from ___ for diverticular bleed, when she required 8 units PRBCs, 2 units FFP, 1 unit platelets, and one related to PUD in ___. Many of the others have been in relation to revision of a AAA (6.7 cm) graft with persistent endoleak. She was scheduled for an open revision/replacement of her aortic graft this coming week. She has had dark stools over the past few weeks that she attributes to taking iron. She has not felt weak/fatigued - feels full of energy. Denies recent travel, antibiotics, sick contacts, or changes in diet.  In the ED, initial VS were 98.0, 71, 113/73, 16, 100 RA. Two large-bore IVs placed: 16G in R wrist, 18G in R hand. Typed and screened. Hct was 36.3 and BUN was slightly elevated at 23. CTA abd/pelvis showed no evidence of aorto-enteric fistula, but did show multiple diverticuli in leftover colon, and continuing type 2 endoleak. Vascular surgery saw her in the ED and felt her GIB was highly unlikely to be related to her AAA history; GI saw her and felt that since her crit was stable and was not actively bleeding, she could be admitted for non-urgent scope. On arrival to the floor, patient reports feeling at her baseline except for a funny taste in her mouth from the oral contrast. Denies lightheadedness, weakness. She denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, fever, and chills.  Past Medical History: - Known diverticulosis-related bleeds s/p sigmoidectomy with colostomy and s/p takedown in ___ ___ last hospitalized at ___ ___ for diverticular bleed, when she required 8 units PRBCs, 2 units FFP, 1 unit platelets. - Diverticulitis - all hospitalizations at ___ - H/o ascending colonic polyp (2.5 cm) on colonoscopy, ___, not amenable to endoscopic removal, s/p removal via subtotal colectomy - S/p subtotal colectomy with ileosigmoid anastomosis, ___, ___, complicated by superficial seroma and possible anastamotic leak (fluid/inflammation seen on CT ___. - H/o rectal polyp seen on colonoscopy ___, s/p polypectomy - + H Pylori - ___, s/p antibiotic tx; per pt, has since tested negative - AAA measuring 6.7 cm - S/p EVAR ___, ___ - S/p EVAR revision for type 1 endoleak and type 2 endoleak with enlarging sac, ___, ___, with persistence of type 2 leak - S/p percutaneous stent graft repair for type 1 leak of the left common iliac artery, ___, ___ - Attempted coil embolization for persistent type 2 endoleak without success (___) - still present. - HTN - Pancreatitis several years ago - Small bowel ventral hernia, s/p reduction ___, ___  Social History: ___ Family History: No history of malignancy, IBD; brother has diverticulosis; multiple family members with HTN and DM.  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM: VS: 97.5, 110/79 (90-130/54-88), 54-75, 20, 100% RA GEN: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress HEENT: Hair of average texture, scalp without lesions. Sclera anicteric, conjunctivae pink. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharyngeal mucosa normal in appearance, without inflammation, exudates, or lesions. Neck: Supple. No anterior cervical, submental, submandibular, or supraclavicular LAD appreciated. Thorax and lungs: Breathing comfortably on RA, with no retractions. Lungs CTAB, with no wheezes/rhonchi/rales. Cardiovascular: RRR, normal S1/S2, no M/R/G. No JVD appreciated. Abdomen: Central bruit appreciated. Multiple surgical scars. Soft, obese, ND, and mostly NT abdomen, except to deep palpation in LLQ (per baseline, says pt). Normoactive bowel sounds. No masses or organomegaly were appreciated. Rectal: No external hemorrhoids. No masses or hard stool on digital rectal exam. Stool guiaic negative. Extremities: WWP, with 2+ radial and DP pulses. No ___ edema appreciated.

PATHOLOGY: DIAGNOSIS: Bone, right hand, biopsy (A): 1. Bone with extensive reparative/regenerative changes. 2. No significant acute inflammation.  Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with 2 month history of right wrist infection with multiple debridements, admitted for osteomyelitis and chronic pain. . # Osteomyelitis: Pt has 2 month history of R wrist wound, has had multiple debridements at other hospitals with questions of compliance ___ the past. She presented with MRI concerning for osteomyelitis. Pt underwent debridement on ___ and had wound VAC placed to right wrist wound. On ___ she underwent further debridement as well as a metacarpophalangeal joint capsulorrhaphy and a wound vac dressing was placed. On ___, patient had skin graft placed to her right wrist wound (from a left thigh donor site) and wound vac dressing was placed over this for constant pressure to graft. This was left ___ place until ___ when the vac dressing was removed to reveal a beautiful graft site with 100% take. ___ graft site was dressed with xeroform dressing and gauze and her wrist was immediately casted to protect the graft site, and to be removed ___ Hand Clinic after 3 weeks. Regarding antibiotic therapy, the patient was initially started on daptomycin and clindamycin based on cultures and sensitivities from tissue culture, but was then changed to ceftriaxone by ID due to a questionable penicillin allergy. The goal of ceftriaxone therapy is to be 6 weeks. A PICC line was placed to accomodate long term antibiotic therapy. Patient will be followed closely by ID upon discharge via weekly outpatient lab work and close outpatient follow up visits. . # pain management: Pt has history of chronic pain, has been referred to pain clinic ___ the past at ___ but has not kept any appointments. Based on outside pharmacy records, pt has filled prescriptions for >500 oxycodone tablets ___ the past 2 months, mostly prescribed from physician at outside pain clinic. Her pain has been difficult to control ___ house. Patient often threatened to sign out AMA if her pain medications were not managed and prescribed to her liking. Patient has allergies to motrin and toradol so these were not options. She often refused tylenol reporting that it didn't 'work' for her. Ultimately, patient was managed on a combination of IV morphine and oxycodone which she reported worked well for her. IV morphine was discontinued on ___ and patient was given oxycondone 10mg q4h PRN which she was requesting every four hours. When wound vac was taken down over skin graft site and patient was placed ___ a cast, Dr. ___ attempted to encourage patient to take just tylenol as her pain should be less and less, the further she gets out from her surgery but she refused stating she had deep bone pain ___ her wrist. She, again, stated that tylenol doesn't work for me. Dr. ___ explained that she would be discharged home with a script for oxycodone that will last for 3 weeks and that she will not be prescribed anymore oxycodone during these next 3 weeks. Patient verbalized understanding of this pain management plan. Patient actively denies having a substance abuse problem even when offered professional assistance. . # seizure disorder: Pt with reported seizure disorder following trauma many years ago, however on presentation her phenytoin level was undetectable. She presented with MRI concerning for osteomyelitis. Pt underwent debridement on ___ and had wound VAC placed to right wrist wound. On ___ she underwent further debridement as well as a metacarpophalangeal joint capsulorrhaphy and a wound vac dressing was placed. On ___, patient had skin graft placed to her right wrist wound (from a left thigh donor site) and wound vac dressing was placed over this for constant pressure to graft. This was left ___ place until ___ when the vac dressing was removed to reveal a beautiful graft site with 100% take. ___ graft site was dressed with xeroform dressing and gauze and her wrist was immediately casted to protect the graft site, and to be removed ___ Hand Clinic after 3 weeks. Regarding antibiotic therapy, the patient was initially started on daptomycin and clindamycin based on cultures and sensitivities from tissue culture, but was then changed to ceftriaxone by ID due to a questionable penicillin allergy. The goal of ceftriaxone therapy is to be 6 weeks. A PICC line was placed to accomodate long term antibiotic therapy. Patient will be followed closely by ID upon discharge via weekly outpatient lab work and close outpatient follow up visits. . # pain management: Pt has history of chronic pain, has been referred to pain clinic ___ the past at ___ but has not kept any appointments. Based on outside pharmacy records, pt has filled prescriptions for >500 oxycodone tablets ___ the past 2 months, mostly prescribed from physician at outside pain clinic. Her pain has been difficult to control ___ house. Patient often threatened to sign out AMA if her pain medications were not managed and prescribed to her liking. Patient has allergies to motrin and toradol so these were not options. She often refused tylenol reporting that it didn't 'work' for her. Ultimately, patient was managed on a combination of IV morphine and oxycodone which she reported worked well for her. IV morphine was discontinued on ___ and patient was given oxycondone 10mg q4h PRN which she was requesting every four hours. When wound vac was taken down over skin graft site and patient was placed ___ a cast, Dr. ___ attempted to encourage patient to take just tylenol as her pain should be less and less, the further she gets out from her surgery but she refused stating she had deep bone pain ___ her wrist. She, again, stated that tylenol doesn't work for me. Dr. ___ explained that she would be discharged home with a script for oxycodone that will last for 3 weeks and that she will not be prescribed anymore oxycodone during these next 3 weeks. Patient verbalized understanding of this pain management plan. Patient actively denies having a substance abuse problem even when offered professional assistance. . # seizure disorder: Pt with reported seizure disorder following trauma many years ago, however on presentation her phenytoin level was undetectable. We continued her on her reported home dose of dilantin and monitored levels. At one point, her level was supratherapeutic, so her dosing was decreased to TID and then again to BID. When she was discharged, her level was therapeutic at 13.2. Patient had no seizure activity during her inpatient stay. . # Psych: Patient was evaluated by our psych service ___ the ER, upon first arrival (see Psych evaluations ___ OMR for details). Patient was also seen by our Social work service to help with coordination of care for her mother (who lives at home with her and is disabled) while patient spent an extended stay ___ the hospital. Communication with the ___ mother's social worker, ___, at ___, were initiated as well when coordinating plan of care. ___ mother was able to spend time ___ a skilled nursing facility during the time that patient was at ___. Both the medicine attending and Dr. ___ spoke with ___ outpatient psychiatrist Dr. ___ (___) who confirmed that the patient is very dedicated to her mother and has a had a difficult time caring for herself since her mother returned home post-stroke. He also confirmed that patient has never had a formal psychiatric diagnosis. Dr. ___ patient to make an appointment with Dr. ___ (___) who specializes ___ working with patients similar to her and who would likely be able to give the patient a formal psychiatric diagnosis. She would also continue to see Dr. ___, as she has been ___ the past. Patient agreed to this plan prior to her discharge. . Patient was discharged home on hospital day #27 with a right wrist cast ___ place with digits pink, warm and mobile. No evidence of digit or forearm swelling. Patient had a patent left arm PICC line ___ place. She also had a small left thigh skin graft donor site with a drying xeroform dressing ___ place. She had a foley catheter ___ place, as well.

Disp:*30 piggyback* Refills:*2* 5. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day) as needed for constipation: over the counter stool softener. 6. senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day) as needed for constipation: over the counter vegetable laxative. 7. trazodone 50 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO HS (at bedtime) as needed for insomnia. 8. phenytoin 50 mg Tablet, Chewable Sig: Two (2) Tablet, Chewable PO BID (2 times a day). 9. prochlorperazine maleate 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q8H (every 8 hours) as needed for nausea. Disp:*90 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 10. acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO TID (3 times a day): do not exceed 4 grams ___ 24 hours . 11. gabapentin 300 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO HS (at bedtime). Disp:*30 Capsule(s)* Refills:*1* 12. ondansetron 4 mg Tablet, Rapid Dissolve Sig: One (1) Tablet, Rapid Dissolve PO every eight (8) hours as needed for nausea. Disp:*40 Tablet, Rapid Dissolve(s)* Refills:*0* 13. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO every six (6) hours as needed for pain for 3 weeks. Disp:*168 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Instructions: Dear Ms ___, It was a pleasure taking care of you at ___ ___. You were admitted for an infected right wrist, with suspicion that the infection has spread to your bone. Throughout your admission, you had multiple debridements and wound vac placements by plastic surgery. While on the medicine service, we managed your pain with IV and oral medications, and treated your infection with antibiotics. We determined that you were not likely allergic to cephalosporins, so we gave you an antibiotic called ceftriaxone. You went to the OR for debridements of your right wrist wound and then eventual coverage with a skin graft taken from your left thigh and were transferred to plastic surgery service. -You should continue with your IV antibiotic, ceftriaxone, as prescribed. Infectious Disease will be following you for this medication and the labs involved. It is very important to follow up with them and attend all appointments so you are able to complete you therapy safely. -Elevate your right arm as much as possible and maintain it ___ your cast for the next three weeks, until your follow up ___ Hand Clinic with Dr. ___ Dr. ___. -Please keep your right hand/cast dry -Please keep your left thigh 'donor' site dry and let the dressing fall off when it's ready. - If your right arm/hand begins to worsen after discharge home with an acute increase ___ swelling or pain, please call the Hand Clinic at the number given and ask to speak with a doctor. . Medications: * Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise. * You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol for mild pain as directed on the packaging. Please note that Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient so do not take these meds with additional Tylenol. * Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by tylenol. * Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking the prescription pain medication to prevent constipation. You may use a different over-the-counter stool softerner if you wish. * You should continue drinking fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that are high ___ fiber. . Return to the ER if: * If you are vomiting and cannot keep ___ fluids or your medications. * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or anything else that is troubling you.

Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ s/p repeat LTCS ___ at 28w5d for worsening HELLP. She had initially presented for elevation of new onset elevated blood pressures and was initially asymptomatic. Her prior obstetric history was notable for severe pre-eclampsia and pulmonary edema requiring delivery at 34 weeks. She had severe range blood pressures and ruled in for severe pre-eclampsia, was started on Magnesium. She developed acute episode of epigastric pain radiating to her mid-scapular area. She had a RUQ ultrasound that demonstrated pleural effusions bilaterally and edematous gallbladder, thought to be a sign of acute hepatitis/third-spacing. Also noted to have acute worsening of LFTs, from 50-60s on ___ AM to 300s ___ ___, and platelets from 139 to 64, all consistent with HELLP. She underwent repeat LTCS on ___, which was overall uncomplicated, EBL 750cc. Magnesium was discontinued after 2 hours given finding of pleural effusion and history of pulmonary edema. On post-op day#3 (___), she developed worsening RUQ pain, worse than at time of admission. On exam, was noted to have RUQ tenderness and palpable firmness. RUQ U/S demonstrated new large subcapsular hematoma, 18cm in maximum dimension. ACS consulted and recommended bedrest, serial exams, serial CBC, and CT scan. Hct trended and stable between ___. VSS. Given potential for hepatic rupture and hemodynamic instability warranting possible need for urgent laparotomy/ACS eval, decision was made to transfer patient to ___ intensive care unit for close monitoring. Her pain had been moderately well controlled with PO dilaudid (holding NSAIDs given ___ and Tylenol given transaminitis/hematoma). Shedid spike a low grade fever ___ ___, but defervesced quickly and had no other localizing signs or symptoms of infection, so an inflammatory response was suspected. Urine culture was normal. Blood cultures were sent and were negative. Her leukocytosis was stable at around 18. For her HELLP/pre-eclampsia, LFTs initially remained elevated but the downtrended. For her blood pressures, she required initiation and uptitration of PO Nifedipine to BID dosing. For ___, Cre stabilized at 2.6-2.7 and then improved. Renal following and suspected underlying acute tubular necrosis. Her urine output remained excellent. On ___ (POD#4) she was transferred to the SICU on the ___ ___ for observation. She was transferred back on ___. On ___ (POD#5), she was transferred out to the post partum floor, where her course was as follows: *) severe preeclampsia/HELLP/Subcapsular hematoma - s/p Magnesium x2h (d/c'ed early) - nifedipine for HTN - per ACS, no activity restrictions and monitor for signs of hepatic rupture *) ___ - voiding spontaneously, monitor Is&Os *) Postpartum - regular diet - anemia ___ surgery, hemolysis, hematoma: s/p 1 unit pRBC ___, 1 unit ___ for Hct 21.5 *) PPX: SCH. Pboots -___ (POD#6) urine cx neg, daily labs ordered, Upcr per renal request ordered ALT 336 AST 128 Cr 2.2 Hct 23.7, plt 189. She was placed on tele and had q 4 hour vital signs. -___ (POD#7) BP 160/95, Nifedipine CR increased to 30mg po BID Triggered for pulse in 150s, repeat labs stable. Repeat RUQ scan stable. Plan CXR and CTA if possible to administer contrast safely to r/o PE. Temp- 101.6 (around ___, 95% O2 sat, CXR showed right pleural effusion and cannot r/o pneumonia ID consulted- started on Cefepime/Vanco empiric treatment for presumed hospital acquired pneumonia. CTA scheduled after getting IVF x 4 hours (250cc/hour) prelim read of CTA showed no PE, still cannot r/o pneumonia. Final CTA read: no evidence for pulmonary embolism, Large right effusion with complete collapse of the right lower lobe, increased in size, Re-demonstration of a large subcapsular hematoma compressing the right lobe of the liver, Mediastinal and left hilar lymph nodes measuring up to 1 cm. She did have swollen lips after antibiotics, given Benadryl and swelling/itching resolved. ___ blood cultures NGTD. ___ (POD#8) A chest tube was placed by Interventional Pulmonology for the right pleural effusion. labs cr 2.0, hct 25.5 plt 344, phos elevated so she was started on a low phos diet. Overnight T 100.5. ___ urine culture negative, blood cultures NGTD and pleural fluid culture NGTD ___ (POD#9) The chest tube was removed. nifedipine CR decreased to 30 mg po QD as bps lower (avoid BPs being too low so that keep up renal perfusion), BLE dopplers negative, labs stable plt 405, phos still elevated. CRP elevated (197). ID recommended MRI of abdomen/pelvis given persistent low grade temps ___ (POD#10) MRI postponed to ___ per Renal to optimize GFR given need for contrast); creat down to 1.7; Hct 23.5 stable; WBC 11.8. ALT 60,AST 34, Phos 4.4 ___ radiology did not feel pt needed repeat pelvic/abdominal MRI, no venous thrombosis, HCT-23, plts-397, ALT- 52, AST- 36 phos- 4.0, WBC-10.4, pt asymptomatic, taken off telemetry. Tmax- 99, BPs ___ ___: ID saw patient and felt that she no longer needed any antibiotics or repeat MRI. General surgery also signed off and felt patient was stable for d/c home.  Medications on Admission: daily PNV ASA 81 mg po qd  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen ___ mg PO Q6H:PRN Pain - Mild RX *acetaminophen 500 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every 6 hours Disp #*50 Tablet Refills:*1 2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID Constipation RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*60 Tablet Refills:*1 3. NIFEdipine CR 30 mg PO DAILY RX *nifedipine 30 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth DAILY Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 4. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q3H:PRN Pain - Moderate RX *oxycodone 5 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every ___ hours Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Severe Preeclampsia, HELLP Syndrome Pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and pleural effusions Subcapsular liver hematoma Acute Kidney Injury  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Please refer to your discharge packet and the instructions below: C/S: Nothing in the vagina for 6 weeks (No sex, douching, tampons) No heavy lifting for 6 weeks Do not drive while taking narcotics (i.e. Oxycodone, Percocet) Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (tylenol) in 24 hrs Do not take more than 2400mg ibuprofen in 24 hrs Please call the on-call doctor at ___ if ___ develop shortness of breath, dizziness, palpitations, fever of 100.4 or above, abdominal pain, increased redness or drainage from your incision, nausea/vomiting, heavy vaginal bleeding, or any other concerns. ___ were diagnosed with severe pre-eclampsia (HELLP syndrome) during this admission. Because of the pre-eclampsia, ___ developed fluid in and around your lungs (pulmonary edema and pleural effusions). ___ had a chest tube placed to drain this fluid by the lung doctors. ___ also developed a pneumonia and ___ were treated with IV antibiotics. ___ developed several fevers and we were initially concerned with blood clots. However, ___ had a CT scan done of your chest, ultrasound of the legs, as well as an MRI of the pelvis which did not show any blood clots. ___ were seen by the infectious disease doctors ___. ___ also developed a large hematoma under the liver capsule and ___ were monitored in the ICU. Your liver function tests have since improved. ___ were seen by the general surgeons here. In addition, your kidneys were stressed with abnormal function which has since also improved. ___ were seen by the kidney doctors ___. ___ also became anemic during this admission and ___ had a blood transfusion. For your pre-eclampsia, we monitored your blood pressures closely and they were well controlled with the Nifedipine. ___ will be continuing this medication when ___ go home. ___ have recovered well and are safe to be discharged home.  Followup Instructions: ___

CRP elevated (197). ID recommended MRI of abdomen/pelvis given persistent low grade temps ___ (POD#10) MRI postponed to ___ per Renal to optimize GFR given need for contrast); creat down to 1.7; Hct 23.5 stable; WBC 11.8. ALT 60,AST 34, Phos 4.4 ___ radiology did not feel pt needed repeat pelvic/abdominal MRI, no venous thrombosis, HCT-23, plts-397, ALT- 52, AST- 36 phos- 4.0, WBC-10.4, pt asymptomatic, taken off telemetry. Acetaminophen ___ mg PO Q6H:PRN Pain - Mild RX *acetaminophen 500 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every 6 hours Disp #*50 Tablet Refills:*1 2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID Constipation RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*60 Tablet Refills:*1 3. NIFEdipine CR 30 mg PO DAILY RX *nifedipine 30 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth DAILY Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 4. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q3H:PRN Pain - Moderate RX *oxycodone 5 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every ___ hours Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Severe Preeclampsia, HELLP Syndrome Pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and pleural effusions Subcapsular liver hematoma Acute Kidney Injury  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Please refer to your discharge packet and the instructions below: C/S: Nothing in the vagina for 6 weeks (No sex, douching, tampons) No heavy lifting for 6 weeks Do not drive while taking narcotics (i.e. Oxycodone, Percocet) Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (tylenol) in 24 hrs Do not take more than 2400mg ibuprofen in 24 hrs Please call the on-call doctor at ___ if ___ develop shortness of breath, dizziness, palpitations, fever of 100.4 or above, abdominal pain, increased redness or drainage from your incision, nausea/vomiting, heavy vaginal bleeding, or any other concerns. ___ were diagnosed with severe pre-eclampsia (HELLP syndrome) during this admission. Because of the pre-eclampsia, ___ developed fluid in and around your lungs (pulmonary edema and pleural effusions). ___ had a chest tube placed to drain this fluid by the lung doctors. ___ also developed a pneumonia and ___ were treated with IV antibiotics. ___ developed several fevers and we were initially concerned with blood clots. However, ___ had a CT scan done of your chest, ultrasound of the legs, as well as an MRI of the pelvis which did not show any blood clots. ___ were seen by the infectious disease doctors ___. ___ also developed a large hematoma under the liver capsule and ___ were monitored in the ICU. Your liver function tests have since improved. ___ were seen by the general surgeons here. In addition, your kidneys were stressed with abnormal function which has since also improved. ___ were seen by the kidney doctors ___. ___ also became anemic during this admission and ___ had a blood transfusion. For your pre-eclampsia, we monitored your blood pressures closely and they were well controlled with the Nifedipine. ___ will be continuing this medication when ___ go home. ___ have recovered well and are safe to be discharged home.  Followup Instructions: ___

fields.诊疗经过: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ s/p repeat LTCS ___ at 28w5d for worsening HELLP. She had initially presented for elevation of new onset elevated blood pressures and was initially asymptomatic. Her prior obstetric history was notable for severe pre-eclampsia and pulmonary edema requiring delivery at 34 weeks. She had severe range blood pressures and ruled in for severe pre-eclampsia, was started on Magnesium. She developed acute episode of epigastric pain radiating to her mid-scapular area. She had a RUQ ultrasound that demonstrated pleural effusions bilaterally and edematous gallbladder, thought to be a sign of acute hepatitis/third-spacing. Also noted to have acute worsening of LFTs, from 50-60s on ___ AM to 300s ___ ___, and platelets from 139 to 64, all consistent with HELLP. She underwent repeat LTCS on ___, which was overall uncomplicated, EBL 750cc. Magnesium was discontinued after 2 hours given finding of pleural effusion and history of pulmonary edema. On post-op day#3 (___), she developed worsening RUQ pain, worse than at time of admission. On exam, was noted to have RUQ tenderness and palpable firmness. RUQ U/S demonstrated new large subcapsular hematoma, 18cm in maximum dimension. ACS consulted and recommended bedrest, serial exams, serial CBC, and CT scan. Hct trended and stable between ___. VSS. Given potential for hepatic rupture and hemodynamic instability warranting possible need for urgent laparotomy/ACS eval, decision was made to transfer patient to ___ intensive care unit for close monitoring. Her pain had been moderately well controlled with PO dilaudid (holding NSAIDs given ___ and Tylenol given transaminitis/hematoma). Shedid spike a low grade fever ___ ___, but defervesced quickly and had no other localizing signs or symptoms of infection, so an inflammatory response was suspected. Urine culture was normal. Blood cultures were sent and were negative. Her leukocytosis was stable at around 18. For her HELLP/pre-eclampsia, LFTs initially remained elevated but the downtrended. For her blood pressures, she required initiation and uptitration of PO Nifedipine to BID dosing. For ___, Cre stabilized at 2.6-2.7 and then improved. Renal following and suspected underlying acute tubular necrosis. Her urine output remained excellent. On ___ (POD#4) she was transferred to the SICU on the ___ ___ for observation. She was transferred back on ___. On ___ (POD#5), she was transferred out to the post partum floor, where her course was as follows: *) severe preeclampsia/HELLP/Subcapsular hematoma - s/p Magnesium x2h (d/c'ed early) - nifedipine for HTN - per ACS, no activity restrictions and monitor for signs of hepatic rupture *) ___ - voiding spontaneously, monitor Is&Os *) Postpartum - regular diet - anemia ___ surgery, hemolysis, hematoma: s/p 1 unit pRBC ___, 1 unit ___ for Hct 21.5 *) PPX: SCH. Pboots -___ (POD#6) urine cx neg, daily labs ordered, Upcr per renal request ordered ALT 336 AST 128 Cr 2.2 Hct 23.7, plt 189. She was placed on tele and had q 4 hour vital signs. -___ (POD#7) BP 160/95, Nifedipine CR increased to 30mg po BID Triggered for pulse in 150s, repeat labs stable. Repeat RUQ scan stable. Plan CXR and CTA if possible to administer contrast safely to r/o PE. Temp- 101.6 (around ___, 95% O2 sat, CXR showed right pleural effusion and cannot r/o pneumonia ID consulted- started on Cefepime/Vanco empiric treatment for presumed hospital acquired pneumonia. CTA scheduled after getting IVF x 4 hours (250cc/hour) prelim read of CTA showed no PE, still cannot r/o pneumonia. Final CTA read: no evidence for pulmonary embolism, Large right effusion with complete collapse of the right lower lobe, increased in size, Re-demonstration of a large subcapsular hematoma compressing the right lobe of the liver, Mediastinal and left hilar lymph nodes measuring up to 1 cm. She did have swollen lips after antibiotics, given Benadryl and swelling/itching resolved. ___ blood cultures NGTD. ___ (POD#8) A chest tube was placed by Interventional Pulmonology for the right pleural effusion. labs cr 2.0, hct 25.5 plt 344, phos elevated so she was started on a low phos diet. Overnight T 100.5. ___ urine culture negative, blood cultures NGTD and pleural fluid culture NGTD ___ (POD#9) The chest tube was removed. nifedipine CR decreased to 30 mg po QD as bps lower (avoid BPs being too low so that keep up renal perfusion), BLE dopplers negative, labs stable plt 405, phos still elevated. CRP elevated (197). ID recommended MRI of abdomen/pelvis given persistent low grade temps ___ (POD#10) MRI postponed to ___ per Renal to optimize GFR given need for contrast); creat down to 1.7; Hct 23.5 stable; WBC 11.8. ALT 60,AST 34, Phos 4.4 ___ radiology did not feel pt needed repeat pelvic/abdominal MRI, no venous thrombosis, HCT-23, plts-397, ALT- 52, AST- 36 phos- 4.0, WBC-10.4, pt asymptomatic, taken off telemetry. Tmax- 99, BPs ___ ___: ID saw patient and felt that she no longer needed any antibiotics or repeat MRI. General surgery also signed off and felt patient was stable for d/c home.

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Sent in with elevated d-dimer and tachycardia  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of opioid use disorder in remission, depression, hypertriglyceridemia, and tobacco use disorder who was referred to the ED by her PCP after she was found to be tachycardic in the 150s with an elevated D-dimer. She reports that about 10 days ago, she developed generalized body aches, joint pain in her ankles and hands, swelling in her ankles, mild sore throat, and canker sores in her mouth. She went to the ___ ER and was discharged home with a prescription for methocarbamol, which she started taking but this did not alleviate her symptoms. After this, she developed a pruritic, pustular rash over her face, chest, arms, and upper back. She went in to urgent care on ___ or ___ because the swelling in her lower extremities was getting worse. She was prescribed Lasix and made an appointment for follow up with her PCP on ___. She went in to her PCP and was found to be tachycardic with an elevated D-dimer and was sent to the ED. Over the last 10 days, she reports her joint pain and body aches have improved. The sore throat is resolved. The rash is stable to slightly improved. The ___ edema is worse, however. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, lightheadedness/dizziness. No coughing or hemoptysis. No orthopnea or PND. No headaches, visual changes. No abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. She is slightly constipated. No dysuria, no frothy urine. She has not been immobilized. No long distance travel recently. She does smoke cigarettes and does not take OCPs. ___ edema is symmetric. No personal history of malignancy. No family history of clotting disorder. No history of miscarriage.  Past Medical History: obesity dyslipidemia  Social History: ___ Family History: HTN - mother CAD - maternal grandmother ___ - both sides of the family Alcohol use disorder - both parents  Physical Exam: ADMISSION EXAM VITALS: T 98.2, HR 97, BP 121/84, RR 18, SpO2 97% on RA GENERAL: Alert, NAD, breathing room air comfortably EYES: Anicteric, PERRL ENT: Aphthous ulcers involving buccal mucosa and the sides of the tongue CV: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no m/r/g, JVP not elevated RESP: CTAB, no wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi GI: Abdomen soft, non-distended, non-tender to palpation. Bowel sounds present. GU: No suprapubic fullness or tenderness to palpation MSK: Neck supple, moves all extremities, strength grossly full and symmetric bilaterally in all limbs EXT: ___ non-pitting edema in bilateral ___ up to the mid-shin SKIN: Pustular rash (many lesions are excoriated with overlying eschar) involving the face, chest, arms, and upper back NEURO: Alert, oriented, face symmetric, gaze conjugate with EOMI, speech fluent, moves all limbs PSYCH: pleasant, appropriate affect  Pertinent Results: CTA chest ___ Small segmental pulmonary embolus in the posterior segment of the right lower lobe. ADMISSION LABS WBC 11.8; otherwise CBC wnl Chem7 wnl ALP 130, otherwise LFTs wnl D-dimer 853 INR 1, PTT 33.6 proBNP 61 TSH 0.86  Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of opioid use disorder in remission, depression, hypertriglyceridemia, and tobacco use disorder who was referred to the ED by her PCP for tachycardia and an elevated D-dimer. She was found to have a small segmental PE. Also with an odd constellation of new symptoms including recent facial and truncal macular/acneiform rash, new bilateral ___ edema and hand puffiness, aphthous ulcers, polyarthralgias, and pharyngitis. # RLL segmental PE This preliminarily appears to be an unprovoked PE. She does not take OCPs and is not currently pregnant. When her labs were last checked, her folate level was undetectable. It is unclear why her folate level would be so low as she does not have an alcohol use disorder and has no dietary restrictions. Perhaps elevated homocysteine levels in the setting of folate deficiency could have predisposed her to thrombosis? Beta-2-Glycoprotein antibodies and cardiolipin antibodies were sent and are pending. She was started on warfarin 5 mg daily with a lovenox bridge (her health insurance generally does not approve DOACs without a warfarin failure). She was treated with Lovenox, which will be continued to therapeutic INR. She will be followed at ___ ___ clinic, and arrangements were made for her to get her INRs checked at ___, which is closer to her home. Unless her odd constellation of symptoms is found to be a reversible process that could potentially explain her VTE, she will likely need lifelong anticoagulation. # Bilateral ___ edema Non-pitting lower extremity edema and puffy hands in a pattern clinically more suggestive of myxedema than anything else, although TSH was not abnormal. LENIs were negative for DVT bilaterally. JVP and BNP were low and TTE was unremarkable, so CHF is excluded. LFTs are within normal limits and she has no history of chronic liver disease. No proteinuria, so nephrotic syndrome is excluded. She had been trying Lasix and NSAIDs for the swelling previously, but was counseled that these are both unlikely to help and was given compression stockings. # Polyarthralgias # Pustular rash # Aphthous ulcers The patient was seen by rheumatology, who suspected a viral syndrome; they comment that SLE could explain some of the symptoms, and also comment that while Behcet's may present with oral ulcer associated with VTE, it is vanishingly rare in patient's of ___ heritage. Seen by ID who did not feel that the rash was typical of any viral exanthem, but wondered if it could be an unusual presentation of a drug rash. If these symptoms do not continue to improve or are recurrent, would consider working up for a rheumatologic process. ___ negative, CRP not elevated, HIV negative, Lyme negative, HCV and HBV negative, VZV negative, monospot negative. RPR, EBV, parvovirus B19, dsDNA are pending. # MDD - continue home citalopram 20 mg daily # Contacts/HCP/Surrogate and Communication: ___ (aunt) ___ # Code Status/ACP: Full code  Medications on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY 2. Furosemide Dose is Unknown PO DAILY 3. Methocarbamol Dose is Unknown PO Frequency is Unknown 4. Ibuprofen 600 mg PO BID:PRN Pain - Mild  Discharge Medications: 1. Enoxaparin Sodium 70 mg SC Q12H RX *enoxaparin 80 mg/0.8 mL 80 mg SQ twice a day Disp #*28 Syringe Refills:*0 2. FoLIC Acid 1 mg PO DAILY RX *folic acid 1 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth daily Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 3. Warfarin 5 mg PO DAILY16 RX *warfarin [Coumadin] 2.5 mg 2 tablet(s) by mouth qPM Disp #*60 Tablet Refills:*0 4. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Pulmonary embolism  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: You were admitted with a pulmonary embolism, or blood clot that went to the lung. We are giving you warfarin (Coumadin) to thin your blood for at least the next three months. You will take twice daily enoxaparin (Lovenox) shots for a few days until the warfarin has fully taken effect. We follow a measure of blood clotting called INR to know if warfarin is being dosed appropriately. A therapeutic INR is between 2.0 and 3.0. You will be followed by the ___ clinic at ___, which is affiliated with your PCP ___. However, you can get your INR checked at ___. Just go to pre-admissions testing which near the main entrance, downstairs near ___. When the lab result comes back, you will be called by the pharmacist in town to adjust your warfarin dose as needed. Your first INR check will be on ___. They tell me there will be a standing order for labs in place and ___ will be expecting you, but I have also given you a paper script for bloodwork just in case. You also had a variety of unusual symptoms that may have been symptoms a virus, an adverse reaction to a medication, deficiency of the vitamin folic acid, or an autoimmune disease. We went a very large number of tests to figure out what might be going on. I will call you if anything really notable comes back positive. If the symptoms persist, you have an appointment with Dr. ___ rheumatology to do further workup. If the symptoms all go away, then it was unlikely to have been an autoimmune disease and you are welcome to cancel this appointment (___).  Followup Instructions: ___
