fields.入院情况: History of Present Illness: ___ male chef here for work from ___ reports having starting on ___ he began to have terrible epigastric pain. The pain was located in the epigastric region but then radiated diffusely throughout the upper abdomen. He also had nausea, but no vomiting initially. The pain continued in waves intermittently for a few days and was initially present after eating but then became present all the time and would get worse with eating or drinking. He on ___ began to have more nausea and tried taking ___ but the taste was so horrible that he vomited. Since then he has had nausea and vomiting with trying to eat and feels that there is like a ball in the epigastric region. He has not been moving his bowels for the past few days but previously no issues, no weight loss, no diarrhea. He denies fevers, had a low grade temp in the ED. He is very thirsty at this time and report poor urine outpt in teh past 2 days. He reports an episode of horrible epigastric pain that occured 2 days after he had what he though was bad fish in ___ of this year and a similar episode of again a few days of abd pain about ___ years ago. With the 2 previous episodes the pain was no terrible he thought that he was going to die. He came to medical attention this time before it became so bad. He does not have a hx of abd pain after eating. He does not usually see MDs, is otherwise healthy. Is helping a friend with a restaurant, here in town for about 2 weeks and was feeling well until this happened. 10 systems reviewed and are negative except where noted in the HPI above  Physical Exam: physical exam most notable for: Afeb VSS Cons: NAD, lying in bed Eyes: E___, no scleral icterus ENT: MMM Neck: nl ROM, no goiter Lymph: no cervical LAD Cardiovasc: rrr, no murmur, no edema Resp: CTA B GI: +hypoactive bs,soft, nd, no significant epigastric pain with palp. MSK: no significant kyphosis Skin: no rashes Neuro: no facial droop Psych: normal range of affect
fields.诊疗经过: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting found to have pancreatitis of unclear etiology. Pancreatitis: Lipase of 400 on admission with nausea at presentation. He has had 2 other episodes requiring hospiatlization in the past in ___, which were attributed to not moving his bowels right. He had pain with the two prior episodes, but only nausea with this presentation. His appetite was down and he hadn't been taking food in ___ days due to the nausea. He denies heavy alcohol use. He denied drug use aside from marijuana. He had no stones on RUQ. His triglycerides were 48. He is not on any medications at home. Given this is possibly his third occurrence of pancreatitis over the last year, an MRCP would be a reasonable next step in evaluation to further evaluate his anatomy. His lipase normalized and his symptoms resolved with hydration. He tolerated advancement in his diet and was discharged in good condition. Hyperbilirubinemia: His bili was elevated to 2.4 with most of it being indirect. Question of possible ___ disease, though hemolysis (mild anemia) is also possible. It was stable on discharge and may require work-up as an outpatient. Atypical cells in differential on admission: Resolved on repeat in the morning.
fields.出院情况: Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.
fields.出院医嘱: Discharge Medications: None  Discharge Instructions: Mr. ___, You were admitted to ___ for pancreatitis and dehydration. your lab work returned to normal and your symptoms resolved with hydration with IV fluids. At this point, it is not clear why you had pancreatitis. I have made an appointment for you with a new primary care physician to further investigate this concern. Please keep the appointments as listed below.

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: NEUROSURGERY  Allergies: Lisinopril / Losartan / Gadolinium-Containing Agents  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: radiculopathic back pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: L3-L4, L4-L5, L5-S1 foramenotomies  History of Present Illness: I had the pleasure of seeing ___ here. I have not seen her in a couple of years. She did reasonably well with the right side with several levels of foraminotomies. She now has left-sided symptoms that are very similar and foraminal narrowing at three levels on the left side. She has had a tough time with this and not had much benefit from physical therapy or injections and the pain has worsened since I last saw her. She would like to pursue surgery on the left side now. I went over the risks and benefits and details. It would be very similar to the other side with L3-L4, L4-L5 and L5-S1 foraminotomies and we will plan to schedule this in the near future if she calls back. She is going to think about it and try and see when her schedule might work out. I spent approximately 35 minutes with her and believe I answered her questions and concerns to the best of my ability.  Past Medical History: CAD (MI ___, last cath ___ showed mild disease), HTN, HLD, OSA CHF (TEE on ___ showede EF 41% on recent echo, Moderate AR/moderate MR) History renal cell carcinoma S/P right nephrectomy ___. Follow up CT abdomen on ___ normal. History of gastritis per EGD, history of rectal bleed, colonoscopy in ___ showed hemorrhoids. Asthma, past ___ years, uses albuterol and occasionally Flovent. L breast cancer - 1.5-cm grade 3 DCIS, ER negative - s/p L lumpectomy/XRT ___ Stiff man's syndrome/back pain, MRI ___ showed DJD at multiple levels, has used Vicodin and Valium in the past.  Social History: ___ Family History: Non-contributory.  Physical Exam: PHysical exam on discharge: Opens eyes: [x]spontaneous [ ]to voice [ ]to noxious Orientation: person, place and time Follows commands: [ ]Simple [x]Complex [ ]None EOM: [x]Full [ ]Restricted Face Symmetric: [x]Yes [ ]NoTongue Midline: [x]Yes [ ]No Speech Fluent: [x]Yes [ ]No Comprehension intact [x]Yes [ ]No Motor: IPQuadHamATEHLGast Right 4 5 5 5 55 Left2 5 5 5 5 5 Motor weakness limited by pain. Sensation intact to light touch in the L3-S1 distributions bilaterally. Wound: open to air; well approximated with staples.  Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with lumbar stenosis was admitted for elective, uncomplicated ___ L3-L4, L4-L5, and S1 foraminotomies. The patient's diet was advanced post-operatively and her pain was controlled with oral narcotics and acetaminophen and anti-spasmotics. Her neurological exam was intact for the first two full days post-operatively. On discharge she demonstrated decreased iliopsoas strength that was related to poor pain control in the setting of spasm. The remainder of her sensory and strength exam was intact distally. Upon discharge she denied other medical complaints, she was tolerating a diet, voiding normally, stooling normally, and ambulating with physical therapy. Physical therapy recommended discharge to home based on deconditioning in the setting of longstanding lumbar radiculopathy.  Medications on Admission: 6. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q6H:PRN asthma 7. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN shortness of breath 8. amLODIPine 10 mg PO DAILY 9. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM 10. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (500/50) 1 INH IH BID 11. Metoprolol Succinate XL 100 mg PO DAILY 12. Montelukast 10 mg PO DAILY 13. OxyCODONE SR (OxyconTIN) 10 mg PO Q12H 14. Ranitidine 150 mg PO BID 15. Spironolactone 25 mg PO DAILY 16. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 17. Torsemide 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Medications: 1. Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR DAILY:PRN constipation 2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 3. HydrALAZINE 25 mg PO Q8H 4. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain OK to request partial fill. Wean as tolerated RX *oxycodone 5 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every 6 hours as needed for Disp #*28 Tablet Refills:*0 5. Senna 17.2 mg PO QHS 6. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q6H:PRN asthma 7. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN shortness of breath 8. amLODIPine 10 mg PO DAILY 9. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM 10. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (500/50) 1 INH IH BID 11. Metoprolol Succinate XL 100 mg PO DAILY 12. Montelukast 10 mg PO DAILY 13. OxyCODONE SR (OxyconTIN) 10 mg PO Q12H 14. Ranitidine 150 mg PO BID 15. Spironolactone 25 mg PO DAILY 16. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 17. Torsemide 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Disposition: Extended Care  Facility: ___  Discharge Diagnosis: lumbar stenosis  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more than 3 lbs. Discharge Instructions Spine Surgery without Fusion Surgery Your dressing may come off on the second day after surgery. Your incision is closed with staples. You will need staple removal in ___ days. Please keep your incision dry until suture/staple removal. Do not apply any lotions or creams to the site. Please avoid swimming for two weeks after suture/staple removal. Call your surgeon if there are any signs of infection like redness, fever, or drainage. Activity We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment. You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your activity at your own pace. ___ try to do too much all at once. No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. Medications You may take Ibuprofen/ Motrin for pain. You should take scheduled doses of acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication. It will decrease the amount of narcotic opiate medicine you will require. It is important to increase fluid intake while taking pain medications. We also recommend a stool softener like Colace. Pain medications can cause constipation. When to Call Your Doctor at ___ for: Severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the incision site. Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit New weakness or changes in sensation in your arms or legs.  Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: ORTHOPAEDICS  Allergies: Iodinated Contrast Media - IV Dye / Delsym  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Neck pain, R arm weakness  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Revision posterior cervical decompression C6-T1, Instrumented posterior fusion C3-T1 with ICBG  History of Present Illness: I had the pleasure of seeing this patient who complains of acute onset of right hand weakness and difficulty using his hands with pain that goes down the back of his arm into his ulnar forearm. He is a previous ___ and finds difficulty holding things. It is getting worse. In ___, he had anterior cervical decompression and fusion from C4-C6 followed by a posterior cervical fusion with instrumentation without decompression from C3-C7. He comes in for further evaluation of his right upper extremity weakness. He has pain that he rates as ___ and ___ with activity and flexing forward makes it worse. He has no bowel or bladder incontinence. No left upper extremity symptoms. He has asthma and allergies, but otherwise healthy. He has had bilateral carpal tunnel release and shoulder surgery. His medications include Advair, ProAir, Singulair, simvastatin, gabapentin for approximately one week. He has allergies to IVP dye with hives and swelling. He is currently disabled. He is a previous smoker. He does not smoke currently. He is married. Family history is significant for blood disorders and platelet disorder, otherwise healthy. He is ___ feet 9 inches and 200 pounds. Multiple studies were reviewed. These include the following: 1. EMG nerve conduction study performed on ___, consistent with a right C8 radiculopathy. 2. An MRI done on ___, which shows previous surgery with foraminal stenosis at C7-T1 with compression of the C8 nerve root. 3. The CT report is not available for review, but I reviewed the CT from ___, which shows a pedicle screw at the C7 with halo bilaterally with traverse of the right foramen of C7-T1. There is no clear fusion posteriorly. There is a clear fusion in the upper cervical spine and there is some lucency around the C6 nerve roots. There is no breakage on the hardware. His overall alignment on last x-rays was within normal limits. New x-rays and CT are pending. On physical examination, this is a healthy-appearing male. Affect is within normal limits. Gait is within normal limits. He has ___ strength in bilateral upper extremities except in his interosseous, finger extension, flexion and abduction. His triceps is ___ on the right side. He has a negative shoulder exam. He has a negative Lhermitte's. He has pain with forward flexion. His sensation is grossly intact to light touch.  Brief Hospital Course: Patient was admitted to the ___ Spine Surgery Service and taken to the Operating Room for the above procedure. Refer to the dictated operative note for further details. The surgery was without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. TEDs/pnemoboots were used for postoperative DVT prophylaxis. Intravenous antibiotics were continued for 24hrs postop per standard protocol. Initial postop pain was controlled with a PCA. Diet was advanced as tolerated. The patient was transitioned to oral pain medication when tolerating PO diet. Patient ambulated independently without the need for physical therapy. His R upper extremity symptoms were improved post operatively. Drain was removed on POD 2. Wound was clean and dry. Hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. On the day of discharge the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control and tolerating a regular diet.  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H 2. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheeze 3. Diazepam 5 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain, spasm RX *diazepam 5 mg 1 tab by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 4. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*30 Capsule Refills:*0 5. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID 6. Montelukast Sodium 10 mg PO DAILY 7. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN Pain RX *oxycodone 5 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth q4-6h Disp #*80 Tablet Refills:*0 8. Simvastatin 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Cervical stenosis  Discharge Condition: Good  Discharge Instructions: You have undergone the following operation: Posterior Cervical Decompression and Fusion Immediately after the operation:  Activity: You should not lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks. You will be more comfortable if you do not sit in a car or chair for more than ~45 minutes without getting up and walking around.  Rehabilitation/ Physical Therapy: ___ ___ times a day you should go for a walk for ___ minutes as part of your recovery. You can walk as much as you can tolerate. ___ Isometric Extension Exercise in the collar: 2x/day x ___xercises as instructed.  Cervical Collar / Neck Brace: You need to wear the brace at all times until your follow-up appointment which should be in 2 weeks. You may remove the collar to take a shower. Limit your motion of your neck while the collar is off. Place the collar back on your neck immediately after the shower.  Wound Care: Remove the dressing in 2 days. If the incision is draining cover it with a new sterile dressing. If it is dry then you can leave the incision open to the air. Once the incision is completely dry (usually ___ days after the operation) you may take a shower. Do not soak the incision in a bath or pool. If the incision starts draining at anytime after surgery, do not get the incision wet. Call the office at that time. If you have an incision on your hip please follow the same instructions in terms of wound care.  You should resume taking your normal home medications.  You have also been given Additional Medications to control your pain. . Please allow 72 hours for refill of narcotic prescriptions, so please plan ahead. You can either have them mailed to your home or pick them up at the clinic located on ___. We are not allowed to call in narcotic prescriptions (oxycontin, oxycodone, percocet) to the pharmacy. In addition, we are only allowed to write for pain medications for 90 days from the date of surgery.  Follow up: ___ Please Call the office and make an appointment for 2 weeks after the day of your operation if this has not been done already. ___ At the 2-week visit we will check your incision, take baseline x rays and answer any questions. ___ We will then see you at 6 weeks from the day of the operation. At that time we will most likely obtain Flexion/Extension X-rays and often able to place you in a soft collar which you will wean out of over 1 week. Please call the office if you have a fever>101.5 degrees Fahrenheit, drainage from your wound, or have any questions.  Followup Instructions: ___

An MRI done on ___, which shows previous surgery with foraminal stenosis at C7-T1 with compression of the C8 nerve root. 3. The CT report is not available for review, but I reviewed the CT from ___, which shows a pedicle screw at the C7 with halo bilaterally with traverse of the right foramen of C7-T1. There is no clear fusion posteriorly. There is a clear fusion in the upper cervical spine and there is some lucency around the C6 nerve roots. There is no breakage on the hardware. His overall alignment on last x-rays was within normal limits. New x-rays and CT are pending. On physical examination, this is a healthy-appearing male. Affect is within normal limits. Gait is within normal limits. He has ___ strength in bilateral upper extremities except in his interosseous, finger extension, flexion and abduction. His triceps is ___ on the right side. He has a negative shoulder exam. He has a negative Lhermitte's. He has pain with forward flexion. His sensation is grossly intact to light touch.  Brief Hospital Course: Patient was admitted to the ___ Spine Surgery Service and taken to the Operating Room for the above procedure. Refer to the dictated operative note for further details. The surgery was without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. TEDs/pnemoboots were used for postoperative DVT prophylaxis. Intravenous antibiotics were continued for 24hrs postop per standard protocol. Initial postop pain was controlled with a PCA. Diet was advanced as tolerated. The patient was transitioned to oral pain medication when tolerating PO diet. Patient ambulated independently without the need for physical therapy. His R upper extremity symptoms were improved post operatively. Drain was removed on POD 2. Wound was clean and dry. Hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. On the day of discharge the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control and tolerating a regular diet.  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H 2. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheeze 3. Diazepam 5 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain, spasm RX *diazepam 5 mg 1 tab by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 4. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*30 Capsule Refills:*0 5. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID 6. Montelukast Sodium 10 mg PO DAILY 7. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN Pain RX *oxycodone 5 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth q4-6h Disp #*80 Tablet Refills:*0 8. Simvastatin 20 mg PO DAILY  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Cervical stenosis  Discharge Condition: Good  Discharge Instructions: You have undergone the following operation: Posterior Cervical Decompression and Fusion Immediately after the operation:  Activity: You should not lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks. You will be more comfortable if you do not sit in a car or chair for more than ~45 minutes without getting up and walking around.  Rehabilitation/ Physical Therapy: ___ ___ times a day you should go for a walk for ___ minutes as part of your recovery. You can walk as much as you can tolerate. ___ Isometric Extension Exercise in the collar: 2x/day x ___xercises as instructed.  Cervical Collar / Neck Brace: You need to wear the brace at all times until your follow-up appointment which should be in 2 weeks. You may remove the collar to take a shower. Limit your motion of your neck while the collar is off. Place the collar back on your neck immediately after the shower.  Wound Care: Remove the dressing in 2 days. If the incision is draining cover it with a new sterile dressing. If it is dry then you can leave the incision open to the air. Once the incision is completely dry (usually ___ days after the operation) you may take a shower. Do not soak the incision in a bath or pool. If the incision starts draining at anytime after surgery, do not get the incision wet. Call the office at that time. If you have an incision on your hip please follow the same instructions in terms of wound care.  You should resume taking your normal home medications.  You have also been given Additional Medications to control your pain. . Please allow 72 hours for refill of narcotic prescriptions, so please plan ahead. You can either have them mailed to your home or pick them up at the clinic located on ___. We are not allowed to call in narcotic prescriptions (oxycontin, oxycodone, percocet) to the pharmacy. In addition, we are only allowed to write for pain medications for 90 days from the date of surgery.  Follow up: ___ Please Call the office and make an appointment for 2 weeks after the day of your operation if this has not been done already. ___ At the 2-week visit we will check your incision, take baseline x rays and answer any questions. ___ We will then see you at 6 weeks from the day of the operation. At that time we will most likely obtain Flexion/Extension X-rays and often able to place you in a soft collar which you will wean out of over 1 week. Please call the office if you have a fever>101.5 degrees Fahrenheit, drainage from your wound, or have any questions.  Followup Instructions: ___

fields.诊疗经过: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with lumbar stenosis was admitted for elective, uncomplicated ___ L3-L4, L4-L5, and S1 foraminotomies. The patient's diet was advanced post-operatively and her pain was controlled with oral narcotics and acetaminophen and anti-spasmotics. Her neurological exam was intact for the first two full days post-operatively. On discharge she demonstrated decreased iliopsoas strength that was related to poor pain control in the setting of spasm. The remainder of her sensory and strength exam was intact distally. Upon discharge she denied other medical complaints, she was tolerating a diet, voiding normally, stooling normally, and ambulating with physical therapy. Physical therapy recommended discharge to home based on deconditioning in the setting of longstanding lumbar radiculopathy.

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Confusion  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ - Cardiac catheterization  History of Present Illness: ___ retired pathologist with history of HTN, HLD, aortic stenosis with aortic root dilation who presents from home after an episode of confusion. Patient reports that he was in his usual state of health until he woke up this morning and felt a little chilly with some shivers. He spent most of the day watching football. Per his family, he had just finished his supper while watching football when he didn't look right then had one episode of emesis. He was sleepier and mildly confused. They asked if they should call ___ and he told them yes (which is abnormal for him). EMS arrived and brought him to ___ and had a possible STEMI on EKG. On arrival he was alert and oriented x3 (except he thought it was ___ and didn't have any localizing symptoms. No fever, chills, cough, neck pain, headache, dysuria/polyuria. No abdominal pain, denied n/v/d. Family said on arrival to the ED at midnight, the patient looks much improved, back to baseline mental status but that he was confused earlier and didn't seem right and seemed warm to the touch. Slight increase in cough today but always has baseline cough from post nasal drip. Denies any sick contacts.. Cardiology was consulted in the ED and felt that his EKG looked improved and the patient was patient was asymptomatic without any recent chest pain or SOB. Prelim cardiac echo reported to show no obvious wall motion abnormalities. Fellow felt urgent cath was not needed and recommended heparin drip and NPO for cath in AM. In the ED intial vitals were: 101.1 85 136/41 16 98% RA Patient was given: ASA 325mg by EMS, CTX, Vanc, atorva 80, and started on heparin bolus plus drip. Labs notable for trop 0.34 and Cr 2. Vitals on transfer: 97.8 86 98/40 18 98% RA On the floor patient is A&O x3 and feels well without any specific complains. Again, denies every having chest pain or SOB. ROS: On review of systems, denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. Denies recent fevers. Denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative.  Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope.  Past Medical History: Aortic Stenosis with aortic root dilation HTN HLD  Social History: ___ Family History: Sister has aortic stenosis. No family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death.  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ================================ VS: Tmax=101.1 T=98 BP=95/34 HR=81 RR=18 O2 sat=96% RA GENERAL: WDWN male in NAD. Oriented x3. Very pleasant and alert.  HEENT: PERRL, EOMI, MMM. NECK: Supple with JVP of 6 cm. CARDIAC: RR, normal S1, S2. No m/r/g. LUNGS: Resp were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. Wheezes diffusely, no crackles. ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No tenderness. EXTREMITIES: No c/c/e. 2+ DP pulses. SKIN: No rashes. DISCHARGE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ================================ VS: Tmax=98.6 T=98.2 BP=108-126/32-48 ___ NSR/pAF RR=18 O2 sat=96% RA GENERAL: WDWN male in NAD. Oriented x3. Very pleasant and alert.  HEENT: PERRL, EOMI, MMM. NECK: Supple with JVP of 6 cm. CARDIAC: RR, normal S1, S2. ___ systolic murmur LUNGS: Resp were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. CTAB ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No tenderness. EXTREMITIES: No c/c/e. 2+ DP pulses. SKIN: No rashes.  Pertinent Results: ADMISSION LABS: ================ OTHER PERTINENT LABS: ====================== MICROBIOLOGY: ================ ___ STOOL C. difficile DNA amplification assay-FINAL INPATIENT ___ SPUTUM GRAM STAIN-FINAL; RESPIRATORY CULTURE-FINAL INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE NOT PROCESSED INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING INPATIENT ___ STOOL C. difficile DNA amplification assay-FINAL; FECAL CULTURE-FINAL; CAMPYLOBACTER CULTURE-FINAL INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING INPATIENT ___ MRSA SCREEN MRSA SCREEN-FINAL INPATIENT ___ SPUTUM GRAM STAIN-FINAL; RESPIRATORY CULTURE-FINAL INPATIENT ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING INPATIENT ___ SEROLOGY/BLOOD RAPID PLASMA REAGIN TEST-FINAL INPATIENT ___ Influenza A/B by ___ DIRECT INFLUENZA A ANTIGEN TEST-FINAL; DIRECT INFLUENZA B ANTIGEN TEST-FINAL EMERGENCY WARD ___ URINE Legionella Urinary Antigen -FINAL INPATIENT ___ CSF;SPINAL FLUID GRAM STAIN-FINAL; FLUID CULTURE-FINAL EMERGENCY WARD ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-FINAL {ESCHERICHIA COLI, ENTEROCOCCUS CASSELIFLAVUS, ESCHERICHIA COLI, ESCHERICHIA COLI}; Aerobic Bottle Gram Stain-FINAL; Anaerobic Bottle Gram Stain-FINAL EMERGENCY WARD ___ BLOOD CULTURE Blood Culture, Routine-FINAL {ESCHERICHIA COLI, ENTEROCOCCUS CASSELIFLAVUS, ESCHERICHIA COLI, ESCHERICHIA COLI}; Aerobic Bottle Gram Stain-FINAL; Anaerobic Bottle Gram Stain-FINAL IMAGING/STUDIES: ================== CXR ___: Biapical pleural scarring. Subtle retrocardiac airspace opacity, best appreciated on the lateral. Trace left pleural effusion with adjacent atelectasis is noted. CT HEAD ___: No evidence of acute intracranial process. TTE ___: LV apex aneurysmal. Mild-moderate regional LV systolic dysfunction with severe HK of distal septum, ant, and inf walls with apical aneurysm/akinesis. LVEF = 40%. IMPRESSION: Normal LV cavity size with regional systolic dysfunction c/w CAD (mid LAD distribution) vs. Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy. Thickened aortic valve. Cath ___: Coronary angiography: right dominant LMCA: normal LAD: <50% proximal disease, long segment stenosis to 90% after D1 LCX: 60% mid lesion RCA: minor irregularities LAD lesion crossed with Prowater wire, dilated with 2.0 balloon and stented with overlapping 2.5x32 (distal) and 2.5x8 (proximal) Premier stents postdilated to 2.75 mm at 16 atm. Dopamine 5 mcg begun at beginning of procedure for SBP 80, stopped at end with return to baseline SBP in ___. Assessment & Recommendations 1. Successful drug-eluting stent of LAD 2. Follow cardiac enzymes 3. Review echo for LV function and severity of aortic stenosis 4. Follow renal function 5. Report following procedure of + blood cultures - if true will need to treat aggressively with antibiotics in view of stent placement. Abdominal Ultrasound ___: Unremarkable abdomen ultrasound with no findings to suggest an abscess. The right kidney is noted to be somewhat atrophic with cortical thinning suggesting chronic renal disease. TTE: ___ The left atrium is mildly dilated. Left ventricular wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is normal. LV systolic function appears mildly-to-moderately depressed (LVEF = 40%) secondary to apical hypokinesis with focal apical akinesis. Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The ascending aorta is moderately dilated. The aortic valve leaflets are moderately thickened. There is moderate aortic valve stenosis (valve area = 1.0cm2). Mild (1+) aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Mild to moderate (___) mitral regurgitation is seen. There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of ___ the findings are similar. CT Abdomen: ___. No specific CT finding in the abdomen or pelvis to explain the patient's symptoms. 2. Bilateral pleural effusions. 3. Non-complicated diverticulosis. 4. Post-surgical pneumobilia. 5. Incidental old compression fractures at T11 and L1. CXR ___: Right lower lobe consolidation has improved since ___, likely pneumonia, but moderate right pleural effusion persists. Heart is normal size and mediastinal vessels are not engorged, but there is pulmonary vascular redistribution. Pulmonary edema has not recurred. No pneumothorax.  Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with a past medical history of HTN, HLD, aortic stenosis, aortic root dilation, who presented from home with fever chills, and confusion x 1 day, dx with STEMI s/p DES x2, bacteremia, ___ and SIADH. # Polymicrobial bacteremia: Pt presents with fever, hypotension and elevated lactate, briefly requiring vasopressor during catheterization. Found to have e. coli and enterococcus bacteremia. He initially had a leukocytosis that has since resolved. Zosyn was started for two week course per ID. S/p midline placement ___. Last dose of Zosyn will be ___. # STEMI: EF 40%. s/p DES to LAD. Has persistent STE ___ apical dyskinesis. No further chest pain. - Cont Plavix 75mg po daily - Cont ASA 81mg po daily - Cont Atorvastatin 80mg po daily - Continue metoprolol succinate - Holding ACE-I in setting of creatinine # ___: Cr 2 on presentation without unknown prior baseline. Throughout hospitalization this stabilized. Pt states it has always been high but doesn't know the specific number. FeNa suggests prerenal cause. At the time of discharge the creatinine was 2.1. As an outpatient Mr. ___ should have an electrolyte check and follow up with his PCP. He should also avoid all nephrotoxic agents and renally dose his medications. # Hyponatremia: FeNa suggests pt is prerenal although looks euvolemic on exam. No obvious medication changes that could contribute. Antibiotics do not have side effect of hyponatremia. T4/T3 nl. No hypotension to suggest cortisol def. Urine studies indicate SIADH. Patient was placed on free water restriction with liberalized diet. Prior to discharge his sodium was 138 # Apical Akinesis: focal apical akinesis on Echo ___. - continue to hold off anticoagulation in setting of recent guaiac positive stools and ? of GI bleed in ICU. As an outpatient he should have a follow up echo. #)paroxysmal Atrial fibrillation: rate controlled -no AC at this time r/t hx GIB -Pt asked to follow up with his PCP for upper/lower GI scope # Hypoxia/COPD: resolved. No wheezes on exam. CXR is improving. Sputum cx neg. - Albuterol/Ipratropium nebs - Outpatient PFTs # Anemia: Mild and stable. Still having heme pos stools. Haptoglobin nl. Possible gastritis vs lower GIB. Not starting warfarin at this time but is on ASA/clopidogrel for stent. Will continue PPI as outpatient. # Confusion: Resolved, appears clear and oriented. # Thrombocytopenia: resolving Baseline not known. - Trend PLT # Transaminitis: DDx includes bile duct pathology vs. hepatic congestion from CHF. - Trend LFTs - Abd US as above  Medications on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Pravastatin 80 mg PO DAILY 2. Losartan Potassium 50 mg PO BID 3. Amlodipine 10 mg PO DAILY 4. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 5. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY  Discharge Medications: 1. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 2. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 3. Atorvastatin 80 mg PO DAILY 4. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 5. Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY 6. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H 7. Piperacillin-Tazobactam 2.25 g IV Q8H 8. Losartan Potassium 50 mg PO BID Take 25 mg twice daily  Discharge Disposition: Home With Service  Facility: ___  Discharge Diagnosis: Primary 1. CAD/STEMI s/p ___ 2 to LAD 2. Sepsis 3. ___ on CKD - resolved 4. SIADH- resolved 5. atrial fibrillation Secondary PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Aortic Stenosis with aortic root dilation HTN HLD COPD Bile duct stricture sp modified Whipple in ___ (no cancer found) L eye blindness ___ macular hole CKD with baseline Cr 2  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Dr. ___, You presented to the hospital with confusion and a fever with low blood pressure. You were found to have sepsis. You also had a heart attack, and 2 stents were placed to re-open the blockage and improve blood flow to your heart. You were started on Plavix. You were already taking Aspirin. Aspirin and Plavix are used to decrease at the risk of clot/blockage in your arteries/stents. You will take Plavix for at least one year, and Aspirin indefinitely. Do not stop taking Plavix until told to do so by your Cardiologist. Stopping Plavix prematurely may increase your risk of a life threatening heart attack. You also had an acute innjury to your kidneys and a low sodium level. Both of these problems have resolved, however please restart your Losartan at half of your home dose. You will need to take an IV antibiotic through ___. Lastly, you had several self resolving episodes of atrial fibrillation while you were in the hospital. Out of concern for some stools that tested positive for blood, you were not started on any anti-coagulation. It is strongly advised that you have scopes of your upper and lower GI tracts to determine if there is any bleeding. Based on those results, a decision can be made about anti-coagulation. You have decided that you do not want to go to have home ___ services, but have instead decided to participate in outpatient Cardiac Rehabilitation. It has been a pleasure to participate in your care. Best Regards, The CCU Team  Followup Instructions: ___

Dopamine 5 mcg begun at beginning of procedure for SBP 80, stopped at end with return to baseline SBP in ___. Assessment & Recommendations 1. Successful drug-eluting stent of LAD 2. Follow cardiac enzymes 3. Review echo for LV function and severity of aortic stenosis 4. Follow renal function 5. Report following procedure of + blood cultures - if true will need to treat aggressively with antibiotics in view of stent placement. The right kidney is noted to be somewhat atrophic with cortical thinning suggesting chronic renal disease. TTE: ___ The left atrium is mildly dilated. Left ventricular wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is normal. LV systolic function appears mildly-to-moderately depressed (LVEF = 40%) secondary to apical hypokinesis with focal apical akinesis. Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The ascending aorta is moderately dilated. The aortic valve leaflets are moderately thickened. There is moderate aortic valve stenosis (valve area = 1.0cm2). Mild (1+) aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Mild to moderate (___) mitral regurgitation is seen. There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of ___ the findings are similar. CT Abdomen: ___. No specific CT finding in the abdomen or pelvis to explain the patient's symptoms. 2. Bilateral pleural effusions. 3. Non-complicated diverticulosis. 4. Post-surgical pneumobilia. 5. Incidental old compression fractures at T11 and L1. CXR ___: Right lower lobe consolidation has improved since ___, likely pneumonia, but moderate right pleural effusion persists. Heart is normal size and mediastinal vessels are not engorged, but there is pulmonary vascular redistribution. Pulmonary edema has not recurred. No pneumothorax. No further chest pain. - Cont Plavix 75mg po daily - Cont ASA 81mg po daily - Cont Atorvastatin 80mg po daily - Continue metoprolol succinate - Holding ACE-I in setting of creatinine # ___: Cr 2 on presentation without unknown prior baseline. Throughout hospitalization this stabilized. Pt states it has always been high but doesn't know the specific number. FeNa suggests prerenal cause. At the time of discharge the creatinine was 2.1. He should also avoid all nephrotoxic agents and renally dose his medications. # Hyponatremia: FeNa suggests pt is prerenal although looks euvolemic on exam. Will continue PPI as outpatient. # Confusion: Resolved, appears clear and oriented. # Thrombocytopenia: resolving Baseline not known. - Trend PLT # Transaminitis: DDx includes bile duct pathology vs. hepatic congestion from CHF. - Trend LFTs - Abd US as above  Medications on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: Dr. ___, You presented to the hospital with confusion and a fever with low blood pressure. You were found to have sepsis. You also had a heart attack, and 2 stents were placed to re-open the blockage and improve blood flow to your heart. You were started on Plavix. You were already taking Aspirin. Aspirin and Plavix are used to decrease at the risk of clot/blockage in your arteries/stents. You will take Plavix for at least one year, and Aspirin indefinitely. Do not stop taking Plavix until told to do so by your Cardiologist. Stopping Plavix prematurely may increase your risk of a life threatening heart attack. You also had an acute innjury to your kidneys and a low sodium level. Both of these problems have resolved, however please restart your Losartan at half of your home dose. You will need to take an IV antibiotic through ___. Lastly, you had several self resolving episodes of atrial fibrillation while you were in the hospital. Out of concern for some stools that tested positive for blood, you were not started on any anti-coagulation. It is strongly advised that you have scopes of your upper and lower GI tracts to determine if there is any bleeding. Based on those results, a decision can be made about anti-coagulation. You have decided that you do not want to go to have home ___ services, but have instead decided to participate in outpatient Cardiac Rehabilitation. It has been a pleasure to participate in your care. Best Regards, The CCU Team  Followup Instructions: ___

fields.入院情况: History of Present Illness: ___ retired pathologist with history of HTN, HLD, aortic stenosis with aortic root dilation who presents from home after an episode of confusion. Patient reports that he was in his usual state of health until he woke up this morning and felt a little chilly with some shivers. He spent most of the day watching football. Per his family, he had just finished his supper while watching football when he didn't look right then had one episode of emesis. He was sleepier and mildly confused. They asked if they should call ___ and he told them yes (which is abnormal for him). EMS arrived and brought him to ___ and had a possible STEMI on EKG. On arrival he was alert and oriented x3 (except he thought it was ___ and didn't have any localizing symptoms. No fever, chills, cough, neck pain, headache, dysuria/polyuria. No abdominal pain, denied n/v/d. Family said on arrival to the ED at midnight, the patient looks much improved, back to baseline mental status but that he was confused earlier and didn't seem right and seemed warm to the touch. Slight increase in cough today but always has baseline cough from post nasal drip. Denies any sick contacts.. Cardiology was consulted in the ED and felt that his EKG looked improved and the patient was patient was asymptomatic without any recent chest pain or SOB. Prelim cardiac echo reported to show no obvious wall motion abnormalities. Fellow felt urgent cath was not needed and recommended heparin drip and NPO for cath in AM. In the ED intial vitals were: 101.1 85 136/41 16 98% RA Patient was given: ASA 325mg by EMS, CTX, Vanc, atorva 80, and started on heparin bolus plus drip. Labs notable for trop 0.34 and Cr 2. Vitals on transfer: 97.8 86 98/40 18 98% RA On the floor patient is A&O x3 and feels well without any specific complains. Again, denies every having chest pain or SOB. ROS: On review of systems, denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. Denies recent fevers. Denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative.  Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope.  Past Medical History: Aortic Stenosis with aortic root dilation HTN HLD  Social History: ___ Family History: Sister has aortic stenosis. No family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death.  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: ================================ VS: Tmax=101.1 T=98 BP=95/34 HR=81 RR=18 O2 sat=96% RA GENERAL: WDWN male in NAD. Oriented x3. Very pleasant and alert.  HEENT: PERRL, EOMI, MMM. NECK: Supple with JVP of 6 cm. CARDIAC: RR, normal S1, S2. No m/r/g. LUNGS: Resp were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. Wheezes diffusely, no crackles. ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No tenderness. EXTREMITIES: No c/c/e. 2+ DP pulses. SKIN: No rashes.

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: fall off dirt bike  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Left chest tube placement at outside hospital.  History of Present Illness: ___ DOB: ___ Otherwise healthy ___ presents to the ED from ___ with c/o MVA. He was reportedly on a dirt bike when he fell off. He was not wearing a helmet, hit his head, and did lose consciousness. He was found by EMS lying in the dirt and taken endorsed alcohol. He was seen at OSH where his work-up was notable for sodium 142, potassium 3.7, creatinine 1.3, glucose 161, AST 261, ALT 103, AP 137, total bilirubin 0.3, lipase 181, INR 0.9, lactate 3.0. He was complaining of shortness of breath and found to have a left-sided pneumothorax, so a chest tube was placed. Subsequent CT scans identified nasal bone fractures, right sided subdural hematoma, left-sided ___ rib fractures, fractures of L2-L4 vertebrae, and inferior/superior pubic rami fractures. He was given tetanus and pain medications and transferred to ___.  Past Medical History: nasal bone fracture right sided subdural hematoma left-sided pneumothorax posterior ___ L rib fractures R ___ fracture non-displaced fracture of the superior body of the sternum L2 vertebral body burst fracture L4 vertebral body compression fracture L1-2, L3-4 epidural hematoma left inferior/superior pubic rami fractures left proximal radius fracture right fibular fracture right calcaneal fracture  Social History: ___ Family History: Not pertinent to this presentation.  Physical Exam: Discharge Physical Exam: VS: 98.1 PO BP: 128/75 L Lying HR: 74 RR: 20 O2: 96% Ra GEN: A+Ox3, NAD, anxious CV: RRR PULM: No respiratory distress, breathing comfortably on room air ABD: soft, non-distended, non-tender EXT: RLE with cast in place over foot and lower leg.  Pertinent Results: Radiology: ___ CT HEAD: 1. No evidence of acute intracranial process or hemorrhage. 2. Unchanged punctate radiopaque foreign body identified posterior to right maxillary region causing streak artifacts. ___ MR ___ Spine: 1. Tricolumnar L2 vertebral body fracture, extending into the right pedicle, right articular joint facet, with retropulsion of the posterosuperior vertebral body fragment into the spinal canal with associated epidural hematoma, exiting from L1-L2 to the level of L4-L5, and measuring up to 1.2 cm in greatest AP dimension at the level of L2, resulting moderate to severe spinal canal stenosis, subarticular recess narrowing, right neural foraminal narrowing, compression of the cauda equina as described above. 2. L4 burst fracture involving the anterior middle columns with minimal 2 mm retropulsion into the ventral spinal canal with associated epidural hematoma resulting in mild spinal canal and bilateral neural foraminal narrowing. 3. Right L3 transverse process fracture, better evaluated on the prior CT. 4. Ventral epidural hematoma extending from the level of L1-L2 to the level of L4-L5 and measuring up to 1.2 cm in maximal AP dimension at the level of L2 with resulting posterior displacement of the cauda equina. 5. Mild multilevel facet hypertrophy and prominent dorsal epidural fat and ventral epidural hematoma, results in multilevel mild spinal canal narrowing, and mild to moderate neural foraminal stenosis as detailed above. 6. Partially visualized left iliopsoas hematoma measuring 5.2 x 2.1 cm ___ CT ___: There are displaced comminuted of the right calcaneus with intra-articular extension within the tail calcaneus joint. There is a 16 mm medially displaced fracture fragment. Additionally, superior subluxation of the calcaneal cuboid joint is demonstrated. A mildly displaced comminuted fracture of the right distal fibula is demonstrated. No tibial fracture demonstrated. No other fractures are demonstrated.  ___ tib/fib: Nondisplaced distal fibular shaft fracture. ___ ANKLE: Comminuted calcaneal fracture. Soft tissue swelling along the lateral calf and ankle. Some areas of lucency within the soft tissue, could represent soft tissue gas. Query open fracture. Apparent fracture of the base of the fifth metatarsal with sclerotic margins, most likely old. ___ Pelvis: Left superior and inferior pubic rami fractures CT head OSH: Thin right frontal acute SDH without mass effect or MLS CT torso OSH: L3 compression and right facet and lamina fx with mild retropulsion into the canal, L3 right TP fx, L4 burst fx without retropulsion and L5 SP fx. In addition: Multiple rib fx, left pneumothorax, right anterior and superior pubic rami fx  Brief Hospital Course: ___ with no PMH who fell of dirt bike striking his head with trauma burden that includes large L pneumothorax, nasal bone fractures, R subdural hematoma, L ___ rib fractures, L2-L4 vertebral fractures, inferior/superior pubic rami fractures, right calcaneal fracture, and left proximal radius fracture. A left chest tube was placed at an OSH for the left pneumothorax. He was given tetanus and pain medications and transferred to ___.  In the ___ ED, he was given Ancef and pain was managed with dilaudid IV. He was evaluated by orthopedics, who splinted his RLE and recommended operative management for the calcaneal fracture. The left proximal radius fracture was managed non-operatively with a sling. Neurosurgery evaluated his subdural hematoma and recommended repeat head CT, Keppra 1 g BID for seizure ppx, Q1h neuro checks, and TBI pathway. Spine recommended MRI L-spine, which on wet read demonstrated a spinal epidural hematoma in the area of his L2-L4 fractures; spine recommended log roll precautions, TLSO brace, and trending neuro exam. He was managed in the Trauma ICU. When stable, he was transferred to the surgical floor. Neuro: The patient was alert and oriented throughout hospitalization; pain was initially managed with a hydromorphone PCA and and then transitioned to oral oxycodone, gabapentin, toradol, acetaminophen, lidocaine patches and Tizanidine prn. The Acute Pain Service was consulted to help with pain management. The patient had a standing L spine x-ray (in the TLSO brace) on HD8 which showed show good alignment of L-spine with bracing. CT Head from ___ was reviewed and showed no evidence of acute intracranial process or hemorrhage, therefore keppra was discontinued.  CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. The patient had interval chest x-rays to evaluate the left pneumothorax. The left chest tube was removed on HD7 and interval chest x-rays were obtained. The patient remained stable on room air in no acute respiratory distress.  GI/GU/FEN: The patient tolerated a regular diet without issue. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored. ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. Musculoskeletal: The Orthopedic Surgery service evaluated the patient's right calcaneal fracture and felt that it would best be managed non-operatively. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was offered the Meds-to-Bed service and declined. The patient was tolerating a diet, moving out of bed independently, voiding without assistance, and pain was controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.  Medications on Admission: None  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H RX *acetaminophen 500 mg 2 tablet(s) by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*56 Tablet Refills:*0 2. Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR DAILY:PRN Constipation - Second Line Reason for PRN duplicate override: Alternating agents for similar severity 3. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID constipation 4. Gabapentin 600 mg PO QHS RX *gabapentin 300 mg 2 capsule(s) by mouth at bedtime Disp #*14 Capsule Refills:*0 5. Gabapentin 300 mg PO BID RX *gabapentin 300 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp #*14 Capsule Refills:*0 6. Ibuprofen 400 mg PO Q6H Take with food RX *ibuprofen 400 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*28 Tablet Refills:*0 7. Lactulose 15 mL PO Q12H:PRN constipation RX *lactulose 10 gram/15 mL (15 mL) 15 ml by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp ___ Milliliter Refills:*0 8. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) 10 mg PO Q3H:PRN Pain - Moderate Wean as tolerated. Patient ___ request partial fill. RX *oxycodone 10 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every three (3) hours Disp #*56 Tablet Refills:*0 9. OxyCODONE SR (OxyCONTIN) 20 mg PO Q12H RX *oxycodone [OxyContin] 20 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp #*14 Tablet Refills:*0 10. Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO DAILY:PRN Constipation - Third Line 11. Senna 8.6 mg PO BID Hold for loose stool  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: -Nasal bone fracture -Right sided subdural hematoma -Left-sided pneumothorax -Fractures of the posterior left, sixth, eighth-tenth ribs -Right-sided ___ fracture -Non-displaced fracture of the superior body of the sternum -L2 vertebral body burst fracture, L4 vertebral body compression fracture -L1-2, L3-4 epidural hematoma -Left inferior/superior pubic rami fractures -Left proximal radius fracture -Right fibular fracture -Right calcaneal fracture  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker or cane).  Discharge Instructions: Dear Mr. ___, You were admitted to ___ after falling off a dirt bike and you were found to have a traumatic brain injury, nasal bone fractures, a sternal fracture and multiple left rib fractures, a left pneumothorax (abnormal air within the lung lining due to a lung puncture), spine fractures, a right fibula (lower leg bone) fracture, a left radial head fracture (forearm bone), left pelvic fractures, and a right heel fracture. The Neurosurgery service was consulted for your internal head injury as well as for your spine fractures. For your traumatic brain injury, you were initially started on a medication, called Keppra, to prevent seizures. This medication was stopped as it was no longer needed and your repeat head CT scan was stable. Your spine fractures are in stable alignment with your TLSO brace. For your left lung injury, a chest tube was placed to help evacuate the air from your lung lining. Follow-up chest x-rays were obtained and your chest tube was removed when it was no longer needed. Your rib fractures will heal on their own. The Plastic Surgery service evaluated your nasal bone fractures and these will heal on their own. The Orthopedic Surgery service evaluated your arm fractures, pelvic fractures, and right heel fracture. Your right heel fracture was deemed non-operative. It is recommended that you remain non-weight bearing on the right leg, weight bear as tolerated on the left leg, and do not bear weight on the left arm. You were seen by the Acute Pain Service to help establish an oral pain control regimen. You will be discharged home with a week of pain medication. Please contact your primary care provider to arrange for outpatient follow-up within one week for evaluation and to address your pain medication.  You were evaluated by Physical Therapy and you are cleared to be discharged home to continue your recovery. Please follow the instructions below to continue your recovery: Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the Emergency Department for any of the following: *You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or tightness. *New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze. *If you are vomiting and cannot keep down fluids or your medications. *You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea, or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. *You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a bowel movement. *You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your urine, or experience a discharge. *Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or back. *You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius. *Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take any new medications as prescribed. Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. Avoid lifting weights greater than ___ lbs until you follow-up with your surgeon. Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications. Incision Care: *Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner if you have increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision site. *Avoid swimming and baths until your follow-up appointment. *You ___ shower, and wash surgical incisions with a mild soap and warm water. Gently pat the area dry. *If you have staples, they will be removed at your follow-up appointment. *If you have steri-strips, they will fall off on their own. Please remove any remaining strips ___ days after surgery. *wear your TLSO brace when out of bed or sitting up >30 degrees. ___ brace at edge of bed, no twisting.  Followup Instructions: ___

sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: fall off dirt bike  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Left chest tube placement at outside hospital.  History of Present Illness: ___ DOB: ___ Otherwise healthy ___ presents to the ED from ___ with c/o MVA. He was reportedly on a dirt bike when he fell off. He was not wearing a helmet, hit his head, and did lose consciousness. He was found by EMS lying in the dirt and taken endorsed alcohol. He was seen at OSH where his work-up was notable for sodium 142, potassium 3.7, creatinine 1.3, glucose 161, AST 261, ALT 103, AP 137, total bilirubin 0.3, lipase 181, INR 0.9, lactate 3.0. He was complaining of shortness of breath and found to have a left-sided pneumothorax, so a chest tube was placed. Subsequent CT scans identified nasal bone fractures, right sided subdural hematoma, left-sided ___ rib fractures, fractures of L2-L4 vertebrae, and inferior/superior pubic rami fractures. He was given tetanus and pain medications and transferred to ___.  Past Medical History: nasal bone fracture right sided subdural hematoma left-sided pneumothorax posterior ___ L rib fractures R ___ fracture non-displaced fracture of the superior body of the sternum L2 vertebral body burst fracture L4 vertebral body compression fracture L1-2, L3-4 epidural hematoma left inferior/superior pubic rami fractures left proximal radius fracture right fibular fracture right calcaneal fracture  Social History: ___ Family History: Not pertinent to this presentation.  Physical Exam: Discharge Physical Exam: VS: 98.1 PO BP: 128/75 L Lying HR: 74 RR: 20 O2: 96% Ra GEN: A+Ox3, NAD, anxious CV: RRR PULM: No respiratory distress, breathing comfortably on room air ABD: soft, non-distended, non-tender EXT: RLE with cast in place over foot and lower leg.  Pertinent Results: Radiology: ___ CT HEAD: 1. No evidence of acute intracranial process or hemorrhage. 2. Unchanged punctate radiopaque foreign body identified posterior to right maxillary region causing streak artifacts. ___ MR ___ Spine: 1. Tricolumnar L2 vertebral body fracture, extending into the right pedicle, right articular joint facet, with retropulsion of the posterosuperior vertebral body fragment into the spinal canal with associated epidural hematoma, exiting from L1-L2 to the level of L4-L5, and measuring up to 1.2 cm in greatest AP dimension at the level of L2, resulting moderate to severe spinal canal stenosis, subarticular recess narrowing, right neural foraminal narrowing, compression of the cauda equina as described above. 2. L4 burst fracture involving the anterior middle columns with minimal 2 mm retropulsion into the ventral spinal canal with associated epidural hematoma resulting in mild spinal canal and bilateral neural foraminal narrowing. 3. Right L3 transverse process fracture, better evaluated on the prior CT. 4. Ventral epidural hematoma extending from the level of L1-L2 to the level of L4-L5 and measuring up to 1.2 cm in maximal AP dimension at the level of L2 with resulting posterior displacement of the cauda equina. 5. Mild multilevel facet hypertrophy and prominent dorsal epidural fat and ventral epidural hematoma, results in multilevel mild spinal canal narrowing, and mild to moderate neural foraminal stenosis as detailed above. 6. Partially visualized left iliopsoas hematoma measuring 5.2 x 2.1 cm ___ CT ___: There are displaced comminuted of the right calcaneus with intra-articular extension within the tail calcaneus joint. There is a 16 mm medially displaced fracture fragment. Additionally, superior subluxation of the calcaneal cuboid joint is demonstrated. A mildly displaced comminuted fracture of the right distal fibula is demonstrated. No tibial fracture demonstrated. No other fractures are demonstrated.  ___ tib/fib: Nondisplaced distal fibular shaft fracture. ___ ANKLE: Comminuted calcaneal fracture. Soft tissue swelling along the lateral calf and ankle. Some areas of lucency within the soft tissue, could represent soft tissue gas. Query open fracture. Apparent fracture of the base of the fifth metatarsal with sclerotic margins, most likely old. ___ Pelvis: Left superior and inferior pubic rami fractures CT head OSH: Thin right frontal acute SDH without mass effect or MLS CT torso OSH: L3 compression and right facet and lamina fx with mild retropulsion into the canal, L3 right TP fx, L4 burst fx without retropulsion and L5 SP fx. In addition: Multiple rib fx, left pneumothorax, right anterior and superior pubic rami fx  Brief Hospital Course: ___ with no PMH who fell of dirt bike striking his head with trauma burden that includes large L pneumothorax, nasal bone fractures, R subdural hematoma, L ___ rib fractures, L2-L4 vertebral fractures, inferior/superior pubic rami fractures, right calcaneal fracture, and left proximal radius fracture. A left chest tube was placed at an OSH for the left pneumothorax. He was given tetanus and pain medications and transferred to ___.  In the ___ ED, he was given Ancef and pain was managed with dilaudid IV. He was evaluated by orthopedics, who splinted his RLE and recommended operative management for the calcaneal fracture. The left proximal radius fracture was managed non-operatively with a sling. Neurosurgery evaluated his subdural hematoma and recommended repeat head CT, Keppra 1 g BID for seizure ppx, Q1h neuro checks, and TBI pathway. Spine recommended MRI L-spine, which on wet read demonstrated a spinal epidural hematoma in the area of his L2-L4 fractures; spine recommended log roll precautions, TLSO brace, and trending neuro exam. He was managed in the Trauma ICU. When stable, he was transferred to the surgical floor. Neuro: The patient was alert and oriented throughout hospitalization; pain was initially managed with a hydromorphone PCA and and then transitioned to oral oxycodone, gabapentin, toradol, acetaminophen, lidocaine patches and Tizanidine prn. The Acute Pain Service was consulted to help with pain management. The patient had a standing L spine x-ray (in the TLSO brace) on HD8 which showed show good alignment of L-spine with bracing. CT Head from ___ was reviewed and showed no evidence of acute intracranial process or hemorrhage, therefore keppra was discontinued.  CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. The patient had interval chest x-rays to evaluate the left pneumothorax. The left chest tube was removed on HD7 and interval chest x-rays were obtained. The patient remained stable on room air in no acute respiratory distress.  GI/GU/FEN: The patient tolerated a regular diet without issue. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored. ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. Musculoskeletal: The Orthopedic Surgery service evaluated the patient's right calcaneal fracture and felt that it would best be managed non-operatively. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was offered the Meds-to-Bed service and declined. The patient was tolerating a diet, moving out of bed independently, voiding without assistance, and pain was controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.  Medications on Admission: None  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H RX *acetaminophen 500 mg 2 tablet(s) by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*56 Tablet Refills:*0 2. Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR DAILY:PRN Constipation - Second Line Reason for PRN duplicate override: Alternating agents for similar severity 3. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID constipation 4. Gabapentin 600 mg PO QHS RX *gabapentin 300 mg 2 capsule(s) by mouth at bedtime Disp #*14 Capsule Refills:*0 5. Gabapentin 300 mg PO BID RX *gabapentin 300 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp #*14 Capsule Refills:*0 6. Ibuprofen 400 mg PO Q6H Take with food RX *ibuprofen 400 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*28 Tablet Refills:*0 7. Lactulose 15 mL PO Q12H:PRN constipation RX *lactulose 10 gram/15 mL (15 mL) 15 ml by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp ___ Milliliter Refills:*0 8. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) 10 mg PO Q3H:PRN Pain - Moderate Wean as tolerated. Patient ___ request partial fill. RX *oxycodone 10 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every three (3) hours Disp #*56 Tablet Refills:*0 9. OxyCODONE SR (OxyCONTIN) 20 mg PO Q12H RX *oxycodone [OxyContin] 20 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every twelve (12) hours Disp #*14 Tablet Refills:*0 10. Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO DAILY:PRN Constipation - Third Line 11. Senna 8.6 mg PO BID Hold for loose stool  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: -Nasal bone fracture -Right sided subdural hematoma -Left-sided pneumothorax -Fractures of the posterior left, sixth, eighth-tenth ribs -Right-sided ___ fracture -Non-displaced fracture of the superior body of the sternum -L2 vertebral body burst fracture, L4 vertebral body compression fracture -L1-2, L3-4 epidural hematoma -Left inferior/superior pubic rami fractures -Left proximal radius fracture -Right fibular fracture -Right calcaneal fracture  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker or cane).  Discharge Instructions: Dear Mr. ___, You were admitted to ___ after falling off a dirt bike and you were found to have a traumatic brain injury, nasal bone fractures, a sternal fracture and multiple left rib fractures, a left pneumothorax (abnormal air within the lung lining due to a lung puncture), spine fractures, a right fibula (lower leg bone) fracture, a left radial head fracture (forearm bone), left pelvic fractures, and a right heel fracture. The Neurosurgery service was consulted for your internal head injury as well as for your spine fractures. For your traumatic brain injury, you were initially started on a medication, called Keppra, to prevent seizures. This medication was stopped as it was no longer needed and your repeat head CT scan was stable. Your spine fractures are in stable alignment with your TLSO brace. For your left lung injury, a chest tube was placed to help evacuate the air from your lung lining. Follow-up chest x-rays were obtained and your chest tube was removed when it was no longer needed. Your rib fractures will heal on their own. The Plastic Surgery service evaluated your nasal bone fractures and these will heal on their own. The Orthopedic Surgery service evaluated your arm fractures, pelvic fractures, and right heel fracture. Your right heel fracture was deemed non-operative. It is recommended that you remain non-weight bearing on the right leg, weight bear as tolerated on the left leg, and do not bear weight on the left arm. You were seen by the Acute Pain Service to help establish an oral pain control regimen. You will be discharged home with a week of pain medication. Please contact your primary care provider to arrange for outpatient follow-up within one week for evaluation and to address your pain medication.  You were evaluated by Physical Therapy and you are cleared to be discharged home to continue your recovery. Please follow the instructions below to continue your recovery: Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the Emergency Department for any of the following: *You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or tightness. *New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze. *If you are vomiting and cannot keep down fluids or your medications. *You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea, or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. *You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a bowel movement. *You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your urine, or experience a discharge. *Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or back. *You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius. *Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take any new medications as prescribed. Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. Avoid lifting weights greater than ___ lbs until you follow-up with your surgeon. Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications. Incision Care: *Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner if you have increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision site. *Avoid swimming and baths until your follow-up appointment. *You ___ shower, and wash surgical incisions with a mild soap and warm water. Gently pat the area dry. *If you have staples, they will be removed at your follow-up appointment. *If you have steri-strips, they will fall off on their own. Please remove any remaining strips ___ days after surgery. *wear your TLSO brace when out of bed or sitting up >30 degrees. ___ brace at edge of bed, no twisting.  Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Penicillins / Watermelon / Almond Oil  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: abdominal pain x 1 day  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Laparoscopic Appendectomy  History of Present Illness: The patient is a ___ male type I diabetic who, about 1 day ago, began having vague mid-abdominal pain of a colicky nature. He was standing on the T platform and had an episode of clear non-bilious non-bloody emesis. He had 2 loose BMs. He continued to feel unwell and his pain persisted. Thinking that he might be going into ketoacidosis, he came to the ___ ED to be evaluated.  Past Medical History: 1. Type I diabetes mellitus, uncontrolled. Last HbA1c 10.8 ___. Followed by Dr. ___ at ___. Complicated by gastroparesis, nephropathy. 2. Erosive gastritis per EGD ___. Noncompliant with GI follow up for EGD after ___ hospitalization/elopement. Noncompliant with PPI. 3. Hypertension, uncontrolled 4. Chronic renal insufficiency, baseline 1.5 5. Gastroesophageal reflux disease 6. Depression   Social History: ___ Family History: Mr. ___ has 4 brothers and 5 sisters, all with no known Hx of diabetes or significant medical problems. His parents are alive and well. He reports that his grandfather had ___, but he isnt sure what type.  Physical Exam: 100.3 ___ 16 99RA Gen: healthy male, NAD, no icterus HEENT: NC/AT, EOMI, PERRLA bilat., MMM, without cervical LAD on my exam Cor: RRR without m/g/r, no JVD, no bruits Lungs: CTA bilat. ___: +BS, soft, ND, tender at ___, +voluntary guarding at ___, no masses, mild focal percussion tenderness Ext: warm feet, no edema ______________________ Labs: | 142 | 105 | 51 / 177 AGap=23 | 4.4 | 18 |6.4 \ ALT: 19 AP: 134 Tbili: 0.5 AST: 23 LDH: 326 Lip: 52 13.3 > 31.2 < 278  N:92.5 L:4.8 M:2.1 E:0.6 Bas:0.1  U/A: Bld Mod Prot 500 Glu Tr Ket 15 RBC ___ WBC ___ Bact Few Epi ___ Imaging: CT ___ with po/IV contrast ___: * findings suggesting acute uncomplicated appendicitis with likely appendicolith/retained contrast from prior imaging at base and more distally in appendix, wall thickening, dilation to 11 mm, and surrounding stranding. suggestion of anemia. prostatic enlargement.  Pertinent Results: ___ 06:00AM BLOOD WBC-6.4 RBC-3.27* Hgb-9.3* Hct-27.6* MCV-84 MCH-28.5 MCHC-33.8 RDW-13.3 Plt ___ ___ 06:00AM BLOOD Glucose-76 UreaN-38* Creat-6.8* Na-144 K-4.0 Cl-109* HCO3-21* AnGap-18 ___ 06:00AM BLOOD Calcium-8.2* Phos-4.1 Mg-1.6  Brief Hospital Course: On ___, he was taken to the OR for laparoscopic appendectomy. Surgeon was Dr. ___. Please see operative note for complete report. He did well postop. He experience a hypoglycemic episode on pod 1 that was treated with juice. He felt nauseated and vomited therefore, he remained in the hospital for another day. On ___, his BP was elevated to 223/128. IV lopressor and iv hydralazine were given with BP decreasing to 184/114. An antiemetic was given as he had nausea again. Bowel sounds were hypoactive. Reglan was given and nausea slowly resolved. Diet was tolerated. Vital signs were stable and he was ambulatory. Lap incision sites were intact and without redness. He was discharged home in stable condition.  Medications on Admission: AMLODIPINE - 10 mg Tablet - 1 Tablet(s) by mouth daily GLUCAGON (HUMAN RECOMBINANT) [GLUCAGON EMERGENCY] - 1 mg Kit - use as directed for low blood sugar or passing out INSULIN LISPRO [HUMALOG] - 100 unit/mL Solution - 10 units twice a day METOPROLOL SUCCINATE [TOPROL XL] - 200 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr - 1 Tablet(s) by mouth daily  Medications - OTC BLOOD SUGAR DIAGNOSTIC [FREESTYLE TEST] - Strip - To be used four times daily DEXTROSE [GLUCOSE GEL] - 40 % Gel - ___ Gel(s) by mouth for blood sugar < 60 If blood sugar < 60, take ___ gels and recheck blood sugar in 30 minutes to one hour. FERROUS SULFATE - 325 mg (65 mg Elemental Iron) Tablet - 1 Tablet(s) by mouth daily INSULIN NPH HUMAN RECOMB [HUMULIN N] - 100 unit/mL Suspension - 30 U in the AM, 29 U in the ___ as directed  Discharge Medications: 1. Amlodipine 5 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 2. Insulin Continue Previous Insulin Regimen. Humalog/Humalin 3. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg (65 mg Iron) Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 4. Metoprolol Succinate 100 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr Sig: Two (2) Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr PO DAILY (Daily). 5. Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen ___ mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO every six (6) hours as needed for pain. Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 6. Colace 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO twice a day. Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Acute Appendicitis  Discharge Condition: Stable  Discharge Instructions: Please call your surgeon if you develop chest pain, shortness of breath, fever greater than 101.5, foul smelling or colorful drainage from your incisions, redness or swelling, severe abdominal pain or distention, persistent nausea or vomiting, inability to eat or drink, or any other symptoms which are concerning to you. No tub baths or swimming. You may shower. If there is clear drainage from your incisions, cover with a dry dressing. Leave white strips above your incisions in place, allow them to fall off on their own. Activity: No heavy lifting of items ___ pounds until the follow up appointment with your doctor. Medications: Resume your home medications. You should take a stool softener, Colace 100 mg twice daily as needed for constipation. Pain medication may make you drowsy. No driving while taking pain medicine.  Followup Instructions: ___
