content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: NEUROSURGERY  Allergies: No Drug Allergy Information on File  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: s/p Bicycle crash  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ Irrigation and suture repair of complex facial lacerations ___ Right Craniotomy, resection of temporal tumor  History of Present Illness: ___ y/o male who was reportedly riding his bicycle and fell. Patient reportedly had no LOC and got back on his bike and continued riding. Unknown whether he was wearing a helmet. Was taken to OSH where he was found to have a reported ?IPH and transferred for further management. Upon transfer patient was intubated for agitation and airway protection. He was found to have an EtOH of 220. Father is at bedside and states that patient was an ___ but had been complaining of some dizziness and headaches prior to this incident.  Past Medical History: Denies  Social History: ___ Family History: Non-contibutory  Physical Exam: Upon presentation: O: T:99.2 BP: 115/51 HR:68 R 18 O2Sats 97% Gen: intubated HEENT: Pupils: 3->2BL Neck: Supple. Lungs: CTA bilaterally. Cardiac: RRR. S1/S2. Abd: Soft, NT, BS+ Extrem: Warm and well-perfused. Neuro: Mental status:intubated, combative Cranial Nerves: Pupils equally round and reactive to light, to 2 mm bilaterally. +strong gag. Face symmetric Motor: moving all extremities spontaneously Toes mute bilaterally Physical Exam upon Discharge: awake a+ox3 PERRL, EOMI decreased eye brow raise on right smile symmetric, tongue midline no pronator drift MAE's with ___ strengths sensation intact to light touch incision- well healing, sutures intact ambulating independently, tolerating PO Diet, pain well controlled  Pertinent Results: ___ Head CT IMPRESSION: 1. No acute intracranial hemorrhage. No acute fracture. 2. Large, predominantly CSF-attenuation, cystic lesion with well-marginated medial border, centered in the right middle cranial fossa, likely representing an incidental arachnoid cyst. This process is unchanged over the ensuing three hours, and associated with some scalloping of the overlying squamosal temporal bone (3:30), further proof of its longstanding nature. ___ CT Torso: IMPRESSION: 1. No acute traumatic injury. 2. Bilateral lower lobe collapse. Cannot exclude aspiration ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Large non enhancing intra-axial mass in the right temporal lobe with local mass effect. Appearances are consistent with a primary glial brain tumor. ___ Brain CTA:IMPRESSION: 1. Stable appearance of the right temporal lobe mass and associated mass effect. 2. CTA demonstrates no convincing vascular supply to the right temporal lobe mass. Major venous sinuses are patent. 3. Possible arteriovenous malformation in the region of the left MCA bifurcation. Cerebral angiography is recommended for further evaluation. 4. Fenestration of the distal A1 segment of the left anterior cerebral artery.  ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Preoperative study performed as requested showing no interval change in the size and extent of the right temporal lobe tumor with local mass effect and mild midline shift to the left. ___ Head CT: IMPRESSION: Status post right craniotomy and right temporal lobe mass resection, now with expected subcutaneous gas and pneumocephalus; small focus of dense material may represent blood, but no large intraparenchymal or extra-axial hemorrhage. ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Postoperative changes of right temporal craniotomy without evidence of acute infarct, increase in mass effect or hydrocephalus. Expected post-surgical changes with blood products in the surgical cavity and pneumocephalus as well as soft tissue swelling are identified in the surgical region. Blood products at the surgical site are from postoperative change.   Brief Hospital Course: ___ He was admitted to the ___ service; his complex facial lacerations were irrigated and suture repaired in the ED. Neurosurgery consultation was obtained for the right mid cranial fossa measuring 5.6 X 3.6 lesion. He was started on Dilantin for seizure prophylaxis. He was cleared from a trauma standpoint and was transfered to the neurosurgery service for further care. Final plastics recommendations included keflex for 1 week, peridex mouthwash three times daily and to follow up in clinic for suture removal on ___. ___ Transfered to neurosurgery service. CTA/CTV of the head was obtained to evaluate the blood supply of his R temporal tumor prior to his surgery on ___. His pain medications were increased to every 3 hours as he continued to have some facial and rib pain,though his CT chest was negative for fracture. Viscous lidocaine mouthwash was also added to help control his mouth pain. In a family discussion with the patient and his parents it was decided to undergo elective craniotomy for resection and biopsy on ___. The neuro-oncology team was consulted for further managment of his lesion. The neuro-opthamology team was also consulted to obtain formal visual field testing given the location of his tumor and the risk of visual field deficit post operatively. On ___ pt underwent MRI head with WAND study for pre-operative planning. He was kept NPO at midnight and placed on IVF for hydration. On ___ he underwent a right temporal craniotomy for evacuation and biopsy of his temporal lesion. Post operatively he was transfered to the ICU for strict blood pressure control and q1 neuro checks. A post operative head ct showed stable post operative changes. He remained stable overnight and was cleared for transfer to the floor on ___. A routine post operative MRI was done which revealed good tumor resection and no hemorrhage or infarct. PO diet was advanced and SQ heparin was initiated. On ___ physical therapy was consulted for discharge planning. He was started on Metoprolol BID for persistant elevated blood pressure and thorazine for hiccups. Pain was adequately controlled. On ___ systolic blood pressure was noted to be under much better control 120-130. He was ambulating independently and pain was well controlled. He was cleared at this time for discharge home. He will follow up in the ___ once he has obtained insurance and will follow up with his PCP for following of his blood pressure. We will see him in approximately 1 week for suture removal.   Medications on Admission: none  Discharge Medications: 1. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day) as needed for constipation. 2. Milk of Magnesia 400 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig: ___ MLs PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for constipation. 3. Senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO DAILY (Daily) as needed for Constipation. 4. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 5. Levetiracetam 500 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 6. Hydromorphone 2 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain/HA. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 7. Ranitidine HCl 150 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 8. Chlorpromazine 25 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for persistent hiccups. Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 9. Dexamethasone 0.5 mg Tablet Sig: taper Tablet PO taper: Take 3mg every 8 hours on ___. Take 3mg every 12hours on ___ and ___. Take 2mg every 12 hours starting ___ and continue until follow up. Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: s/p Bicycle crash Complex facial lacerations Right mid cranial fossa 5.6 X 3.6 lesion s/p craniotomy and resection  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: GENERAL INSTRUCTIONS WOUND CARE: You or a family member should inspect your wound every day and report any of the following problems to your physician. Keep your incision clean and dry. You may wash your hair with a mild shampoo 24 hours after your sutures are removed. Do NOT apply any lotions, ointments or other products to your incision. DO NOT DRIVE until you are seen at the first follow up appointment. Do not lift objects over 10 pounds until approved by your physician. DIET Usually no special diet is prescribed after a craniotomy. A normal well balanced diet is recommended for recovery, and you should resume any specially prescribed diet you were eating before your surgery. Be sure however, to remain well hydrated, and increase your consumption of fiber, as pain medications may cause constipation. MEDICATIONS: Take all of your medications as ordered. You do not have to take pain medication unless it is needed. It is important that you are able to cough, breathe deeply, and is comfortable enough to walk. Do not use alcohol while taking pain medication. Medications that may be prescribed include: oNarcotic pain medication such as Dilaudid (hydromorphone). oAn over the counter stool softener for constipation (Colace or Docusate). If you become constipated, try products such as Dulcolax, Milk of Magnesia, first, and then Magnesium Citrate or Fleets enema if needed). Often times, pain medication and anesthesia can cause constipation. If you have been prescribed Dilantin (Phenytoin) for anti-seizure medicine, take it as prescribed and follow up with laboratory blood drawing in one week. This can be drawn at your PCPs office, but please have the results faxed to ___. If you have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam), you will not require blood work monitoring. Unless directed by your doctor, do not take any anti-inflammatory medicines such as Motrin, Aspirin, Advil, and Ibuprofen etc, as this can increase your chances of bleeding. If you are being sent home on steroid medication, make sure you are taking a medication to protect your stomach (Prilosec, Protonix, or Pepcid), as these medications can cause stomach irritation. Make sure to take your steroid medication with meals, or a glass of milk. ACTIVITY: The first few weeks after you are discharged you may feel tired or fatigued. This is normal. You should become a little stronger every day. Activity is the most important measure you can take to prevent complications and to begin to feel like yourself again. In general: Follow the activity instructions given to you by your doctor and therapist. Increase your activity slowly; do not do too much because you are feeling good. You may resume sexual activity as your tolerance allows. If you feel light headed or fatigued after increasing activity, rest, decrease the amount of activity that you do, and begin building your tolerance to activity more slowly. DO NOT DRIVE until you speak with your physician. Do not lift objects over 10 pounds until approved by your physician. Avoid any activity that causes you to hold your breath and push, for example weight lifting, lifting or moving heavy objects, or straining at stool. Do your breathing exercises every two hours. Use your incentive spirometer 10 times every hour, that you are awake.  Followup Instructions: ___  Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: PSYCHIATRY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: I'm feeling suicidal.  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: ___ y/o man with h/o POTS and bradycardia who had a recent lengthy Deaconess 4 admission for depression and suicidal ideation treated with ECT, self-presents to the ___ with thoughts of killing himself by overdosing on pills. Mr. ___ has had multiple past psychiatric hopsitalizations for suicidal ideation and one past suicide attempt by stabbing himself in the heart. Although there is no discharge summary available in OMR from his recent inpatient psychiatric hospitalization on ___, Dr. ___ from ___ indicates that the patient's hospitalization was for an apparent acute exacerbation of chronic, fairly severe depression, likely related both to an endogenous depression and to characterologic features that have contributed as much as anything to his current social isolation, poor frustration tolerance, and generalized demoralization. The patient reported tolerating ECT well, had improved mood with ECT, and was feeling more optimistic upon his recent ___ 4 discharge. However, Mr. ___ reports not being able to follow through with the outpatient plan for maintenance ECT due to complications with his POTS causing transportation difficulties. Mr. ___ has had multiple recent presentations to the ___ emergency department with negative cardiac workups. The patient reports being angry with ___ and believes that they are not providing him with proper medical care. On presentation to the ED, the paitent reported walking up and down ___ for a couple hours prior to entering the ED. He reports trying to find some way to resolve his feelings of hopelessness; however, when the urge to kill himself scared him, he finally made the decision to self-present for psychiatric evaluation. The patient reports active suicidality with a plan to overdose on his home medications. He says there is no safe outpatient plan. He reports that he has plenty of pills at home that he could overdose. He denies access to firearms but does report having knives similar to the one he stabbed himself with in a past suicide attempt. On ROS, the patient reports poor mood, +guilt about not being able to see his children often, +racing thoughts, +chronic insomnia ___ hours per night at baseline). He denies changes in his concentration, appetite, or weight. He is equivocal about whether or not he has anhedonia. His main protective factor is reportedly his children. He denies history of manic symptoms. He denies symptoms of psychosis.  Past Medical History: PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: - CAD, followed by Dr. ___ at ___. Extensive cardiac workup (see ___ notes for full details). Reliant monitor implanted in his chest in ___. ___ - Seen by neurology at ___ and thought to have POTS. Did respond to midodrine but with exacerbation of urinary retention. - Urinary retention: High PVRs, seen by Dr. ___ at ___. - S/P self-inflicted stab wound: anterior left chest ___ - ligation of diag coranary artery and resxn of LUL lung tear, repaired superficial lac to LV. Fully recovered per thoracic surgeon at ___ without ___ - s/p diverticulitis with partial bowel resection about ___ years ago - s/p right knee ACL repair - PCP is ___ at ___ ___ PAST PSYCHIATRIC HISTORY: - Major depression with narcissistic character features. Attempted suicide in ___ by stabbing himself (see above). Multiple psychiatry admits to ___, ___, and most recently at ___ Deac 4 where he underwent ECT with good results. Followed by Dr ___ at ___. - Previous medical trials: Depakote, Zoloft, Effexor, Lamictal, Lithium, Remeron, and Trazodone. Currently on Wellbutrin.  Social History: ___ Family History: Denies.  Physical Exam: PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: VS: BP: 118/77 HR: 52 temp: 97.6 resp: 16 O2 sat: 100% height: 70 weight: 244.6 lb  MENTAL STATUS EXAM: --appearance: good grooming with good eye contact --behavior/attitude: cooperative; exhibited no PMR, PMA --speech: normal rate and tone --mood (in patient's words): lousy --affect: constricted affect; appropriate to context --thought content (describe): Future-oriented, hoping to get help for his psychiatric and medical problems --thought process: linear --perception: without AH, VH, delusions, paranoia --SI/HI: denies SI/HI; verbalized safety plan --insight: fair --judgment: fair  COGNITIVE EXAM: --orientation: alert to person, place, time, situation --attention/concentration: able to spell World backwards --memory (ball, chair, purple): registration ___, recall ___ initially and ___ with hints --calculations: quarters in $2.25 = 9 --language: grossly intact --fund of knowledge: able to recall the president on ___ --proverbs: provided accurate interpretations of look before leap, cry over spilt milk, apple falling from tree --similarities/analogies: understood analogy of apples to oranges PE: General: Well-nourished, in no distress. HEENT: Normocephalic. PERRL, EOMI. ___ normal. Oropharynx clear. Neck: Supple, trachea midline. No adenopathy or thyromegaly. Back: No significant deformity, no focal tenderness Lungs: Clear to auscultation; no crackles or wheezes. CV: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs/rubs/gallops; 2+ pedal pulses Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended; no masses or organomegaly. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin: Warm and dry, no rash or significant lesions. Neurological: *Cranial Nerves- I: Not tested II: Pupils equally round and reactive to light bilaterally. Visual fields are full to confrontation. III, IV, VI: Extraocular movements intact bilaterally without nystagmus. V, VII: Facial strength and sensation intact and symmetric. VIII: Hearing intact to voice. IX, X: Palatal elevation symmetrical. XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius normal bilaterally. XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations. *No gross focal motor or sensory deficits, normal gait. *Reflexes- B 1+ T 1+ Pa 1+ *Coordination- Normal on rapid alternating movements, heel to shin  Pertinent Results: ___ 02:10AM URINE bnzodzpn-NEG barbitrt-NEG opiates-NEG cocaine-NEG amphetmn-NEG mthdone-NEG ___ 02:10AM URINE BLOOD-NEG NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-NEG GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-1 PH-7.0 LEUK-NEG ___ 12:49AM GLUCOSE-83 UREA N-18 CREAT-0.9 SODIUM-140 POTASSIUM-4.2 CHLORIDE-106 TOTAL CO2-27 ANION GAP-11 ___ 12:49AM ALT(SGPT)-10 AST(SGOT)-16 ALK PHOS-65 TOT BILI-0.5 ___ 12:49AM CALCIUM-8.5 PHOSPHATE-3.7 MAGNESIUM-2.1 ___ 12:49AM VIT B12-172* FOLATE-10.8 ___ 12:49AM TSH-1.2 ___ 12:49AM ASA-NEG ETHANOL-NEG ACETMNPHN-NEG bnzodzpn-NEG barbitrt-NEG tricyclic-NEG ___ 12:49AM WBC-7.0 RBC-4.19* HGB-12.7* HCT-36.0* MCV-86 MCH-30.2 MCHC-35.2* RDW-13.9 ___ 12:49AM NEUTS-67.5 ___ MONOS-5.2 EOS-1.3 BASOS-0.6 ___ 12:49AM PLT COUNT-208  Brief Hospital Course: Psychiatric: Mr. ___ was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric service on ___ on ___. He settled into the milieu uneventfully. At his initial meeting with his care team, he was tearful and depressed, discussing his recent thoughts of suicide following his multiple medical admissions for symptoms of POTS, since being discharged from ___ three weeks prior. The team spoke with his outpatient psychiatrist Dr. ___ provided the collateral information that Mr. ___ had been on a great variety of anti-depressant regimens in the past with little to no effect on his mood. Dr. ___ that the only major improvmement he had ever seen in the patient's depression and suicidality occured after his recent course of ECT. The team then contacted Dr. ___ an ECT consultation. He was evaluated by medicine and anesthesia and found to be low-risk for this treatment modality. He started his first session of ECT on ___ and had a total of 5 sessions while in house. The team also opted to augment his medical antidepressant therapy of wellbutrin SR with abilify. His abilify dose was titrated up throughout his admission. When his abilify dose reached 20 mg, he began to experience some akathisia. His dose was then decreased to 15 mg, he he tolerated this well. He frequently spent time in the common areas of the psychiatric unit and attended groups regularly with good particpation. The team worked with Mr. ___ to find individuals who might be able to help him to complete ECT as an outpatient, but he was not able to identify anyone. He ultimately decided that he would be willing to pay someone to bring him to and from ECT. He could then be observed at home by a friend, who was free during the day but who was unable to drive. By the time of discharge, his mood had brigthened and he was no longer have suicidal thoughts. The team spoke with his outpatient psychiatrist, Dr. ___, ___ his outpatient PCP, ___, to update them on his progress and discharge plan. Dr. ___ ECT ___, and the inpatient team agreed that he would be discharged home on ___ to follow up at the ___ the following ___ to begin maintenance therapy. He would receive one session per week for four weeks, and then would move to one session every 2 weeks. He was scheduled to see his Psychiatrist and PCP within ___ week of discharge. When he left the unit, he was future-oriented, looking forward to seeing his kids at his son's football game this weekend and returning to work. He voiced strategies to cope with future suicidal thoughts, including distracting himself with work or TV, reaching out to a friend, or contacting one of his care providers if necessary. Medical: Mr. ___ was continued on his outpatient midodrine and pindolol for postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome while on the unit. He experienced intermittent light-headedness, but had no syncopal episodes. His blood pressure was consistently within the normal range. The team scheduled an apppointment for him at the ___ Clinics for early ___. The clinic was hopeful that they could fit him into a cancellation spot before that time. The team also made him a follow up appointment with his PCP ___ for the week after discharge. Legal: ___ Safety: Mr. ___ was maintained on q 15 minute checks and did not have any behavioral triggers during his stay. Group/Milieu: Over the course of his admission, the patient participated well in groups. It was recommended to him that he attend an outpatient interpersonal/psychodynamic group to address his ongoing pattern of difficulty within relationships, something that contributes to his depression and suicidal thoughts. He was provided with the phone numbers of several groups at ___ that may meet his needs. He agreed to consider contacting them for an interview.  Medications on Admission: - Pindolol 10 mg po BID - Midodrine 5 mg po BID - Wellbutrin 200 mg po BID - Ambien 10 mg po QHS  Discharge Medications: 1. pindolol 5 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 2. midodrine 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 3. bupropion HCl 100 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: Two (2) Tablet Sustained Release PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*120 Tablet Sustained Release(s)* Refills:*0* 4. aripiprazole 15 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 5. zolpidem 5 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: I. Major Depression, severe recurrent II. defer III. POTS syndrome (orthostatic tachycardia syndrome), diverticulitis IV. social isolation, multiple recent medical hospitalizations V. 55  Discharge Condition: Vitals 97.9 57 107/61 12 98% RA MSE: General: well groomed, good eye contact, looking stated age, pleasant and cooperative Speech: regular Mood/Affect: 'good'/euthymic T. Form: linear T. Content: denies hallucinations. Ongoing improvement in suicidality, denies any current suicidal thoughts. No longer hopeless. No paranoia or delusuions. More future oriented, reflective on social interactions Cognition : a and o x 3 J/I: fair/fair  Discharge Instructions: You have been started on a new medication called abilify. Please take all your medications as prescribed and follow up at the appointments listed below.  Followup Instructions: ___

sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: NEUROSURGERY  Allergies: No Drug Allergy Information on File  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: s/p Bicycle crash  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ Irrigation and suture repair of complex facial lacerations ___ Right Craniotomy, resection of temporal tumor  History of Present Illness: ___ y/o male who was reportedly riding his bicycle and fell. Patient reportedly had no LOC and got back on his bike and continued riding. Unknown whether he was wearing a helmet. Was taken to OSH where he was found to have a reported ?IPH and transferred for further management. Upon transfer patient was intubated for agitation and airway protection. He was found to have an EtOH of 220. Father is at bedside and states that patient was an ___ but had been complaining of some dizziness and headaches prior to this incident.  Past Medical History: Denies  Social History: ___ Family History: Non-contibutory  Physical Exam: Upon presentation: O: T:99.2 BP: 115/51 HR:68 R 18 O2Sats 97% Gen: intubated HEENT: Pupils: 3->2BL Neck: Supple. Lungs: CTA bilaterally. Cardiac: RRR. S1/S2. Abd: Soft, NT, BS+ Extrem: Warm and well-perfused. Neuro: Mental status:intubated, combative Cranial Nerves: Pupils equally round and reactive to light, to 2 mm bilaterally. +strong gag. Face symmetric Motor: moving all extremities spontaneously Toes mute bilaterally Physical Exam upon Discharge: awake a+ox3 PERRL, EOMI decreased eye brow raise on right smile symmetric, tongue midline no pronator drift MAE's with ___ strengths sensation intact to light touch incision- well healing, sutures intact ambulating independently, tolerating PO Diet, pain well controlled  Pertinent Results: ___ Head CT IMPRESSION: 1. No acute intracranial hemorrhage. No acute fracture. 2. Large, predominantly CSF-attenuation, cystic lesion with well-marginated medial border, centered in the right middle cranial fossa, likely representing an incidental arachnoid cyst. This process is unchanged over the ensuing three hours, and associated with some scalloping of the overlying squamosal temporal bone (3:30), further proof of its longstanding nature. ___ CT Torso: IMPRESSION: 1. No acute traumatic injury. 2. Bilateral lower lobe collapse. Cannot exclude aspiration ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Large non enhancing intra-axial mass in the right temporal lobe with local mass effect. Appearances are consistent with a primary glial brain tumor. ___ Brain CTA:IMPRESSION: 1. Stable appearance of the right temporal lobe mass and associated mass effect. 2. CTA demonstrates no convincing vascular supply to the right temporal lobe mass. Major venous sinuses are patent. 3. Possible arteriovenous malformation in the region of the left MCA bifurcation. Cerebral angiography is recommended for further evaluation. 4. Fenestration of the distal A1 segment of the left anterior cerebral artery.  ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Preoperative study performed as requested showing no interval change in the size and extent of the right temporal lobe tumor with local mass effect and mild midline shift to the left. ___ Head CT: IMPRESSION: Status post right craniotomy and right temporal lobe mass resection, now with expected subcutaneous gas and pneumocephalus; small focus of dense material may represent blood, but no large intraparenchymal or extra-axial hemorrhage. ___ Brain MRI: IMPRESSION: Postoperative changes of right temporal craniotomy without evidence of acute infarct, increase in mass effect or hydrocephalus. Expected post-surgical changes with blood products in the surgical cavity and pneumocephalus as well as soft tissue swelling are identified in the surgical region. Blood products at the surgical site are from postoperative change.   Brief Hospital Course: ___ He was admitted to the ___ service; his complex facial lacerations were irrigated and suture repaired in the ED. Neurosurgery consultation was obtained for the right mid cranial fossa measuring 5.6 X 3.6 lesion. He was started on Dilantin for seizure prophylaxis. He was cleared from a trauma standpoint and was transfered to the neurosurgery service for further care. Final plastics recommendations included keflex for 1 week, peridex mouthwash three times daily and to follow up in clinic for suture removal on ___. ___ Transfered to neurosurgery service. CTA/CTV of the head was obtained to evaluate the blood supply of his R temporal tumor prior to his surgery on ___. His pain medications were increased to every 3 hours as he continued to have some facial and rib pain,though his CT chest was negative for fracture. Viscous lidocaine mouthwash was also added to help control his mouth pain. In a family discussion with the patient and his parents it was decided to undergo elective craniotomy for resection and biopsy on ___. The neuro-oncology team was consulted for further managment of his lesion. The neuro-opthamology team was also consulted to obtain formal visual field testing given the location of his tumor and the risk of visual field deficit post operatively. On ___ pt underwent MRI head with WAND study for pre-operative planning. He was kept NPO at midnight and placed on IVF for hydration. On ___ he underwent a right temporal craniotomy for evacuation and biopsy of his temporal lesion. Post operatively he was transfered to the ICU for strict blood pressure control and q1 neuro checks. A post operative head ct showed stable post operative changes. He remained stable overnight and was cleared for transfer to the floor on ___. A routine post operative MRI was done which revealed good tumor resection and no hemorrhage or infarct. PO diet was advanced and SQ heparin was initiated. On ___ physical therapy was consulted for discharge planning. He was started on Metoprolol BID for persistant elevated blood pressure and thorazine for hiccups. Pain was adequately controlled. On ___ systolic blood pressure was noted to be under much better control 120-130. He was ambulating independently and pain was well controlled. He was cleared at this time for discharge home. He will follow up in the ___ once he has obtained insurance and will follow up with his PCP for following of his blood pressure. We will see him in approximately 1 week for suture removal.   Medications on Admission: none  Discharge Medications: 1. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2 times a day) as needed for constipation. 2. Milk of Magnesia 400 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig: ___ MLs PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for constipation. 3. Senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO DAILY (Daily) as needed for Constipation. 4. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 5. Levetiracetam 500 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 6. Hydromorphone 2 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain/HA. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 7. Ranitidine HCl 150 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a day). Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2* 8. Chlorpromazine 25 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for persistent hiccups. Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 9. Dexamethasone 0.5 mg Tablet Sig: taper Tablet PO taper: Take 3mg every 8 hours on ___. Take 3mg every 12hours on ___ and ___. Take 2mg every 12 hours starting ___ and continue until follow up. Disp:*120 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: s/p Bicycle crash Complex facial lacerations Right mid cranial fossa 5.6 X 3.6 lesion s/p craniotomy and resection  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: GENERAL INSTRUCTIONS WOUND CARE: You or a family member should inspect your wound every day and report any of the following problems to your physician. Keep your incision clean and dry. You may wash your hair with a mild shampoo 24 hours after your sutures are removed. Do NOT apply any lotions, ointments or other products to your incision. DO NOT DRIVE until you are seen at the first follow up appointment. Do not lift objects over 10 pounds until approved by your physician. DIET Usually no special diet is prescribed after a craniotomy. A normal well balanced diet is recommended for recovery, and you should resume any specially prescribed diet you were eating before your surgery. Be sure however, to remain well hydrated, and increase your consumption of fiber, as pain medications may cause constipation. MEDICATIONS: Take all of your medications as ordered. You do not have to take pain medication unless it is needed. It is important that you are able to cough, breathe deeply, and is comfortable enough to walk. Do not use alcohol while taking pain medication. Medications that may be prescribed include: oNarcotic pain medication such as Dilaudid (hydromorphone). oAn over the counter stool softener for constipation (Colace or Docusate). If you become constipated, try products such as Dulcolax, Milk of Magnesia, first, and then Magnesium Citrate or Fleets enema if needed). Often times, pain medication and anesthesia can cause constipation. If you have been prescribed Dilantin (Phenytoin) for anti-seizure medicine, take it as prescribed and follow up with laboratory blood drawing in one week. This can be drawn at your PCPs office, but please have the results faxed to ___. If you have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam), you will not require blood work monitoring. Unless directed by your doctor, do not take any anti-inflammatory medicines such as Motrin, Aspirin, Advil, and Ibuprofen etc, as this can increase your chances of bleeding. If you are being sent home on steroid medication, make sure you are taking a medication to protect your stomach (Prilosec, Protonix, or Pepcid), as these medications can cause stomach irritation. Make sure to take your steroid medication with meals, or a glass of milk. ACTIVITY: The first few weeks after you are discharged you may feel tired or fatigued. This is normal. You should become a little stronger every day. Activity is the most important measure you can take to prevent complications and to begin to feel like yourself again. In general: Follow the activity instructions given to you by your doctor and therapist. Increase your activity slowly; do not do too much because you are feeling good. You may resume sexual activity as your tolerance allows. If you feel light headed or fatigued after increasing activity, rest, decrease the amount of activity that you do, and begin building your tolerance to activity more slowly. DO NOT DRIVE until you speak with your physician. Do not lift objects over 10 pounds until approved by your physician. Avoid any activity that causes you to hold your breath and push, for example weight lifting, lifting or moving heavy objects, or straining at stool. Do your breathing exercises every two hours. Use your incentive spirometer 10 times every hour, that you are awake.  Followup Instructions: ___

sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: PSYCHIATRY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: I'm feeling suicidal.  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: ___ y/o man with h/o POTS and bradycardia who had a recent lengthy Deaconess 4 admission for depression and suicidal ideation treated with ECT, self-presents to the ___ with thoughts of killing himself by overdosing on pills. Mr. ___ has had multiple past psychiatric hopsitalizations for suicidal ideation and one past suicide attempt by stabbing himself in the heart. Although there is no discharge summary available in OMR from his recent inpatient psychiatric hospitalization on ___, Dr. ___ from ___ indicates that the patient's hospitalization was for an apparent acute exacerbation of chronic, fairly severe depression, likely related both to an endogenous depression and to characterologic features that have contributed as much as anything to his current social isolation, poor frustration tolerance, and generalized demoralization. The patient reported tolerating ECT well, had improved mood with ECT, and was feeling more optimistic upon his recent ___ 4 discharge. However, Mr. ___ reports not being able to follow through with the outpatient plan for maintenance ECT due to complications with his POTS causing transportation difficulties. Mr. ___ has had multiple recent presentations to the ___ emergency department with negative cardiac workups. The patient reports being angry with ___ and believes that they are not providing him with proper medical care. On presentation to the ED, the paitent reported walking up and down ___ for a couple hours prior to entering the ED. He reports trying to find some way to resolve his feelings of hopelessness; however, when the urge to kill himself scared him, he finally made the decision to self-present for psychiatric evaluation. The patient reports active suicidality with a plan to overdose on his home medications. He says there is no safe outpatient plan. He reports that he has plenty of pills at home that he could overdose. He denies access to firearms but does report having knives similar to the one he stabbed himself with in a past suicide attempt. On ROS, the patient reports poor mood, +guilt about not being able to see his children often, +racing thoughts, +chronic insomnia ___ hours per night at baseline). He denies changes in his concentration, appetite, or weight. He is equivocal about whether or not he has anhedonia. His main protective factor is reportedly his children. He denies history of manic symptoms. He denies symptoms of psychosis.  Past Medical History: PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: - CAD, followed by Dr. ___ at ___. Extensive cardiac workup (see ___ notes for full details). Reliant monitor implanted in his chest in ___. ___ - Seen by neurology at ___ and thought to have POTS. Did respond to midodrine but with exacerbation of urinary retention. - Urinary retention: High PVRs, seen by Dr. ___ at ___. - S/P self-inflicted stab wound: anterior left chest ___ - ligation of diag coranary artery and resxn of LUL lung tear, repaired superficial lac to LV. content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: Augmentin / Bactrim / Biaxin / Iodine / Nsaids / Penicillins / IV Dye, Iodine Containing / Symbicort  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Weakness, hypotension  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Cardiac catheterization with PCI ___  History of Present Illness: HISTORY OF THE PRESENTING ILLNESS: ___ with complicated past medical history who presents with about 1 month of weakness and hypotension. Mr. ___ primary complaint is ___ weeks of generalized weakness and hypotension. His metoprolol was recently stopped due to BPs below his baseline (prior baseline ON antihypertensives was 140, now 90-100 off BP meds). He gets lightheaded when SBP 90. His HD team was unable to finish HD today b/c of hypotension down to systolics in the ___ and weakness (he was fully dialyzed but with minimal volume removal, currently 101kg post-HD with dry weight of 100kg). His wife brought him to the ED due to this persistent hypotension. This comes in the contxt of a very complicated recent course: He had a recent admission ___ for fever thought to be due to bacteremia and PNA (though source never confirmed) during which he had an NSTEMI that was medically managed. On that admission stress showed inferior mild reversible perfusion defect at the apex, moderate fixed inferior wall perfusion defect. An echocardiogram showed asymmetric septal hypertrophy with mild global LV systolic dysfunction with an estimated EF of 45-50%. He saw his cardiologist (___) in follow up ___ with complaint of hypotension and weakness. To r/o cardiac cause he had an ECHO which showed newly depressed EF: Overall left ventricular systolic function is moderately to severely depressed (LVEF= ___ %) with relative preservation of the lateral wall and profound hypokinesis of the septum. Left ventricular dysnchrony is present. A left ventricular mass/thrombus cannot be excluded. Also had a Holter monitor without significant arrhythmia noted. Given LV thrombus, he was scheduled for cardiac MRI today but missed the apt due to the events at HD as above. He also has a chronic R lateral malleolus wound being treated by his podiatrist Dr. ___. This was evaluated by podiatry inpatient at ___ during ___ admission and it was felt that it was not infected. Per wife this has grown in size and also has begun to express foul smelling odor. She says the outpt podiatrist reported that the wound was very close to the bone when he debrided it. Superficial culture has been prelim concerning for staph but no final speciation reported. He started HD dosing of vanco with 2nd dose today). In the ED, initial vitals were: 96.5 80 105/63 14 100% 3L NC. Labs notable for Cr 5.1 (at baseline), hgb 13 (at baseline), no leukocytosis. Trop 0.63 with normal ck/mb. CXR neg. Ankle x ray showed Soft tissue ulceration inferior to the right lateral malleolus without radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. EKG showed SR @ 81, NA, PR 204ms/QTc 491, TWI I/aVL and 1mm STE V1/V2 CWP. He received: IV cefepime. On the floor, he denies any current or recent chest pain, sob, orthopnea beyond baseline orthopnea. He's had no fevers. He has no pain at R lateral malleolus (he has neuropathy to the knee).  Past Medical History: ESRD (on HD since ___, AVF in forearm, ___ CAD status-post MI in ___ status-post PTCAx2 at ___, STEMI s/p ___ ___ He is followed by Dr. ___. Type II Diabetes (poorly controlled, complicated by nephropathy and retinopathy and neuropathy, FSBG 180-240 usually) Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure EF>55% in ___ Restrictive Lung Disease (after heart attack, sees Dr. ___ ___ on 3L Home O2 at rest/sitting or 4L if exerting himself Obstructive Sleep Apnea on BiPAP ___ @ 6L) Morbid Obesity Neurogenic Bladder with recurrent UTIs and Suprapubic Catheter since ___ (since ___ gave ibuprofen, Kidneys went down and had fluid overload and never regained muscle tone) Hypertension Osteomyelitis status-post left ___ metatarsal amputation Recurrent Clostridium difficile infections Glaucoma Cataract Surgery (bilateral, no glasses, left eye is good) Anxiety GERD  Social History: ___ Family History: Unknown biological family history and as patient is adopted and has no siblings. Knows that mother was ___ ___, father was ___, and he was a product of rape  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM: ============================= Vital Signs: 97.5 ___ 18 95 4L (at baseline) General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress HEENT: Sclerae anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear, EOMI, PERRL, neck supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally but diminished air entry, no wheezes, rales, rhonchi Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding GU: No foley Ext: Pitting edema to the knee, chronic venous stasis changes overlying. Ulcer with weeping purulent discharge, about 4 cm by 3 cm, 0.4 cm deep in the deepest part, with no exposed bone, overall lateral right ankle. Some necrotic skin in areas surrounding. Neuro: CNII-XII grossly intact, moving all extremities. DISCHARGE PHYSICAL EXAM: ============================== Vital Signs: 97.4 ___ ___ 92% 3L Weight 104.1kg -> 101.6kg -> 100.7kg -> 106.9kg (at HD) Pt makes minimal urine. General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress, resting comfortably in bed at dialysis HEENT: Sclerae anicteric, MMM, poor dentition, neck supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD. CV: RRR, no m/r/g Lungs: mildly diminished air entry diffusely, fine rales at bilateral bases. Abdomen: non-distended, bowel sounds present; soft and non-tender with no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding. GU: Suprapubic catheter in place, no surrounding erythema. UEs: R forearm fistula with successful dialysis access LEs: Right foot in boot. Trace pitting edema bilaterally. Warm extremities, bilaterally. MSE: A&Ox3, mood good, affect euthymic and stable, normal range. CN: Pupils 3mm and minimally reactive b/l, EOMI, CNII-XII otherwise intact. Reflexes: symmetric ___ in upper and lower extremities. Motor: Normal muscle bulk and tone. No adventitious movements, no asterixis, no pronator drift. ___ strength biceps, ___ triceps, wrist flexion/extension, hip flexors b/l. Sensation to LT intact to just below knee b/l, absent distally; intact to LT in b/l UEs. Unable to plantar or dorsiflex bilateral feet at baseline. Coordination: intact to FNF b/l. Gait: deferred, wheelchair bound  Pertinent Results: ADMISSION LABS: =================== ___ 04:50PM PLT COUNT-141* ___ 04:50PM NEUTS-74.8* LYMPHS-14.9* MONOS-7.6 EOS-2.1 BASOS-0.3 IM ___ AbsNeut-6.42* AbsLymp-1.28 AbsMono-0.65 AbsEos-0.18 AbsBaso-0.03 ___ 04:50PM WBC-8.6 RBC-4.00* HGB-13.4* HCT-39.8* MCV-100* MCH-33.5* MCHC-33.7 RDW-14.6 RDWSD-52.3* ___ 04:50PM ALBUMIN-3.5 CALCIUM-9.4 PHOSPHATE-3.5 MAGNESIUM-2.1 ___ 04:50PM CK-MB-3 ___ 04:50PM cTropnT-0.62* ___ 04:50PM CK(CPK)-81 ___ 04:50PM estGFR-Using this ___ 04:50PM GLUCOSE-174* UREA N-33* CREAT-5.1* SODIUM-134 POTASSIUM-5.0 CHLORIDE-96 TOTAL CO2-28 ANION GAP-15 ___ 05:01PM LACTATE-1.2 K+-4.7 PERTINENT LABS: =================== ___ 04:50PM BLOOD CK-MB-3 ___ 04:50PM BLOOD cTropnT-0.62* ___ 12:04AM BLOOD CK-MB-3 cTropnT-0.59* ___ 06:17AM BLOOD CK-MB-2 cTropnT-0.65* ___ 06:17AM BLOOD Cortsol-29.2* ___ 12:04AM BLOOD CRP-40.7* ___ 06:22AM BLOOD Vanco-8.2* IMAGING/STUDIES: ==================== ___ FOCUSED TTE FOCUSED STUDY- Overall left ventricular systolic function is moderately to severely depressed (LVEF <30 %) with dyskinesis of the apex and distal septum, akinesis of the basal to mid septum, and hypokinesis of all other segments. The basal lateral wall is relatively preserved in contractility. The right ventricle is at least mildly dilated with mild to moderate global hypokinesis. No masses or thrombi are seen in the left ventricle. ___ X RAY ANKLE Soft tissue ulceration inferior to the right lateral malleolus without radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. Please note that MRI is a more sensitive examination for the detection of osteomyelitis. ___ MR ___ contrast: IMPRESSION: 1. No MR evidence of osteomyelitis. 2. There is a small amount of non-specific edema in the calcaneus just inferior to a chronic fracture in the posterior superior calcaneus. 2. Diffuse edema in the plantar muscles may be related to denervation or myositis. ___ Cath: Coronary Anatomy Dominance: Right * Left Main Coronary Artery The LMCA is calcified without significant stenosis. * Left Anterior Descending There is a 99% stenosis in the Proximal LAD. The lesion has a TIMI flow of 2 and has severe calcification noted. This lesion is further described as tubular. An intervention was performed on the Proximal LAD with a final stenosis of 0%. There were no lesion complications. * Circumflex The Circumflex hjas poroximal calcification and mild eccentric stenosis. Beyond OM1 there is 70% stenosis. * Right Coronary Artery The RCA is totally occluded at site of prior ___ thrombosis. Interventional Details A 6 ___ XB LAD 4 provided good support. A Prowater wire was first placed in circumflex for protection and the LAD lesion was then crossed with a second Prowater wire. The lesion was predilated with 2.5 x 15 balloon at 12 ATM. A 3.0 x 16 Promus DES was deployed at 16 ATM with proximal edge just inside the left main and post dilated with 3.5 x 12 balloon at ___ ATM. Final angiography showed no residual, TIMI 3 flow and no dissection. There was no compromise of the left circumflex. Intra-procedural Complications: None Impressions: 1. Severe 3 vessel CAD with culprit ostial LAD lesion. 2. Successful ___. Recommendations 1. Continue aspirin 81 mg daily; Plavix 75 mg daily. Discharge Instructions: Dear Mr. ___: You were admitted because of low blood pressures and generally feeling weak. You had a cardiac cath which showed a narrowing in one of the major blood vessels that brings blood to your heart (the LAD) so a drug-eluting ___ (DES) was placed. You had an MRI of your foot which did NOT show any signs of infection of the bone, so you will undergo a 2 week course of two antibiotics for infection of the skin (___) with vancomycin at dialysis. You were started on a medication called midodrine to keep your blood pressures up, which should be taken three times per day on the days you get dialysis. If your blood pressures are less than 90 on the days you do NOT get dialysis, or if you are symptomatic (dizzy, weak, lightheaded) on those days, you should take the midodrine three times per day on those days as well. Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more than 3 lbs. It was a pleasure to care for you. Your ___ Team  Followup Instructions: ___

sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: Augmentin / Bactrim / Biaxin / Iodine / Nsaids / Penicillins / IV Dye, Iodine Containing / Symbicort  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Weakness, hypotension  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Cardiac catheterization with PCI ___  History of Present Illness: HISTORY OF THE PRESENTING ILLNESS: ___ with complicated past medical history who presents with about 1 month of weakness and hypotension. Mr. ___ primary complaint is ___ weeks of generalized weakness and hypotension. His metoprolol was recently stopped due to BPs below his baseline (prior baseline ON antihypertensives was 140, now 90-100 off BP meds). He gets lightheaded when SBP 90. His HD team was unable to finish HD today b/c of hypotension down to systolics in the ___ and weakness (he was fully dialyzed but with minimal volume removal, currently 101kg post-HD with dry weight of 100kg). His wife brought him to the ED due to this persistent hypotension. This comes in the contxt of a very complicated recent course: He had a recent admission ___ for fever thought to be due to bacteremia and PNA (though source never confirmed) during which he had an NSTEMI that was medically managed. On that admission stress showed inferior mild reversible perfusion defect at the apex, moderate fixed inferior wall perfusion defect. An echocardiogram showed asymmetric septal hypertrophy with mild global LV systolic dysfunction with an estimated EF of 45-50%. He saw his cardiologist (___) in follow up ___ with complaint of hypotension and weakness. To r/o cardiac cause he had an ECHO which showed newly depressed EF: Overall left ventricular systolic function is moderately to severely depressed (LVEF= ___ %) with relative preservation of the lateral wall and profound hypokinesis of the septum. Left ventricular dysnchrony is present. A left ventricular mass/thrombus cannot be excluded. Also had a Holter monitor without significant arrhythmia noted. Given LV thrombus, he was scheduled for cardiac MRI today but missed the apt due to the events at HD as above. He also has a chronic R lateral malleolus wound being treated by his podiatrist Dr. ___. This was evaluated by podiatry inpatient at ___ during ___ admission and it was felt that it was not infected. Per wife this has grown in size and also has begun to express foul smelling odor. She says the outpt podiatrist reported that the wound was very close to the bone when he debrided it. Superficial culture has been prelim concerning for staph but no final speciation reported. He started HD dosing of vanco with 2nd dose today). In the ED, initial vitals were: 96.5 80 105/63 14 100% 3L NC. Labs notable for Cr 5.1 (at baseline), hgb 13 (at baseline), no leukocytosis. Trop 0.63 with normal ck/mb. CXR neg. Ankle x ray showed Soft tissue ulceration inferior to the right lateral malleolus without radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. EKG showed SR @ 81, NA, PR 204ms/QTc 491, TWI I/aVL and 1mm STE V1/V2 CWP. He received: IV cefepime. On the floor, he denies any current or recent chest pain, sob, orthopnea beyond baseline orthopnea. He's had no fevers. He has no pain at R lateral malleolus (he has neuropathy to the knee).  Past Medical History: ESRD (on HD since ___, AVF in forearm, ___ CAD status-post MI in ___ status-post PTCAx2 at ___, STEMI s/p ___ ___ He is followed by Dr. ___. Type II Diabetes (poorly controlled, complicated by nephropathy and retinopathy and neuropathy, FSBG 180-240 usually) Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure EF>55% in ___ Restrictive Lung Disease (after heart attack, sees Dr. ___ ___ on 3L Home O2 at rest/sitting or 4L if exerting himself Obstructive Sleep Apnea on BiPAP ___ @ 6L) Morbid Obesity Neurogenic Bladder with recurrent UTIs and Suprapubic Catheter since ___ (since ___ gave ibuprofen, Kidneys went down and had fluid overload and never regained muscle tone) Hypertension Osteomyelitis status-post left ___ metatarsal amputation Recurrent Clostridium difficile infections Glaucoma Cataract Surgery (bilateral, no glasses, left eye is good) Anxiety GERD  Social History: ___ Family History: Unknown biological family history and as patient is adopted and has no siblings. Knows that mother was ___ ___, father was ___, and he was a product of rape  Physical Exam: ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM: ============================= Vital Signs: 97.5 ___ 18 95 4L (at baseline) General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress HEENT: Sclerae anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear, EOMI, PERRL, neck supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally but diminished air entry, no wheezes, rales, rhonchi Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding GU: No foley Ext: Pitting edema to the knee, chronic venous stasis changes overlying. Ulcer with weeping purulent discharge, about 4 cm by 3 cm, 0.4 cm deep in the deepest part, with no exposed bone, overall lateral right ankle. Some necrotic skin in areas surrounding. Neuro: CNII-XII grossly intact, moving all extremities. DISCHARGE PHYSICAL EXAM: ============================== Vital Signs: 97.4 ___ ___ 92% 3L Weight 104.1kg -> 101.6kg -> 100.7kg -> 106.9kg (at HD) Pt makes minimal urine. General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress, resting comfortably in bed at dialysis HEENT: Sclerae anicteric, MMM, poor dentition, neck supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD. CV: RRR, no m/r/g Lungs: mildly diminished air entry diffusely, fine rales at bilateral bases. Abdomen: non-distended, bowel sounds present; soft and non-tender with no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding. GU: Suprapubic catheter in place, no surrounding erythema. UEs: R forearm fistula with successful dialysis access LEs: Right foot in boot. Trace pitting edema bilaterally. Warm extremities, bilaterally. MSE: A&Ox3, mood good, affect euthymic and stable, normal range. CN: Pupils 3mm and minimally reactive b/l, EOMI, CNII-XII otherwise intact. Reflexes: symmetric ___ in upper and lower extremities. Motor: Normal muscle bulk and tone. No adventitious movements, no asterixis, no pronator drift. ___ strength biceps, ___ triceps, wrist flexion/extension, hip flexors b/l. Sensation to LT intact to just below knee b/l, absent distally; intact to LT in b/l UEs. Unable to plantar or dorsiflex bilateral feet at baseline. Coordination: intact to FNF b/l. Gait: deferred, wheelchair bound  Pertinent Results: ADMISSION LABS: =================== ___ 04:50PM PLT COUNT-141* ___ 04:50PM NEUTS-74.8* LYMPHS-14.9* MONOS-7.6 EOS-2.1 BASOS-0.3 IM ___ AbsNeut-6.42* AbsLymp-1.28 AbsMono-0.65 AbsEos-0.18 AbsBaso-0.03 ___ 04:50PM WBC-8.6 RBC-4.00* HGB-13.4* HCT-39.8* MCV-100* MCH-33.5* MCHC-33.7 RDW-14.6 RDWSD-52.3* ___ 04:50PM ALBUMIN-3.5 CALCIUM-9.4 PHOSPHATE-3.5 MAGNESIUM-2.1 ___ 04:50PM CK-MB-3 ___ 04:50PM cTropnT-0.62* ___ 04:50PM CK(CPK)-81 ___ 04:50PM estGFR-Using this ___ 04:50PM GLUCOSE-174* UREA N-33* CREAT-5.1* SODIUM-134 POTASSIUM-5.0 CHLORIDE-96 TOTAL CO2-28 ANION GAP-15 ___ 05:01PM LACTATE-1.2 K+-4.7 PERTINENT LABS: =================== ___ 04:50PM BLOOD CK-MB-3 ___ 04:50PM BLOOD cTropnT-0.62* ___ 12:04AM BLOOD CK-MB-3 cTropnT-0.59* ___ 06:17AM BLOOD CK-MB-2 cTropnT-0.65* ___ 06:17AM BLOOD Cortsol-29.2* ___ 12:04AM BLOOD CRP-40.7* ___ 06:22AM BLOOD Vanco-8.2* IMAGING/STUDIES: ==================== ___ FOCUSED TTE FOCUSED STUDY- Overall left ventricular systolic function is moderately to severely depressed (LVEF <30 %) with dyskinesis of the apex and distal septum, akinesis of the basal to mid septum, and hypokinesis of all other segments. The basal lateral wall is relatively preserved in contractility. The right ventricle is at least mildly dilated with mild to moderate global hypokinesis. No masses or thrombi are seen in the left ventricle. ___ X RAY ANKLE Soft tissue ulceration inferior to the right lateral malleolus without radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. Please note that MRI is a more sensitive examination for the detection of osteomyelitis. ___ MR ___ contrast: IMPRESSION: 1. No MR evidence of osteomyelitis. 2. There is a small amount of non-specific edema in the calcaneus just inferior to a chronic fracture in the posterior superior calcaneus. 2. Diffuse edema in the plantar muscles may be related to denervation or myositis. ___ Cath: Coronary Anatomy Dominance: Right * Left Main Coronary Artery The LMCA is calcified without significant stenosis. * Left Anterior Descending There is a 99% stenosis in the Proximal LAD. The lesion has a TIMI flow of 2 and has severe calcification noted. This lesion is further described as tubular. An intervention was performed on the Proximal LAD with a final stenosis of 0%. There were no lesion complications. * Circumflex The Circumflex hjas poroximal calcification and mild eccentric stenosis. Beyond OM1 there is 70% stenosis. * Right Coronary Artery The RCA is totally occluded at site of prior ___ thrombosis. Interventional Details A 6 ___ XB LAD 4 provided good support. A Prowater wire was first placed in circumflex for protection and the LAD lesion was then crossed with a second Prowater wire. The lesion was predilated with 2.5 x 15 balloon at 12 ATM. A 3.0 x 16 Promus DES was deployed at 16 ATM with proximal edge just inside the left main and post dilated with 3.5 x 12 balloon at ___ ATM. Final angiography showed no residual, TIMI 3 flow and no dissection. There was no compromise of the left circumflex. Intra-procedural Complications: None Impressions: 1. Severe 3 vessel CAD with culprit ostial LAD lesion. 2. Successful ___. Recommendations 1. Continue aspirin 81 mg daily; Plavix 75 mg daily. Type II Diabetes (poorly controlled, complicated by nephropathy and retinopathy and neuropathy, FSBG 180-240 usually) Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure EF>55% in ___ Restrictive Lung Disease (after heart attack, sees Dr. ___ ___ on 3L Home O2 at rest/sitting or 4L if exerting himself Obstructive Sleep Apnea on BiPAP ___ @ 6L) Morbid Obesity Neurogenic Bladder with recurrent UTIs and Suprapubic Catheter since ___ (since ___ gave ibuprofen, Kidneys went down and had fluid overload and never regained muscle tone) Hypertension Osteomyelitis status-post left ___ metatarsal amputation Recurrent Clostridium difficile infections Glaucoma Cataract Surgery (bilateral, no glasses, left eye is good) Anxiety GERD  Social History: ___ Family History: Unknown biological family history and as patient is adopted and has no siblings. fields.诊疗经过: ___ man with complicated past medical history including CAD s/p multiple MI and 2x stents, CHF, ESRD on HD, T2DM with chronic foot ulcer and prior osteomyelitis, prior bacteremia with MDR organisms, p/w one month of weakness and hypotension of unclear etiology, most likely cardiac in origin or sympathetic dysregulation. ACUTE ISSUES: #Hypotension: Presented with lower blood pressures over past month requiring stopping his home metoprolol and limiting ability to take off fluid at HD (SBPs as low as ___ at HD). ECHO prior to admission showed newly depressed EF (<30%). AM cortisol was 29 ruling out adrenal insufficiency. Sepsis thought unlikely given chronicity of hypotension, negative blood cultures, and ultimately MRI without evidence of osteo. Hypovolemic etiology unlikely as pt actually above his dry weight due to difficulty removing fluid at HD recently. No e/o bleed. Given hypotension and concern for ischemic cardiomyopathy as etiology, he underwent cath, further details below. He was also started on Midodrine 5mg tid on dialysis days which he tolerated well. Patient's SBPs at discharge were: 110s. #CAD: Patient with echo with decreased EF (45-50% in ___, now 30%), which was concerning for missed MI. ___ stress test showed reversible ischemia. In setting of ESRD, troponins were difficult to interpret. Patient had Cardiac Catheterization on ___ which found severe 3 vessel CAD with culprit ostial LAD lesion and so DES to LAD. Patient was transferred to the Cardiology service for further management of high risk PCI. Increased Atorva to 80, was on 40 for unclear reason. Patient's Metoprolol was held given hypotension. Continued ASA 81mg, Plavix. #Chronic CHFrEF (EF 30%): As above, newly depressed EF on recent TTE. He did not have clinical evidence of an acute exacerbation this admission. As above, all antihypertensives were held. Likely not ICD candidate given history of chronic foot ulcer and also chronic suprapubic catheter. Volume diuresis with HD ___, Th, ___.  #Non healing L malleolus ulcer: Initially concerning for ostemomyelitis per his outpatient nephrologist Dr. ___. MRI showed no evidence of osteomyelitis. Given recent increase in purulence/drainage of ulcer, he was started on 2 week course with vanco/ceftaz dosed at HD for skin/soft tissue infection. However, Ceftaz was stopped given low suspicion for infection. Vancomycin was continued per request of Podiatry team. #LV Thrombus: Initial concern for LV thrombus on outpatient ECHO. This was ruled out on repeat TTE with IV Lumason dye. Patient was briefly on a heprarin drip which was discontinued when the ECHO was performed. CHRONIC ISSUES: #CKD: Continued on HD ___. Continued sevelemer, nephrocaps, cinacalcet. #AV fistula: His fistula has had problems with low flows and poor clearances and recently had a fistulagram and angioplasty of a stenosis above its aneurysms. Fistula was usable this admission. #DM: Continued NPH dosing at 40 U BID, ISS, gabapentin. #Neurogenic Bladder: Has chronic suprapubic catheter in place and often has asymptomatic bacturia. He had MDR history and is well-known to the ID service, who has recommended that pt's bacteuria NOT be treated as it is chronic for the patient and simply leads to antibiotic resistance. Urine was not cultured this admission.

Cinacalcet 30 mg PO DAILY 4. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 5. Docusate Sodium 300 mg PO DAILY 6. Gabapentin 300 mg PO HS 7. Metoclopramide 5 mg PO BID 8. Nephrocaps 1 CAP PO HS 9. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H 10. sevelamer CARBONATE 2400 mg PO TID W/MEALS 11. Midodrine 5 mg PO TID Take on dialysis days. Take on NON-dialysis days only if SBP < 90 or if symptomatic RX *midodrine 5 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth Three times per day Disp #*90 Tablet Refills:*0 12. ___ ___ unit/gram TOPICAL DAILY 13. Vancomycin 1250 mg IV QHD Last day of vancomycin at HD: ___ 14. NPH 40 Units Breakfast NPH 40 Units Dinner Insulin SC Sliding Scale using HUM Insulin   Discharge Instructions: Dear Mr. ___: You were admitted because of low blood pressures and generally feeling weak. You had a cardiac cath which showed a narrowing in one of the major blood vessels that brings blood to your heart (the LAD) so a drug-eluting ___ (DES) was placed. You had an MRI of your foot which did NOT show any signs of infection of the bone, so you will undergo a 2 week course of two antibiotics for infection of the skin (___) with vancomycin at dialysis. You were started on a medication called midodrine to keep your blood pressures up, which should be taken three times per day on the days you get dialysis. If your blood pressures are less than 90 on the days you do NOT get dialysis, or if you are symptomatic (dizzy, weak, lightheaded) on those days, you should take the midodrine three times per day on those days as well. Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more than 3 lbs. It was a pleasure to care for you. Your ___ Team

Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain. ? adrenal insufficiency  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Push-Enteroscopy Small Bowel Follow Through  History of Present Illness: ___ year old woman with h/o celiac disease, pernicious anemia, Ulcerative Colitis, addison's, polyglandular syndrome?, s/p ___ fundiplication who p/w abdominal pain with nausea, vomitting, diarrhea. The patient has a very extensive workup for persistent nausea, abd pains - located midepigastric region and RLQ area, and watery diarrhea. She has been admitted 4 times in the past six months. By reports, she initially presented in ___ with diarrhea, abd pain. Colonoscopy and EGD at that time showed mild diverticulosis, bx from antrum revealed mild chronic gastritis, SB bx nl. She was somehow diagnosed with celiac disease (report of dermatitis hermatiformis in the records) and placed on gluten-free diet. Despite the gluten free diet, she still had 5x/day of diarrhea, persisent abd pain. Workup for the diarrhea included VIP, calcitonin, SPEP, somatostatin, 24 hr HIAA, metanephrine,normetahnephrine, and antihistaminsgastrin - all normal. Stool cxs were negative. Only abnormality was an elevated gastrin(241 with upper normal at 100).  In ___, she was diagnosed with Addison's disease and was admitted to OSH due to n/v, abd pain and inability to tolerate PO hydrocortisoneon. Apparently cortisol stim test showed levels of 3.04, 10.58 (30 mins), and 13.3 (60 mins). She was admitted from ___ and given iv hydrocortisone. Bilirub-NEG Urobiln-NEG pH-6.0 Leuks-NEG ___ 7:43 am URINE CLEAN CATCH URINE . **FINAL REPORT ___ URINE CULTURE (Final ___: MIXED BACTERIAL FLORA ( >= 3 COLONY TYPES), CONSISTENT WITH SKIN AND/OR GENITAL CONTAMINATION. ABDOMEN (SUPINE & ERECT) Study Date of ___ 11:16 ___ IMPRESSION: No evidence of obstruction or ileus. Push Enteroscopy ___ Findings: Esophagus: Normal esophagus. Stomach: Mucosa: Diffuse erythema of the stomach consistent with gastritis Cold forceps biopsies were performed for histology at the stomach antrum and stomach body. Other pH of stomach fluid was 3.0. Duodenum: Mucosa: Normal mucosa was noted in the duodenum and jejunum. No ulcerations were noted. Cold forceps biopsies were performed for histology at the jejunum and second part of the duodenum. Impression: Abnormal mucosa in the stomach (biopsy) Normal mucosa in the duodenum (biopsy) PH of stomach fluid was 3.0. Otherwise normal EGD to jejunum Small Bowel Follow-Through pending at time of discharge  Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman h/o pernicious anemia (see below), celiac disease, ?ulcerative colitis, addison's disease, endometriosis with extensive surgery history (s/p Nissen fundiplication, s/p cecal volvulus repair, lysis adhesions, TAH, biliary sphincterotomy) admitted with abd pain, N/V/D. Past workup significant for elevated gastrin level, ___ stim test, reduced Vit B12. 1. Abdomen pain - cause unclear. EGD, colonoscopy, x-lap, abd CT, MRI, LFT's unremarkable. Although pain certainly possible with Addison's, the persistent pain after steroid replacement is rather unusual. Other possibilities: endometriosis, AIP, dyspepsia, IBS, abdominal migraine, gastrinoma (elevated gastrin levels likely due to PPI), postsurgical related pain, vasculitis (unlikely). Nothing in the reports suggests that she has UC. - GI consult was obtained with Dr. ___. He recommended a push-enteroscopy which was signigiant only for mild gastritis. A small bowel follow through was also performed and is pending at time of discharge. - During enteroscopy the pH of the stomach was measured at 3.0 which rules out pernicous anemia. - stopped carafate, start low dose PPI (10mg omeprazole) - very low ESR, CRP - may also consider secretin stimulation test (although gastrinoma unlikely) which is only performed at Dr. ___ ___ practice, and she will follow up with him at the appointment below. - morphine, antiemetics - cont with prednisone, although the timing of her symptoms appear to actually be related to the onset of the steroids rather than the abscense of them. 2. Diarrhea - stool cultures, secretory/infectious causes workup largely negative - imodium PRN - DC magnesium - possibly related to nexium, which was reduced 3. Addison's - no evidence of polyglandular syndrome - TSH wnl and no evidence of diabetes (occasional elevated glucose). - Hgb A1c was normal - cannot check ACTH/cortisol while on prednisone - checked renin level (for mineralocorticoid deficiency) which is pending at time of discharge - may benefit from florinef at some point - Given the onset of her symptoms is always ___ hours after taking a prednisone tablet, and I observed it 45 minutes after administering a piggyback of hydrocortisone, particularly with shaking this may be the onset of the steroids rather than adrenal crisis. Complicating this is that she gets very tense, which if she had carotid body hypersensitivity, this would cause reactive hypotension. I am recommended that she taper down the dose, and as she has seen undigested pills in her feces, that she take liquid prednisone which may work better in her high-pH environment. This is a 6 week slow taper.  Medications on Admission: Prednisone 10 QAM, 5 Q2PM Carafate 1 gm TID Nexium 40 mg QD Wellbutrin XL 150 QD Mg 500 QD MVI, B-complex, caltrate Vicodin 7.5 mg QD PRN Nasonex, Astelin 2 puffs QD Lunesta 3 mg QD Zofran 4 mg PRN Albuterol 2 puff Q6 PRN Vit B12  Discharge Medications: 1. Prednisone Taper Prednison Suspension 5mg/5ml Dispense: ***1 bottle*** Refills: ***3*** Take 5mg PO Twice Daily for 2 weeks. Then 2.5mg PO Twice Daily for 2 weeks, then 2.5mg Once Daily for 2 weeks 2. Omeprazole 10 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO once a day. Disp:*30 Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.)(s)* Refills:*2* 3. Bupropion HCl 150 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release PO QAM (once a day (in the morning)). 4. Loperamide 2 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO QID (4 times a day) as needed for diarrhea. 5. Phenergan 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every six (6) hours as needed for nausea. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 6. Morphine 15 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every six (6) hours as needed for pain. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Abdominal Pain Adrenal Insufficiency Ulcerative Colitis Secondary Diabetes  Discharge Condition: Good  Discharge Instructions: Please return to the hospital with fever/chills, inability to eat, low blood sugar, low blood pressure. For further diagnosis it is important to get more information about your daily activities. You will need to keep a log of your day. This is actually quite difficult: Please log your blood sugar and blood pressure 4 times/day (on awakening, before lunch, before dinner and before bedtime). Please also log EXACTLY what you eat (for instance, steak with butter and garlic rather than just steak). Then if you experience one of the episodes, please record your pulse, blood sugar and blood pressure. Also please log exactly what situation you were in when the episode occured. Please bring this log to your doctors, including the visit with Dr. ___ you return. You should discuss with your endocrinologist about adding Fludricortisone (Florinef) to your steroid regimen. You should confirm with your endocrinologist about the medrol dosing, as this would be a 10x increase in your steroid dosing. We are discharging you on a steroid taper as the shaking appeared here in the hospital with administration with steroids. You are being discharged on the painkiller morphine. This medication can make you drowsy and confused. Do not operate any machinery, drive or put yourself in situations where having impaired thinking is dangerous. It can also be constipating. If you find yourself constipated, you can try over the counter Mirilax; if this is unsuccessful, contact your doctor for assistance as this can become serious. As you are on high dose steroid, watch for unusual infections such as white plaques in your mouth, difficulty breathing, or fevers.  Followup Instructions: ___

Otherwise normal EGD to jejunum Small Bowel Follow-Through pending at time of discharge  Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman h/o pernicious anemia (see below), celiac disease, ?ulcerative colitis, addison's disease, endometriosis with extensive surgery history (s/p Nissen fundiplication, s/p cecal volvulus repair, lysis adhesions, TAH, biliary sphincterotomy) admitted with abd pain, N/V/D. Past workup significant for elevated gastrin level, ___ stim test, reduced Vit B12. 1. Abdomen pain - cause unclear. EGD, colonoscopy, x-lap, abd CT, MRI, LFT's unremarkable. Although pain certainly possible with Addison's, the persistent pain after steroid replacement is rather unusual. Other possibilities: endometriosis, AIP, dyspepsia, IBS, abdominal migraine, gastrinoma (elevated gastrin levels likely due to PPI), postsurgical related pain, vasculitis (unlikely). Nothing in the reports suggests that she has UC. - GI consult was obtained with Dr. ___. He recommended a push-enteroscopy which was signigiant only for mild gastritis. A small bowel follow through was also performed and is pending at time of discharge. - During enteroscopy the pH of the stomach was measured at 3.0 which rules out pernicous anemia. - stopped carafate, start low dose PPI (10mg omeprazole) - very low ESR, CRP - may also consider secretin stimulation test (although gastrinoma unlikely) which is only performed at Dr. ___ ___ practice, and she will follow up with him at the appointment below. - morphine, antiemetics - cont with prednisone, although the timing of her symptoms appear to actually be related to the onset of the steroids rather than the abscense of them. 2. Diarrhea - stool cultures, secretory/infectious causes workup largely negative - imodium PRN - DC magnesium - possibly related to nexium, which was reduced 3. Addison's - no evidence of polyglandular syndrome - TSH wnl and no evidence of diabetes (occasional elevated glucose). - Hgb A1c was normal - cannot check ACTH/cortisol while on prednisone - checked renin level (for mineralocorticoid deficiency) which is pending at time of discharge - may benefit from florinef at some point - Given the onset of her symptoms is always ___ hours after taking a prednisone tablet, and I observed it 45 minutes after administering a piggyback of hydrocortisone, particularly with shaking this may be the onset of the steroids rather than adrenal crisis. Complicating this is that she gets very tense, which if she had carotid body hypersensitivity, this would cause reactive hypotension. I am recommended that she taper down the dose, and as she has seen undigested pills in her feces, that she take liquid prednisone which may work better in her high-pH environment. This is a 6 week slow taper.  Medications on Admission: Prednisone 10 QAM, 5 Q2PM Carafate 1 gm TID Nexium 40 mg QD Wellbutrin XL 150 QD Mg 500 QD MVI, B-complex, caltrate Vicodin 7.5 mg QD PRN Nasonex, Astelin 2 puffs QD Lunesta 3 mg QD Zofran 4 mg PRN Albuterol 2 puff Q6 PRN Vit B12  Discharge Medications: 1. Prednisone Taper Prednison Suspension 5mg/5ml Dispense: ***1 bottle*** Refills: ***3*** Take 5mg PO Twice Daily for 2 weeks. Then 2.5mg PO Twice Daily for 2 weeks, then 2.5mg Once Daily for 2 weeks 2. Omeprazole 10 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO once a day. Disp:*30 Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.)(s)* Refills:*2* 3. Bupropion HCl 150 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release PO QAM (once a day (in the morning)). 4. Loperamide 2 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO QID (4 times a day) as needed for diarrhea. 5. Phenergan 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every six (6) hours as needed for nausea. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 6. Morphine 15 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every six (6) hours as needed for pain. Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Abdominal Pain Adrenal Insufficiency Ulcerative Colitis Secondary Diabetes  Discharge Condition: Good  Discharge Instructions: Please return to the hospital with fever/chills, inability to eat, low blood sugar, low blood pressure. For further diagnosis it is important to get more information about your daily activities. You will need to keep a log of your day. This is actually quite difficult: Please log your blood sugar and blood pressure 4 times/day (on awakening, before lunch, before dinner and before bedtime). Please also log EXACTLY what you eat (for instance, steak with butter and garlic rather than just steak). Then if you experience one of the episodes, please record your pulse, blood sugar and blood pressure. Also please log exactly what situation you were in when the episode occured. Please bring this log to your doctors, including the visit with Dr. ___ you return. You should discuss with your endocrinologist about adding Fludricortisone (Florinef) to your steroid regimen. You should confirm with your endocrinologist about the medrol dosing, as this would be a 10x increase in your steroid dosing. We are discharging you on a steroid taper as the shaking appeared here in the hospital with administration with steroids. You are being discharged on the painkiller morphine. This medication can make you drowsy and confused. Do not operate any machinery, drive or put yourself in situations where having impaired thinking is dangerous. It can also be constipating. If you find yourself constipated, you can try over the counter Mirilax; if this is unsuccessful, contact your doctor for assistance as this can become serious. As you are on high dose steroid, watch for unusual infections such as white plaques in your mouth, difficulty breathing, or fevers.  Followup Instructions: ___
