fields.入院情况: History of Present Illness: ___ year old woman with h/o celiac disease, pernicious anemia, Ulcerative Colitis, addison's, polyglandular syndrome?, s/p ___ fundiplication who p/w abdominal pain with nausea, vomitting, diarrhea. The patient has a very extensive workup for persistent nausea, abd pains - located midepigastric region and RLQ area, and watery diarrhea. She has been admitted 4 times in the past six months. By reports, she initially presented in ___ with diarrhea, abd pain. Colonoscopy and EGD at that time showed mild diverticulosis, bx from antrum revealed mild chronic gastritis, SB bx nl. She was somehow diagnosed with celiac disease (report of dermatitis hermatiformis in the records) and placed on gluten-free diet. Despite the gluten free diet, she still had 5x/day of diarrhea, persisent abd pain. Workup for the diarrhea included VIP, calcitonin, SPEP, somatostatin, 24 hr HIAA, metanephrine,normetahnephrine, and antihistaminsgastrin - all normal. Stool cxs were negative. Only abnormality was an elevated gastrin(241 with upper normal at 100).  In ___, she was diagnosed with Addison's disease and was admitted to OSH due to n/v, abd pain and inability to tolerate PO hydrocortisoneon. Apparently cortisol stim test showed levels of 3.04, 10.58 (30 mins), and 13.3 (60 mins). She was admitted from ___ and given iv hydrocortisone. She obtained a abd CT which showed pneumobilia, but no significant ___. A MRI abdomen showed no pancreatic tumor. Adrenal glands and kidneys unremarkable. TSH, ESR, CRP nl. EGD - showed hemorrhagic gastritis and Colonoscopy with no ___. She still had persistent pain and was admitted on ___ for an xlap. The report suggested that it was remarkable for lack of adhesions. They identified small areas of endometriosis (small R lateral wall) which was cauterized. A few adhesions were lysed. She was discharged on ___ and claimed that there was some improvement. She was again admitted for same complaints of abd pain, N/V/D on ___. And CT on ___ showed no pathology and pelvic U/S showed no torsion, s/p R oophorectomy. And discharged on ___. On ___ - passed out and was seen in the ER. She went to see her endocrinologist today who recommended that she make the trip down to ___ for further workup. Her husband also notes that she was given (he thinks) 16mg of Medrol to start (112mg Prednisone Equivelent) but isn't positive that, that is the dose. She has not started that on top of her prednisone yet.  In the ED: VS: 98.4 72 128/86 22 98% on RA. She was given morphine, zofran, and phenergan. ROS: LGTemp of 99 degress. Denies chills. + Fatigue, weakness, lightheadedness. ROS otherwise negative  Past Medical History: Celiac disease - ? dermatitis herpetaformis Addison's disease Endometriosis Pernicious anemia Ulcerative colitis Migraines Polyglandular disease? s/p appy, choly, s/p sphincterotomy sludge CBD 07, s/p NISSEN fundoplication, csection, TAH R oophorectomy, cystocele repair for endometriosis  Social History: ___ Family History: FH: F: HTN, DM2 M: Thyroid disease S: Lupus, thyroid disease  Physical Exam: VS: 98.2, 106/72, 64, 16, 93% Gen: NAD Eye: extra-occular movements intact, pupils equal round, reactive to light, sclera anicteric, not injected, no exudates ENT: dry mucus membranes, no ulcerations or exudates Neck: no thyromegally, JVD: Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm, normal s1, s2, no murmurs, rubs or gallops Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales or rhonchi Abd: Soft, tender midepigastric and RLQ quadrant, no heptosplenomegally, bowel sounds present, guaiac negative, no rebound, + partial guarding, +BS Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, edema, joint swelling Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, CN II-XII intact, normal attention, sensation normal, asterixis absent, speech fluent, DTR's 2+ patellar, achilles, biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally, babinski down-going bilaterally Integument: Warm, moist, no rash or ulceration Psychiatric: appropriate, sleepy, not anxious Hematologic: no cervical or supraclavicular LAD

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: repair of recurrent incisional hernia  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Laparoscopic recurrent incisional hernia repair, laparoscopic lysis of adhesions in (extensive) and small bowel resection with anastomosis.  History of Present Illness: This patient had originally undergone a low anterior resection for rectal cancer followed by radiation. Presented several years ago with the bowel obstruction and an incisional hernia and underwent surgery for that. He presented on this occasion with a recurrent incisional hernia as well as an episode of bowel obstruction. That resolved and he was scheduled for elective surgery.  Past Medical History: Rectal cancer diagnosed in ___ s/p chemotherapy and surgical excision. PSH: low anterior resection ___  Social History: ___ Family History: No history of rectal cancer in the family.  Physical Exam: At Discharge: Vitals: 98.6, 63, 113/74, 20, 95% RA GEN: NAD, A/Ox3 CV: RRR, no m/r/g RESP: CTAB, no w/r/r ABD: soft, ND, appropriately TTP, +BS, +flatus Incision: Multiple small laparoscopic abdomal incisions. OTA with staples, CDI. Abdominal binder in place. Extrem: no c/c/e  Pertinent Results: ___ 07:05AM BLOOD WBC-8.4 RBC-4.82 Hgb-15.4 Hct-46.2 MCV-96 MCH-32.0 MCHC-33.4 RDW-13.1 Plt ___ ___ 07:05AM BLOOD Glucose-118* UreaN-18 Creat-0.9 Na-138 K-4.7 Cl-102 HCO3-28 AnGap-13 . Pathology Examination Procedure date ___ DIAGNOSIS: Small bowel, segmental resection 1. Segment of small intestine with multifocal submucosal, subserosal and mesenteric fibrosis with focal granulation tissue formation; see note 2. Rare focus of mucosal regeneration and vascular congestion; no intrinsic mucosal abnormalities otherwise recognized. 3. Viable resection margins. Note: The findings are mild and non-specific, but can be seen with chronic incarceration in association with herniation. Clinical: Incisional hernia.  Brief Hospital Course: Patient's operative course was uncomplicated. Routinely observed in PACU. Admitted to ___ for post-op care. Pain controlled with PCA. Abdominal incision intact with staples, CDI. POD1, patient reportedly bending over to pick up cell phone that had fallen to floor; there was a gush of bright red blood from distal incision. Bleeding stopped once patient returned to a seated position. No more bleeding noted with non-abdominal straining activity. Vitals stable. Patient asymptomatic. Patient counseled on proper body mechanics to minimize tension on abdomen. . Diet advanced gradually from sips to regular food as bowel function and abdominal distention improved. Reported flatus, and eventual loose stools. IV fluid discontinued. Foley removed. Voided without issue. Medications switched to oral. Pain well controlled with oral Dilaudid. Activity returned to baseline. Ambulated in halls independently. . Advised to call for a follow-up appointment with Dr. ___ in ___ weeks for staple removal.  Medications on Admission: Cabergoline 0.5 twice weekly, Toprol XL 50, Solifenacin 10, Testosterone 1% gel, ASA 325, MVI, Benefiber  Discharge Medications: 1. Solifenacin 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. 2. Metoprolol Succinate 50 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release 24 hr PO DAILY (Daily). 3. Cabergoline 0.5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice weekly. 4. Testosterone 1 %(50 mg/5 gram) Gel in Packet Sig: One (1) Transdermal once a day. 5. Multivitamin Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO once a day. 6. Aspirin 325 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. 7. Benefiber (Guar Gum) Packet Sig: One (1) PO once a day as needed for constipation. 8. Hydromorphone 2 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO every four (4) hours as needed for pain for 2 weeks. Disp:*45 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0* 9. Acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for pain/HA for 2 weeks: Do not exceed 4000mg in 24hrs . 10. Colace 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO twice a day as needed for constipation for 2 weeks: Take with Hydromorphone. Disp:*30 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Recurrent ventral hernia  Discharge Condition: Stable Tolerating a regular low residue diet Adequate pain control with oral medication  Discharge Instructions: Please call your doctor or return to the ER for any of the following: * If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your medications. * You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. * You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a bowel movement. * Your pain is not improving within ___ hours or not gone within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or is changing location or moving to your chest or back. *Avoid lifting objects > 5lbs until your follow-up appointment with the surgeon. *DO NOT BEND OVER TO PICK UP OBJECTS. This will increase tension across your abdomen. KEEP BINDER ON AT ALL TIMES except for shower. *Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications. * You have shaking chills, or a fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38(C) degrees. * Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. * Please resume all regular home medications and take any new meds as ordered. * Continue to ambulate several times per day. . Incision Care: -Your staples will be removed at your follow-up appointment. Steri strips will be applied. -Your steri-strips will fall off on their own. Please remove any remaining strips ___ days after surgery. -You may shower, and wash surgical incisions. -Avoid swimming and baths until your follow-up appointment. -Please call the doctor if you have increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision sites.  Followup Instructions: ___

Acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for pain/HA for 2 weeks: Do not exceed 4000mg in 24hrs . 10. Colace 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO twice a day as needed for constipation for 2 weeks: Take with Hydromorphone. Disp:*30 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*  Discharge Instructions: Please call your doctor or return to the ER for any of the following: * If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your medications. * You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid heartbeat or feeling dizzy or faint when standing. * You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a bowel movement. * Your pain is not improving within ___ hours or not gone within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is getting worse or is changing location or moving to your chest or back. *Avoid lifting objects > 5lbs until your follow-up appointment with the surgeon. *DO NOT BEND OVER TO PICK UP OBJECTS. This will increase tension across your abdomen. KEEP BINDER ON AT ALL TIMES except for shower. *Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications. * You have shaking chills, or a fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38(C) degrees. * Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern you. * Please resume all regular home medications and take any new meds as ordered. * Continue to ambulate several times per day. . Incision Care: -Your staples will be removed at your follow-up appointment. Steri strips will be applied. -Your steri-strips will fall off on their own. Please remove any remaining strips ___ days after surgery. -You may shower, and wash surgical incisions. -Avoid swimming and baths until your follow-up appointment. -Please call the doctor if you have increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision sites

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Estrogens,Conjugated / Claritin / Nsaids / Latex / Codeine / Dilaudid / Dilaudid / Dilaudid  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___: Laparoscopic converted to open, adhesiolysis and small-bowel resection.  History of Present Illness: ___ presents w/ 5 days of abdominal pain. The pain was diffuse throughout the abdomen and described as sharp and constant, then worsened more on the L side of abdomen. There was associated nausea and dry heaves. She also noted abdominal distension and she started to vomit yesterday, small bilious amounts. She continued to have emesis this AM and multiple times in the ED. Her last bowel movement was yesterday, after she gave herself an enema. Prior to that, she had a bowel movement on ___. She has continued to pass flatus up to today. In the ED, an NGT was placed, which initially put out 900cc of bilious fluid. KUB showed obstruction. She denies fevers at home.  Past Medical History: Past Medical History: Paget's disease, anemia, allergic rhinitis, borderline hypertension, DVT ___, hypercholesterolemia, hypothyroidism, psychosis psychotic break, urinary frequency, uterine prolapse Past Surgical History: tubal ligation, R hip replacement ___, meniscal tear, breast surgery 1950s, nose surgery, vaginal hysterectomy ___  Social History: ___ Family History: non-contributory  Physical Exam: On admission: Vitals: 98 97 171/98 22 96% GEN: A&O, NAD HEENT: No scleral icterus, mucus membranes moist CV: RRR PULM: Clear to auscultation b/l, No W/R/R ABD: Soft, distended, tympanitic, tender to palpation on L side, +rebound in LLQ DRE: normal tone, no gross or occult blood Ext: No ___ edema, ___ warm and well perfused  Pertinent Results: CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST Study Date of ___ 5:48 ___ IMPRESSION: 1. Diffuse severe dilatation of proximal small bowel with a low transition point (602B, 30), consistent with high grade small bowel obstruction. Most likely etiology is adhesions related to prior hysterectomy. Small ascites. 2. A C-shaped small bowel loop in the low anterior abdomen with two converging transition points and interdigitating moderate fluid, suggests a closed loop component to obstruction. No bowel wall non-enhancement, pneumatosis, or perforation. 3. 10 cm segment of small caliber small bowel interposed between dilated bowel loops in the mid abdomen with mucosal hyperemia, may suggest congestion, even early ischemia. 4. Mild mesenteric swirling and rightward shift of the jejunum, significance uncertain, but raises question of potential internal hernia, although not diagnostic. 5. Large axial hiatal hernia without obstruction. 6. Subcentimeter hepatic segment VI lesions too small to fully characterize. 7. Left hemipelvic cortical thickening, sclerosis, and trabecular coarsening, consistent with Paget's disease.  ___ 03:40PM WBC-10.2 RBC-4.93 HGB-13.6 HCT-41.1 MCV-83 MCH-27.6 MCHC-33.1 RDW-13.8 ___ 03:40PM NEUTS-89.2* LYMPHS-6.5* MONOS-4.0 EOS-0.1 BASOS-0.1 ___ 03:40PM PLT COUNT-319 ___ 03:40PM GLUCOSE-140* UREA N-18 CREAT-0.8 SODIUM-135 POTASSIUM-4.1 CHLORIDE-98 TOTAL CO2-27 ANION GAP-14 ___ 05:45PM URINE COLOR-Yellow APPEAR-Clear SP ___ ___ 05:45PM URINE BLOOD-NEG NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-30 GLUCOSE-TR KETONE-TR BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-2* PH-6.0 LEUK-NEG ___ 05:45PM URINE RBC-0 WBC-<1 BACTERIA-NONE YEAST-NONE EPI-0 ___ 05:45PM URINE MUCOUS-OCC ___ 11:09PM LACTATE-1.3  Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted on ___ under the Acute Care Surgical service for management of her small bowel obstruction. She was taken urgently to the operating room and underwent laparoscopic converted to open, adhesiolysis and small-bowel resection (see operative note by Dr. ___ details). She tolerated the procedure well and was extubated upon completion and transferred to the PACU for recovery. She was subsequently transferred to the surgical floor. She was kept NPO with an NG tube for decompression and given IV fluids for hydration. On ___ the patient self d/c'd her NG tube. On ___ she had evidence of bowel function and was started on a clear liquid diet, which was slowly advanced as tolerated to a regular diet. A foley catheter was placed perioperatively for urine output monitoring. This was removed on ___, but was subsequently reinserted for urinary retention. She was noted to have hallucinations during her postoperative course as well as some tachycardia and hypertension. It was unclear whether the cause of this was narcotics, benzo withdrawal from her regular home medications (on clonazepam at home), or if it was related to her history of schizoaffective disorder. Psychiatry was consulted given her waxing and waning mental status with concerns about confusion with disorientation. Her home medications were restarted when tolerating PO's. Medical work up for causes of delirium was initiated. Her electrolytes were monitored and repleted as needed. Her WBC trended down to normal and her u/a and urine culture were negative. ECG showed NSR with no changes. She was started on metoprolol for hypertension and tachycardia. On ___ some erythema and drainage was noted from the inferior portion of her surgical incision and a couple staples were removed to allow drainage. Daily dressing changes were performed. She was encouraged to mobilize out of bed and ambulate as tolerated. Physical therapy was consulted and have recommended rehab after her acute hospital stay.  Medications on Admission: estrace 0.01% vaginal cream, percocet q6h prn, seroquel 450', clonazepam 1.5', fluticasone 50mcg nasal spray BID, ipratropium bromide 0.06% nasal spray each nostril BID, iron ER 325', pravastatin 20', zaleplon 5'  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H 2. Clonazepam 1.5 mg PO DAILY 3. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY NU BID 4. Pravastatin 20 mg PO DAILY 5. Quetiapine Fumarate 450 mg PO QHS 6. Bisacodyl 10 mg PR HS:PRN constipation 7. Estrogens Conjugated 1 gm VG 2X/WEEK (___) ___ & ___ 8. Heparin 5000 UNIT SC TID 9. Ipratropium Bromide MDI 2 PUFF IH QID 10. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg PO BID Hold for systolic BP <100 and/or HR <60. 11. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H 12. Senna 2 TAB PO HS:PRN constpation 13. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID  Discharge Disposition: Extended Care  Facility: ___  Discharge Diagnosis: Small bowel obstruction, closed loop and adhesive Delirium; reported history of schizoaffective disorder  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker or cane).  Discharge Instructions: You were admitted to the hospital with a small bowel obstruction. You were taken to the operating room and underwent an exploratory laparatomy with lysis of adhesions and a small bowel resection. You bowel function has returned after the surgery and you are now being discharged with the following instructions: Please follow up in ___ clinic at the appointment sdcheduled for you below. Your staples will be removed at this appointment. ACTIVITY:  Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and feel you could respond in an emergency. You may climb stairs. You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you see your surgeon at your next visit. Don't lift more than ___ lbs for ___ weeks. (This is about the weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to lifting children, but they may sit on your lap. You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable. You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you can resume tub baths or swimming. Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common sense and go slowly at first.  HOW YOU MAY FEEL:  You may feel weak or washed out for 6 weeks. You might want to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you. You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your throat during surgery. You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. You might feel somewhat depressed. You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem unappealing. All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.  YOUR INCISION: Your incision may be slightly red around the staples. This is normal. You may gently wash away dried material around your incision. It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This will go away. Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area. Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told otherwise. You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call your surgeon. You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may resume tub baths or swimming. Ove the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become less prominent.  YOUR BOWELS: Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these medicines without a prescription. If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving the bowels, call your surgeon.   PAIN MANAGEMENT: It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal surgery. This pain is often described as soreness. Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon. You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directied. Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more medicine at one time than prescribed. Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your pain gets too severe. Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your surgeon has said its okay. If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of pain medicine. Remember to use your cough pillow for splinting when you cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises. If you experience any of the folloiwng, please contact your surgeon: - sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours - pain that is getting worse over time - pain accompanied by fever of more than 101 - a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain  MEDICATIONS: Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as you did before, unless you have been told differently. In some cases you will have a prescription for antibiotics or other medication. If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to take, please call your surgeon.  DANGER SIGNS: Please call your surgeon if you develop: - worsening abdominal pain - sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours - temperature of 101 degrees or higher - severe diarrhea - vomiting - redness around the incision that is spreading - increased swelling around the incision - excessive bruising around the incision - cloudy fluid coming from the wound - bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the wound - an increase in drainage from the wound  Followup Instructions: ___  Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: altered mental status  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ intubation  History of Present Illness: HPI: Mr. ___ is an ___ y/o man with PMH notable for ESRD on HD and recent ___ s/p Burr hole sent to the ED for altered mental status. He was at his nursing home and found to have altered mental status at 8:30 am. At that time, T 96.5, P ___, BP 120/70, RR 40, 93% on NRB and 88% on NC (later reported 83% on RA). He was diaphoretic at the time FSBS 145. Per NH reports, he had dialysis on ___. He was sent to the ___ emergency room for further evaluation. There, he was noted to have a rectal temp 100.7, RR 30, P ___, and BP 112/90. His O2 sat was 98% on NRB. CXR showed left base atelectasis versus infiltrate; CT head demonstrated SDH and he was then transferred to ___ for further evaluation with neurosurgery. . In the ED, initial vitals BP 85/48, HR 110, RR 24, 100% on NRB. He was found to be hyperkalemic and received calcium gluconate, D50/insulin. Due to decreased mental status, he was intubated with 20 mg etomidate and 40 mg rocuronium. He was hypotensive to the ___ and started on levophed gtt. Right femoral CVL was placed for IV access. He was noted to have dark brown output (400 cc in total) from his NGT. He received 2 U FFP and vitamin K as well as profilin (activated factor 9). He received vancomycin 1 g X 1 and zosyn. On arrival to the ICU, the patient he was intubated and sedated with markedly distended abdomen. . ROS: unable to obtain  Past Medical History: * ESRD on HD (L AVF), dialyzed MWF * Left ___ s/p evacuation on ___ * CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago * ? AAA * Hypothyroidism * Hypercholesterolemia * s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and a small aortic aneurysm (4.4 cm), followed by vascular surgery in the past  Social History: ___ Family History: Family History: unable to obtain  Physical Exam: expired  Pertinent Results: ___ 12:17PM URINE ___ BACTERIA-RARE YEAST-NONE EPI-0 ___ 12:17PM URINE BLOOD-SM NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-30 GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-NEG PH-7.0 LEUK-NEG ___ 12:17PM URINE COLOR-Yellow APPEAR-Clear SP ___ ___ 12:24PM LACTATE-8.2* K+-7.3* ___ 12:45PM ___ ___ 12:45PM ___ PTT-72.0* ___ ___ 12:45PM PLT COUNT-323 ___ 12:45PM NEUTS-93.0* LYMPHS-4.5* MONOS-2.4 EOS-0.1 BASOS-0.1 ___ 12:45PM WBC-22.4*# RBC-3.90* HGB-12.5* HCT-41.6 MCV-107* MCH-32.0 MCHC-30.0* RDW-15.6* ___ 12:45PM ALBUMIN-2.7* CALCIUM-9.5 PHOSPHATE-5.4* MAGNESIUM-4.1* ___ 12:45PM CK-MB-8 cTropnT-0.19* ___ 12:45PM LIPASE-9 ___ 12:45PM ALT(SGPT)-123* AST(SGOT)-226* LD(___)-777* CK(CPK)-552* ALK PHOS-173* AMYLASE-132* TOT BILI-0.3 ___ 12:45PM estGFR-Using this ___ 12:45PM GLUCOSE-290* UREA N-72* CREAT-6.3* SODIUM-144 POTASSIUM-6.2* CHLORIDE-103 TOTAL CO2-18* ANION GAP-29* ___ 01:12PM GLUCOSE-273* LACTATE-6.8* K+-5.8* ___ 02:28PM HGB-11.9* calcHCT-36 ___ 02:28PM GLUCOSE-181* LACTATE-5.2* K+-5.6* ___ 03:49PM ___ PTT-58.9* ___ ___ 06:08PM LACTATE-2.8* ___ 06:08PM TYPE-ART PO2-289* PCO2-31* PH-7.42 TOTAL CO2-21 BASE XS--2 ___ 06:29PM ALBUMIN-3.0* CALCIUM-9.4 ___ 06:29PM LIPASE-10 ___ 06:29PM ALT(SGPT)-398* AST(SGOT)-732* LD(LDH)-875* ALK PHOS-175* AMYLASE-165* TOT BILI-0.4 CT head (___): FINDINGS: Again demonstrated is a hypodense left subdural collection, not significantly changed in size. There has been interval decrease in the amount of pneumocephalus and rightward shift of midline structures, now measuring 4 mm. There is overall less edema and mass effect, as the sulci are better defined on this study. There is no intraparenchymal hemorrhage or right extra- axial collection. Basal cisterns are patent. Burr holes within the left frontal and parietal calvarium are unchanged. IMPRESSION: Left subdural collection, unchanged in size, with slight decrease in rightward shift of midline structures and sulcal effacement. CT thorax ___ BASES: There are small bilateral pleural effusions, with associated atelectasis. Pacer wires are incompletely imaged on this study. Median sternotomy and surgical clips are consistent with history of CABG. There is no pericardial effusion. CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: The liver, gallbladder, spleen, and adrenals are normal. The kidneys are atrophic, with multiple vascular calcifications, unchanged. Pancreas is atrophic. Nasogastric tube terminates just distal to the gastroesophageal junction. There is fluid within the dependent portion of the esophagus (2:4). Small bowel is unremarkable. The colon diffusely is markedly distended, measuring up to 7.7 cm in diameter, and contains a large amount of fecal material, particularly in the rectum. There is no pneumatosis, bowel wall thickening, or abnormal bowel wall enhancement. There is no free fluid or free air within the abdomen. There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The aorta demonstrates marked atherosclerotic calcifications and noncalcified ulcerative plaques. The origins of the celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are calcified but patent. The inferior mesenteric artery which arises from the aortic aneurysm, does not opacify. Infrarenal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, is stable and demonstrates peripheral thrombus. The iliac arteries are heavily calcified but patent as visualized. CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: There is significant amount of fecal material throughout the colon. The rectum is distended with stool. There is a Foley catheter within the urinary bladder, with air in the non-dependent portion of the urinary bladder. There is no free pelvic fluid, and no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. BONE WINDOWS: There are multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine and calcified intervertebral discs of the thoracolumbar junction. IMPRESSION: 1. No secondary signs of bowel ischemia. 2. The inferior mesenteric artery does not opacify, and originates from the infrarenal aortic aneurysm. The celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are heavily calcified but patent. 3. Stable infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, and demonstrating peripheral thrombus. 4. Distended colon measuring up to 7.7 cm, containing abundant amount of fecal material. EKG: afib ~105. IVCD. LAD. no ischemic changes.  Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ y/o man with ESRD on HD and SDH s/p recent evacuation per NSG admitted with altered mental status. . # Shock, likely septic in etiology: Potential sources include urine (though only ___ WBCs with culture pending) and intra-abdominal. Should also consider CNS source with recent instrumentation. Of these most concerning would be mesenteric ischemia secondary to atrial fibrillation - aggressive volume repletion to CVP ___ - continue vasopressors as necessary - surgery consult for acute abdomen - broad spectrum antibiotics Surgical consult revealed a massively distended, diffusely tender abdomen with increase tenderness in left lower quadrant with guarding and rebound tenderness. His lactate level was 8 and he was hemodynamically unstable. I discussion was held with his 3 children. The very poor prognosis, even with surgical intervention was conveyed to them, but they wished to proceed with an exploratory laparotomy. The patient was found to have a completely necrotic sigmoid colon from the distal descending colon to the peritoneal reflection despite good vascular pulses in the mesentary. The transverse colon was massively dilated, but viable. There were two patchy areas of complete full thickness ischemia in the distal terminal ileum. Once again there were strong pulses leading to these ischemia areas. A total abdominal colectomy with resection of the ischemic ileum in continuity was performed and an end ileostomy was made. The patient was taken intubated and on pressors to the ICU for recovery. He remained unstable for 2 days, and then became hemodynamically refractile to pressors with increasing acidosis. A family conference was held< and he was made CMO.  . # Altered mental status: likely related to abdominal sepsis in compromised host. no clear change in head imaging per Neurosurg to explain. - Neurosurgery team following . # Respiratory failure: intubated for airway protection during altered mental status. currently oxygenation well on current vent settings. - continue current vent settings with wean FIO2 by sats - VAP prevention bundle - PPI . # ESRD on HD: - Renal team consulted from ED . # Elevated INR: Unclear if drawn from heparinized line or due to primary coagulopathy (i.e., DIC). Platelets normal so DIC less likely. Received vitamin K and FFP in the ED with plan for reversal to < 1.4 per NSG. - repeat coags . # Hepatitis: likely related to shock liver from sepsis # FEN: NPO . # ACCESS: Right femoral line, AV fistula . # PPx: no heparin given, BID PPI, no need for bowel meds currently . # CODE: CMO . # COMMUNICATION: with daughters and son . # DISPO: ICU care On ___ Patient made CMO after discussion with his daughters and son. Pt expired soon after extubation. Family meeting was conducted, all questions/concerns answered.  Medications on Admission: renagel 800 TID with meals levothyroxine 50 mcg daily amlodipine 5 mg daily simvastatin 10 mg daily omeprazole 20 mg daily terazosin 2 mg QHS furosemide 40 mg BID tylenol ___ mg PO q4h prn ri-gel suspension 30 mL q4h prn dyspepsia bisacodyl 10 mg PR prn MVI daily keppra 500 mg BID senna 8.6 mg bid hep 5000 U SC TID colace 100 mg BID oxycodone 5 mg PO prn pain oxycodone 10 mg PO prn severe pain zofran 4 mg PO q8h prn  Discharge Medications: none  Discharge Disposition: Expired  Discharge Diagnosis: End stage renal disease. Left subdural hematome, s/p evacuation on ___ CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago Hypothyroidism Hypercholesterolemia s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and AAA. Death secondary to multiorgan failure and respiratory insufficiency.  Discharge Condition: expired  Discharge Instructions: none  Followup Instructions: ___

Discharge Instructions: You were admitted to the hospital with a small bowel obstruction. You were taken to the operating room and underwent an exploratory laparatomy with lysis of adhesions and a small bowel resection. You bowel function has returned after the surgery and you are now being discharged with the following instructions: Please follow up in ___ clinic at the appointment sdcheduled for you below. Your staples will be removed at this appointment. ACTIVITY:  Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and feel you could respond in an emergency. You may climb stairs. You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you see your surgeon at your next visit. Don't lift more than ___ lbs for ___ weeks. (This is about the weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to lifting children, but they may sit on your lap. You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable. You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you can resume tub baths or swimming. Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common sense and go slowly at first.  HOW YOU MAY FEEL:  You may feel weak or washed out for 6 weeks. You might want to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you. You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your throat during surgery. You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. You might feel somewhat depressed. You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem unappealing. All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.  YOUR INCISION: Your incision may be slightly red around the staples. This is normal. You may gently wash away dried material around your incision. It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This will go away. Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area. Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told otherwise. You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call your surgeon. You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may resume tub baths or swimming. Ove the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become less prominent.  YOUR BOWELS: Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these medicines without a prescription. If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving the bowels, call your surgeon.   PAIN MANAGEMENT: It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal surgery. This pain is often described as soreness. Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon. You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directied. Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more medicine at one time than prescribed. Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your pain gets too severe. Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your surgeon has said its okay. If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of pain medicine. Remember to use your cough pillow for splinting when you cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises. If you experience any of the folloiwng, please contact your surgeon: - sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours - pain that is getting worse over time - pain accompanied by fever of more than 101 - a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain  MEDICATIONS: Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as you did before, unless you have been told differently. In some cases you will have a prescription for antibiotics or other medication. If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to take, please call your surgeon.  DANGER SIGNS: Please call your surgeon if you develop: - worsening abdominal pain - sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours - temperature of 101 degrees or higher - severe diarrhea - vomiting - redness around the incision that is spreading - increased swelling around the incision - excessive bruising around the incision - cloudy fluid coming from the wound - bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the wound - an increase in drainage from the wound  Followup Instructions: ___

fields.入院情况: History of Present Illness: ___ presents w/ 5 days of abdominal pain. The pain was diffuse throughout the abdomen and described as sharp and constant, then worsened more on the L side of abdomen. There was associated nausea and dry heaves. She also noted abdominal distension and she started to vomit yesterday, small bilious amounts. She continued to have emesis this AM and multiple times in the ED. Her last bowel movement was yesterday, after she gave herself an enema. Prior to that, she had a bowel movement on ___. She has continued to pass flatus up to today. In the ED, an NGT was placed, which initially put out 900cc of bilious fluid. KUB showed obstruction. She denies fevers at home.  Past Medical History: Past Medical History: Paget's disease, anemia, allergic rhinitis, borderline hypertension, DVT ___, hypercholesterolemia, hypothyroidism, psychosis psychotic break, urinary frequency, uterine prolapse Past Surgical History: tubal ligation, R hip replacement ___, meniscal tear, breast surgery 1950s, nose surgery, vaginal hysterectomy ___  Social History: ___ Family History: non-contributory  Physical Exam: On admission: Vitals: 98 97 171/98 22 96% GEN: A&O, NAD HEENT: No scleral icterus, mucus membranes moist CV: RRR PULM: Clear to auscultation b/l, No W/R/R ABD: Soft, distended, tympanitic, tender to palpation on L side, +rebound in LLQ DRE: normal tone, no gross or occult blood Ext: No ___ edema, ___ warm and well perfused
fields.诊疗经过: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted on ___ under the Acute Care Surgical service for management of her small bowel obstruction. She was taken urgently to the operating room and underwent laparoscopic converted to open, adhesiolysis and small-bowel resection (see operative note by Dr. ___ details). She tolerated the procedure well and was extubated upon completion and transferred to the PACU for recovery. She was subsequently transferred to the surgical floor. She was kept NPO with an NG tube for decompression and given IV fluids for hydration. On ___ the patient self d/c'd her NG tube. On ___ she had evidence of bowel function and was started on a clear liquid diet, which was slowly advanced as tolerated to a regular diet. A foley catheter was placed perioperatively for urine output monitoring. This was removed on ___, but was subsequently reinserted for urinary retention. She was noted to have hallucinations during her postoperative course as well as some tachycardia and hypertension. It was unclear whether the cause of this was narcotics, benzo withdrawal from her regular home medications (on clonazepam at home), or if it was related to her history of schizoaffective disorder. Psychiatry was consulted given her waxing and waning mental status with concerns about confusion with disorientation. Her home medications were restarted when tolerating PO's. Medical work up for causes of delirium was initiated. Her electrolytes were monitored and repleted as needed. Her WBC trended down to normal and her u/a and urine culture were negative. ECG showed NSR with no changes. She was started on metoprolol for hypertension and tachycardia. On ___ some erythema and drainage was noted from the inferior portion of her surgical incision and a couple staples were removed to allow drainage. Daily dressing changes were performed. She was encouraged to mobilize out of bed and ambulate as tolerated. Physical therapy was consulted and have recommended rehab after her acute hospital stay.

sub_contents.sub_content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: altered mental status  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ intubation  History of Present Illness: HPI: Mr. ___ is an ___ y/o man with PMH notable for ESRD on HD and recent ___ s/p Burr hole sent to the ED for altered mental status. He was at his nursing home and found to have altered mental status at 8:30 am. At that time, T 96.5, P ___, BP 120/70, RR 40, 93% on NRB and 88% on NC (later reported 83% on RA). He was diaphoretic at the time FSBS 145. Per NH reports, he had dialysis on ___. He was sent to the ___ emergency room for further evaluation. There, he was noted to have a rectal temp 100.7, RR 30, P ___, and BP 112/90. His O2 sat was 98% on NRB. CXR showed left base atelectasis versus infiltrate; CT head demonstrated SDH and he was then transferred to ___ for further evaluation with neurosurgery. . In the ED, initial vitals BP 85/48, HR 110, RR 24, 100% on NRB. He was found to be hyperkalemic and received calcium gluconate, D50/insulin. Due to decreased mental status, he was intubated with 20 mg etomidate and 40 mg rocuronium. He was hypotensive to the ___ and started on levophed gtt. Right femoral CVL was placed for IV access. He was noted to have dark brown output (400 cc in total) from his NGT. He received 2 U FFP and vitamin K as well as profilin (activated factor 9). He received vancomycin 1 g X 1 and zosyn. On arrival to the ICU, the patient he was intubated and sedated with markedly distended abdomen. . ROS: unable to obtain  Past Medical History: * ESRD on HD (L AVF), dialyzed MWF * Left ___ s/p evacuation on ___ * CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago * ? AAA * Hypothyroidism * Hypercholesterolemia * s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and a small aortic aneurysm (4.4 cm), followed by vascular surgery in the past  Social History: ___ Family History: Family History: unable to obtain  Physical Exam: expired  Pertinent Results: ___ 12:17PM URINE ___ BACTERIA-RARE YEAST-NONE EPI-0 ___ 12:17PM URINE BLOOD-SM NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-30 GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-NEG PH-7.0 LEUK-NEG ___ 12:17PM URINE COLOR-Yellow APPEAR-Clear SP ___ ___ 12:24PM LACTATE-8.2* K+-7.3* ___ 12:45PM ___ ___ 12:45PM ___ PTT-72.0* ___ ___ 12:45PM PLT COUNT-323 ___ 12:45PM NEUTS-93.0* LYMPHS-4.5* MONOS-2.4 EOS-0.1 BASOS-0.1 ___ 12:45PM WBC-22.4*# RBC-3.90* HGB-12.5* HCT-41.6 MCV-107* MCH-32.0 MCHC-30.0* RDW-15.6* ___ 12:45PM ALBUMIN-2.7* CALCIUM-9.5 PHOSPHATE-5.4* MAGNESIUM-4.1* ___ 12:45PM CK-MB-8 cTropnT-0.19* ___ 12:45PM LIPASE-9 ___ 12:45PM ALT(SGPT)-123* AST(SGOT)-226* LD(___)-777* CK(CPK)-552* ALK PHOS-173* AMYLASE-132* TOT BILI-0.3 ___ 12:45PM estGFR-Using this ___ 12:45PM GLUCOSE-290* UREA N-72* CREAT-6.3* SODIUM-144 POTASSIUM-6.2* CHLORIDE-103 TOTAL CO2-18* ANION GAP-29* ___ 01:12PM GLUCOSE-273* LACTATE-6.8* K+-5.8* ___ 02:28PM HGB-11.9* calcHCT-36 ___ 02:28PM GLUCOSE-181* LACTATE-5.2* K+-5.6* ___ 03:49PM ___ PTT-58.9* ___ ___ 06:08PM LACTATE-2.8* ___ 06:08PM TYPE-ART PO2-289* PCO2-31* PH-7.42 TOTAL CO2-21 BASE XS--2 ___ 06:29PM ALBUMIN-3.0* CALCIUM-9.4 ___ 06:29PM LIPASE-10 ___ 06:29PM ALT(SGPT)-398* AST(SGOT)-732* LD(LDH)-875* ALK PHOS-175* AMYLASE-165* TOT BILI-0.4 CT head (___): FINDINGS: Again demonstrated is a hypodense left subdural collection, not significantly changed in size. There has been interval decrease in the amount of pneumocephalus and rightward shift of midline structures, now measuring 4 mm. There is overall less edema and mass effect, as the sulci are better defined on this study. There is no intraparenchymal hemorrhage or right extra- axial collection. Basal cisterns are patent. Burr holes within the left frontal and parietal calvarium are unchanged. IMPRESSION: Left subdural collection, unchanged in size, with slight decrease in rightward shift of midline structures and sulcal effacement. CT thorax ___ BASES: There are small bilateral pleural effusions, with associated atelectasis. Pacer wires are incompletely imaged on this study. Median sternotomy and surgical clips are consistent with history of CABG. There is no pericardial effusion. CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: The liver, gallbladder, spleen, and adrenals are normal. The kidneys are atrophic, with multiple vascular calcifications, unchanged. Pancreas is atrophic. Nasogastric tube terminates just distal to the gastroesophageal junction. There is fluid within the dependent portion of the esophagus (2:4). Small bowel is unremarkable. The colon diffusely is markedly distended, measuring up to 7.7 cm in diameter, and contains a large amount of fecal material, particularly in the rectum. There is no pneumatosis, bowel wall thickening, or abnormal bowel wall enhancement. There is no free fluid or free air within the abdomen. There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The aorta demonstrates marked atherosclerotic calcifications and noncalcified ulcerative plaques. The origins of the celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are calcified but patent. The inferior mesenteric artery which arises from the aortic aneurysm, does not opacify. Infrarenal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, is stable and demonstrates peripheral thrombus. The iliac arteries are heavily calcified but patent as visualized. CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: There is significant amount of fecal material throughout the colon. The rectum is distended with stool. There is a Foley catheter within the urinary bladder, with air in the non-dependent portion of the urinary bladder. There is no free pelvic fluid, and no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. BONE WINDOWS: There are multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine and calcified intervertebral discs of the thoracolumbar junction. IMPRESSION: 1. No secondary signs of bowel ischemia. 2. The inferior mesenteric artery does not opacify, and originates from the infrarenal aortic aneurysm. The celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are heavily calcified but patent. 3. Stable infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, and demonstrating peripheral thrombus. 4. Distended colon measuring up to 7.7 cm, containing abundant amount of fecal material. EKG: afib ~105. IVCD. LAD. no ischemic changes.  Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ y/o man with ESRD on HD and SDH s/p recent evacuation per NSG admitted with altered mental status. . # Shock, likely septic in etiology: Potential sources include urine (though only ___ WBCs with culture pending) and intra-abdominal. Should also consider CNS source with recent instrumentation. Of these most concerning would be mesenteric ischemia secondary to atrial fibrillation - aggressive volume repletion to CVP ___ - continue vasopressors as necessary - surgery consult for acute abdomen - broad spectrum antibiotics Surgical consult revealed a massively distended, diffusely tender abdomen with increase tenderness in left lower quadrant with guarding and rebound tenderness. His lactate level was 8 and he was hemodynamically unstable. I discussion was held with his 3 children. The very poor prognosis, even with surgical intervention was conveyed to them, but they wished to proceed with an exploratory laparotomy. The patient was found to have a completely necrotic sigmoid colon from the distal descending colon to the peritoneal reflection despite good vascular pulses in the mesentary. The transverse colon was massively dilated, but viable. There were two patchy areas of complete full thickness ischemia in the distal terminal ileum. Once again there were strong pulses leading to these ischemia areas. A total abdominal colectomy with resection of the ischemic ileum in continuity was performed and an end ileostomy was made. The patient was taken intubated and on pressors to the ICU for recovery. He remained unstable for 2 days, and then became hemodynamically refractile to pressors with increasing acidosis. A family conference was held< and he was made CMO.  . # Altered mental status: likely related to abdominal sepsis in compromised host. no clear change in head imaging per Neurosurg to explain. - Neurosurgery team following . # Respiratory failure: intubated for airway protection during altered mental status. currently oxygenation well on current vent settings. - continue current vent settings with wean FIO2 by sats - VAP prevention bundle - PPI . # ESRD on HD: - Renal team consulted from ED . # Elevated INR: Unclear if drawn from heparinized line or due to primary coagulopathy (i.e., DIC). Platelets normal so DIC less likely. Received vitamin K and FFP in the ED with plan for reversal to < 1.4 per NSG. - repeat coags . # Hepatitis: likely related to shock liver from sepsis # FEN: NPO . # ACCESS: Right femoral line, AV fistula . # PPx: no heparin given, BID PPI, no need for bowel meds currently . # CODE: CMO . # COMMUNICATION: with daughters and son . # DISPO: ICU care On ___ Patient made CMO after discussion with his daughters and son. Pt expired soon after extubation. Family meeting was conducted, all questions/concerns answered.  Medications on Admission: renagel 800 TID with meals levothyroxine 50 mcg daily amlodipine 5 mg daily simvastatin 10 mg daily omeprazole 20 mg daily terazosin 2 mg QHS furosemide 40 mg BID tylenol ___ mg PO q4h prn ri-gel suspension 30 mL q4h prn dyspepsia bisacodyl 10 mg PR prn MVI daily keppra 500 mg BID senna 8.6 mg bid hep 5000 U SC TID colace 100 mg BID oxycodone 5 mg PO prn pain oxycodone 10 mg PO prn severe pain zofran 4 mg PO q8h prn  Discharge Medications: none  Discharge Disposition: Expired  Discharge Diagnosis: End stage renal disease. Left subdural hematome, s/p evacuation on ___ CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago Hypothyroidism Hypercholesterolemia s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and AAA. Death secondary to multiorgan failure and respiratory insufficiency.  Discharge Condition: expired  Discharge Instructions: none  Followup Instructions: ___

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Myalgia  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: Mr. ___ is a ___ with a history of EtOH/HBV/HDV cirrhosis, s/p TIPS (___), baseline MELD ___, currently being evaluated for liver transplantation as an outpatient who presents to the ED with myalgias and found to have hyperkalemia. Patient reports muscle pain and cramping of at least ___ years duration. Came to ED today due to worsening. Notably, per PCP note from ___, pt was involved in a MVA several weeks ago and has had worsened low back and shoulder pain with some sciatic symptoms of RLE. He was getting ___ as an outpatient. Involves bilateral arms and thighs. Endorses bilateral thigh weakness but no focal deficits. Some difficulty going up stairs. Denies headache, change in vision. In the ED his initial vitals were 7 99.4 88 148/83 16 100% RA. Patient was given 2L NS. Labs were shown to the K up to 5.7 which improved with NS. Also creatinine of 1.6 which is slightly worse than baseline of 1.3.  On arrival to the floor pt reports his muscle aches are improved.  Past Medical History: 1. MELD sodium score of 28 for hepatitis B cirrhosis and delta virus hep B cirrhosis. 2. Hepatocellular carcinoma with previous radiofrequency ablation of a 2.7 cm lesion in the liver on ___. 3. Followup of a lesion that was seen on ___, at 2.4 cm, increasing to 3 cm on ___, and now scheduled for radiofrequency ablation. ACHALASIA s/p esophageal dilation ___ HELICOBACTER PYLORI GASTRITIS treated with omeprazole, Biaxin and amoxicillin ___ - per PCP note, normal colonoscopy in ___ #HBV/HDV/ETOH Cirrhosis - ___ Class C; Complicated by grade 1 esophageal varices and 1.9 cm OPTN5a HCC s/p RFA ___ MELD baseline low ___, on entecavir/tenofovir since ___ --> monotherapy entecavir since ___ ongoing surveillance of segment VIII liver lesion  Social History: ___ Family History: His father died at age ___ due to GI bleeding. content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: UROLOGY  Allergies: No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: right testicular pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Right scrotal exploration, bilateral orchiopexy  History of Present Illness: ___ with no past medical history who presents with 9 hours of severe right testicular pain. The patient developed a dull, nagging pain of the right testis last night, but was able to go to sleep. He awoke this morning at 7AM with severe, ___ right testicular pain. Nothing made the pain better and it was worse with movement. He tried to go to work, but the pain persisted. He did not arrive to the ED for evaluation until this afternoon at 4pm. His exam was consistent with torsion, so a manual detorsion was attempted. An testicular ultrasound was then performed which showed no flow in the right testis. Urology was then consulted.  Past Medical History: None  Social History: ___ Family History: NC  Physical Exam: AVSS NAD Warm well perfused Unlabored breathing Abd soft, NT, ND Scrotal incisions c/d/i, no hematoma  Pertinent Results: ___ 05:15PM BLOOD WBC-6.8 RBC-5.32 Hgb-15.3 Hct-44.9 MCV-85 MCH-28.8 MCHC-34.0 RDW-13.7 Plt ___ ___ 05:15PM BLOOD Neuts-79.2* Lymphs-16.9* Monos-3.0 Eos-0.1 Baso-0.8 ___ 05:15PM BLOOD Glucose-103* UreaN-13 Creat-0.8 Na-141 K-3.8 Cl-103 HCO3-28 AnGap-14  Brief Hospital Course: The patient was emergently taken to the OR from the ED for right scrotal exploration and bilateral orchiopexy. The right testicle was ashen when the scrotum was opened, but on detorsing the color gradually improved and became less swollen so bilateral orchiopexy was performed. The patient recovered on the floor overnight and on POD1 his pain was well controlled, he was tolerating a diet, and incision was C/D/I without evidence of hematoma. He was discharged home with oral pain medication and instructions to wear supportive underwear for ___ weeks. He will follow up with Dr. ___ in ___ weeks.  Medications on Admission: None  Discharge Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H 2. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain RX *oxycodone 5 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth q4 hours Disp #*30 Tablet Refills:*0 3. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*30 Capsule Refills:*0  Discharge Disposition: Home  Discharge Diagnosis: Right testicular torsion  Discharge Condition: Mental Status: Clear and coherent. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: -You may take Tylenol or narcotic for pain control. Use Tylenol, replace Tylenol with prescribed narcotic (oxycodone) for break through pain >4. Please note the maximum dose of Tylenol per day is 4 gms. -Please do not drive or consume alcohol while taking pain medications -Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation and constipation related to narcotic pain medication, discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. You may use milk of magnesia to help you have a bowel movement. -Apply Bacitracin ointment to your incision twice a day -You may shower 2 days after surgery, but do not tub bathe, swim, soak, or scrub incision for 2 weeks -Wear scrotal support (tight fitting underwear) for 1 week.  -Allow bandage strips to fall off over time, remove all remaining dressings in 2 days  -No heavy lifting for 4 weeks (no more than 10 pounds)  Followup Instructions: ___

Nothing made the pain better and it was worse with movement. He tried to go to work, but the pain persisted. He did not arrive to the ED for evaluation until this afternoon at 4pm. The right testicle was ashen when the scrotum was opened, but on detorsing the color gradually improved and became less swollen so bilateral orchiopexy was performed. The patient recovered on the floor overnight and on POD1 his pain was well controlled, he was tolerating a diet, and incision was C/D/I without evidence of hematoma. He was discharged home with oral pain medication and instructions to wear supportive underwear for ___ weeks. He will follow up with Dr. ___ in ___ weeks.

content: Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M  Service: SURGERY  Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs  Attending: ___  Chief Complaint: altered mental status  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: ___ intubation  History of Present Illness: HPI: Mr. ___ is an ___ y/o man with PMH notable for ESRD on HD and recent ___ s/p Burr hole sent to the ED for altered mental status. He was at his nursing home and found to have altered mental status at 8:30 am. At that time, T 96.5, P ___, BP 120/70, RR 40, 93% on NRB and 88% on NC (later reported 83% on RA). He was diaphoretic at the time FSBS 145. Per NH reports, he had dialysis on ___. He was sent to the ___ emergency room for further evaluation. There, he was noted to have a rectal temp 100.7, RR 30, P ___, and BP 112/90. His O2 sat was 98% on NRB. CXR showed left base atelectasis versus infiltrate; CT head demonstrated SDH and he was then transferred to ___ for further evaluation with neurosurgery. . In the ED, initial vitals BP 85/48, HR 110, RR 24, 100% on NRB. He was found to be hyperkalemic and received calcium gluconate, D50/insulin. Due to decreased mental status, he was intubated with 20 mg etomidate and 40 mg rocuronium. He was hypotensive to the ___ and started on levophed gtt. Right femoral CVL was placed for IV access. He was noted to have dark brown output (400 cc in total) from his NGT. He received 2 U FFP and vitamin K as well as profilin (activated factor 9). He received vancomycin 1 g X 1 and zosyn. On arrival to the ICU, the patient he was intubated and sedated with markedly distended abdomen. . ROS: unable to obtain  Past Medical History: * ESRD on HD (L AVF), dialyzed MWF * Left ___ s/p evacuation on ___ * CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago * ? AAA * Hypothyroidism * Hypercholesterolemia * s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and a small aortic aneurysm (4.4 cm), followed by vascular surgery in the past  Social History: ___ Family History: Family History: unable to obtain  Physical Exam: expired  Pertinent Results: ___ 12:17PM URINE ___ BACTERIA-RARE YEAST-NONE EPI-0 ___ 12:17PM URINE BLOOD-SM NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-30 GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-NEG PH-7.0 LEUK-NEG ___ 12:17PM URINE COLOR-Yellow APPEAR-Clear SP ___ ___ 12:24PM LACTATE-8.2* K+-7.3* ___ 12:45PM ___ ___ 12:45PM ___ PTT-72.0* ___ ___ 12:45PM PLT COUNT-323 ___ 12:45PM NEUTS-93.0* LYMPHS-4.5* MONOS-2.4 EOS-0.1 BASOS-0.1 ___ 12:45PM WBC-22.4*# RBC-3.90* HGB-12.5* HCT-41.6 MCV-107* MCH-32.0 MCHC-30.0* RDW-15.6* ___ 12:45PM ALBUMIN-2.7* CALCIUM-9.5 PHOSPHATE-5.4* MAGNESIUM-4.1* ___ 12:45PM CK-MB-8 cTropnT-0.19* ___ 12:45PM LIPASE-9 ___ 12:45PM ALT(SGPT)-123* AST(SGOT)-226* LD(___)-777* CK(CPK)-552* ALK PHOS-173* AMYLASE-132* TOT BILI-0.3 ___ 12:45PM estGFR-Using this ___ 12:45PM GLUCOSE-290* UREA N-72* CREAT-6.3* SODIUM-144 POTASSIUM-6.2* CHLORIDE-103 TOTAL CO2-18* ANION GAP-29* ___ 01:12PM GLUCOSE-273* LACTATE-6.8* K+-5.8* ___ 02:28PM HGB-11.9* calcHCT-36 ___ 02:28PM GLUCOSE-181* LACTATE-5.2* K+-5.6* ___ 03:49PM ___ PTT-58.9* ___ ___ 06:08PM LACTATE-2.8* ___ 06:08PM TYPE-ART PO2-289* PCO2-31* PH-7.42 TOTAL CO2-21 BASE XS--2 ___ 06:29PM ALBUMIN-3.0* CALCIUM-9.4 ___ 06:29PM LIPASE-10 ___ 06:29PM ALT(SGPT)-398* AST(SGOT)-732* LD(LDH)-875* ALK PHOS-175* AMYLASE-165* TOT BILI-0.4 CT head (___): FINDINGS: Again demonstrated is a hypodense left subdural collection, not significantly changed in size. There has been interval decrease in the amount of pneumocephalus and rightward shift of midline structures, now measuring 4 mm. There is overall less edema and mass effect, as the sulci are better defined on this study. There is no intraparenchymal hemorrhage or right extra- axial collection. Basal cisterns are patent. Burr holes within the left frontal and parietal calvarium are unchanged. IMPRESSION: Left subdural collection, unchanged in size, with slight decrease in rightward shift of midline structures and sulcal effacement. CT thorax ___ BASES: There are small bilateral pleural effusions, with associated atelectasis. Pacer wires are incompletely imaged on this study. Median sternotomy and surgical clips are consistent with history of CABG. There is no pericardial effusion. CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: The liver, gallbladder, spleen, and adrenals are normal. The kidneys are atrophic, with multiple vascular calcifications, unchanged. Pancreas is atrophic. Nasogastric tube terminates just distal to the gastroesophageal junction. There is fluid within the dependent portion of the esophagus (2:4). Small bowel is unremarkable. The colon diffusely is markedly distended, measuring up to 7.7 cm in diameter, and contains a large amount of fecal material, particularly in the rectum. There is no pneumatosis, bowel wall thickening, or abnormal bowel wall enhancement. There is no free fluid or free air within the abdomen. There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The aorta demonstrates marked atherosclerotic calcifications and noncalcified ulcerative plaques. The origins of the celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are calcified but patent. The inferior mesenteric artery which arises from the aortic aneurysm, does not opacify. Infrarenal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, is stable and demonstrates peripheral thrombus. The iliac arteries are heavily calcified but patent as visualized. CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST: There is significant amount of fecal material throughout the colon. The rectum is distended with stool. There is a Foley catheter within the urinary bladder, with air in the non-dependent portion of the urinary bladder. There is no free pelvic fluid, and no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. BONE WINDOWS: There are multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine and calcified intervertebral discs of the thoracolumbar junction. IMPRESSION: 1. No secondary signs of bowel ischemia. 2. The inferior mesenteric artery does not opacify, and originates from the infrarenal aortic aneurysm. The celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery are heavily calcified but patent. 3. Stable infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm, measuring up to 4.5 cm in diameter, and demonstrating peripheral thrombus. 4. Distended colon measuring up to 7.7 cm, containing abundant amount of fecal material. EKG: afib ~105. IVCD. LAD. no ischemic changes.  Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ y/o man with ESRD on HD and SDH s/p recent evacuation per NSG admitted with altered mental status. . # Shock, likely septic in etiology: Potential sources include urine (though only ___ WBCs with culture pending) and intra-abdominal. Should also consider CNS source with recent instrumentation. Of these most concerning would be mesenteric ischemia secondary to atrial fibrillation - aggressive volume repletion to CVP ___ - continue vasopressors as necessary - surgery consult for acute abdomen - broad spectrum antibiotics Surgical consult revealed a massively distended, diffusely tender abdomen with increase tenderness in left lower quadrant with guarding and rebound tenderness. His lactate level was 8 and he was hemodynamically unstable. I discussion was held with his 3 children. The very poor prognosis, even with surgical intervention was conveyed to them, but they wished to proceed with an exploratory laparotomy. The patient was found to have a completely necrotic sigmoid colon from the distal descending colon to the peritoneal reflection despite good vascular pulses in the mesentary. The transverse colon was massively dilated, but viable. There were two patchy areas of complete full thickness ischemia in the distal terminal ileum. Once again there were strong pulses leading to these ischemia areas. A total abdominal colectomy with resection of the ischemic ileum in continuity was performed and an end ileostomy was made. The patient was taken intubated and on pressors to the ICU for recovery. He remained unstable for 2 days, and then became hemodynamically refractile to pressors with increasing acidosis. A family conference was held< and he was made CMO.  . # Altered mental status: likely related to abdominal sepsis in compromised host. no clear change in head imaging per Neurosurg to explain. - Neurosurgery team following . # Respiratory failure: intubated for airway protection during altered mental status. currently oxygenation well on current vent settings. - continue current vent settings with wean FIO2 by sats - VAP prevention bundle - PPI . # ESRD on HD: - Renal team consulted from ED . # Elevated INR: Unclear if drawn from heparinized line or due to primary coagulopathy (i.e., DIC). Platelets normal so DIC less likely. Received vitamin K and FFP in the ED with plan for reversal to < 1.4 per NSG. - repeat coags . # Hepatitis: likely related to shock liver from sepsis # FEN: NPO . # ACCESS: Right femoral line, AV fistula . # PPx: no heparin given, BID PPI, no need for bowel meds currently . # CODE: CMO . # COMMUNICATION: with daughters and son . # DISPO: ICU care On ___ Patient made CMO after discussion with his daughters and son. Pt expired soon after extubation. Family meeting was conducted, all questions/concerns answered.  Medications on Admission: renagel 800 TID with meals levothyroxine 50 mcg daily amlodipine 5 mg daily simvastatin 10 mg daily omeprazole 20 mg daily terazosin 2 mg QHS furosemide 40 mg BID tylenol ___ mg PO q4h prn ri-gel suspension 30 mL q4h prn dyspepsia bisacodyl 10 mg PR prn MVI daily keppra 500 mg BID senna 8.6 mg bid hep 5000 U SC TID colace 100 mg BID oxycodone 5 mg PO prn pain oxycodone 10 mg PO prn severe pain zofran 4 mg PO q8h prn  Discharge Medications: none  Discharge Disposition: Expired  Discharge Diagnosis: End stage renal disease. Left subdural hematome, s/p evacuation on ___ CAD s/p MI and pacemaker placement, CABG ___ years ago Hypothyroidism Hypercholesterolemia s/p left carotid endarterectomy, with right carotid stenosis and AAA. Death secondary to multiorgan failure and respiratory insufficiency.  Discharge Condition: expired  Discharge Instructions: none  Followup Instructions: ___  Name: ___ Unit No: ___  Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___  Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F  Service: MEDICINE  Allergies: Ciprofloxacin / Dilaudid / Remicade  Attending: ___.  Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain  Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: None  History of Present Illness: ___ MEDICINE ATTENDING ADMISSION NOTE .  Date: ___ Time:___ _ ________________________________________________________________ PCP: Name: ___ MD Email: ___ _ ________________________________________________________________ HPI: The patient is a ___ year old female with a long standing history of Crohn's disease on humira who has chonic RLQ pain and then developed LUQ over the course of 3 days. Her pain is worsened by eating and drinking, even drinking water. It is also worsened by moving. She rates the pain as ___. She has not had a bm for 2 weeks. She was taking senna, colace, golytely for two weeks and then she was straining to have a bm today which made the pain worse, then she had sensation that something in the LUQ popped and a severe onset of LUQ associated with dizziness, nausea and blood in toilet. She has been having vaginal and rectal bleeding for the past three weeks but no stool until today which was a small amount. CT ABDOMEN: CT Abdomen: There are small bilateral pleural effusions and dependent atelectasis at both lung bases.  The liver enhances homogeneously. There are no focal concerning liver lesions. There is no intra or extrahepatic biliary dilatation. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. Gallbladder is normal. The spleen has normal attenuation and enhancement. The pancreas and the spleen enhance homogeneously. There is no evidence of splenic infarct. The kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast promptly. There is no ascites or abdominal adenopathy. The aorta and its major branches are patent. The stomach and proximal loops of bowel have normal caliber and appearance. Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT / PLAN: ___ year old female with h/o Crohn's on ___ admitted in ___ admitted with LUQ pain . #ABDOMINAL PAIN: The patient's pain was likely due to constipation versus functional. She had an MRI pelvis that did not demonstrate new fistula formation or abscess. She had a CT abdomen that did not demonstrate significant colitis or acute pathology. CXR did not demonstrate thoracic pathology such as pneumonia or rib fracture. Her inflammatory markers were normal. She was afebrile with negative urine and blood cultures. She reported not having a bowel movement in over ten days. She was given a bowel regimen of senna/colase/miralax. She was also given 1 liter of golytely, which was effective in producing multiple bowel movements resulting in improvement in her symptoms. #FREE TUBAL LIGATION CLIP: CT scan demonstrated that the right tubal ligation clip was likely dislodged. This was discussed with the patient, including the need for alternative contraception. She was also scheduled for follow up with OB-GYN. #CROHN DISEASE: She was continued on humira. #IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA: Her hematocrit was stable. She was continued on supplemental iron. Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive. Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.  Discharge Instructions: It was a pleasure to participate in your care. You were admitted with abdominal pain. You had an MRI that did not show any new fistulas. You had a CT scan that was also negative. You were seen by the GI specialists. Constipation may have been contributing to your symptoms so it is important to take medication to prevent this. If you don't have a bowel movement despite taking these medications please contact your GI doctor ___ Dr. ___ for further instructions. Your symptoms improved and you were discharged home. Importantly, as we discussed, the CT scan showed that one of the tubal ligation clips has dislodged. It may be possible for you to become pregnant at this time so we recommend using an alternative form of contraception. We have arranged for a follow up appointment with your OB-GYN doctor. He was at his nursing home and found to have altered mental status at 8:30 am. At that time, T 96.5, P ___, BP 120/70, RR 40, 93% on NRB and 88% on NC (later reported 83% on RA). He was diaphoretic at the time FSBS 145. Per NH reports, he had dialysis on ___. He was sent to the ___ emergency room for further evaluation. There, he was noted to have a rectal temp 100.7, RR 30, P ___, and BP 112/90. His O2 sat was 98% on NRB. CXR showed left base atelectasis versus infiltrate; CT head demonstrated SDH and he was then transferred to ___ for further evaluation with neurosurgery. . In the ED, initial vitals BP 85/48, HR 110, RR 24, 100% on NRB. He was found to be hyperkalemic and received calcium gluconate, D50/insulin. Due to decreased mental status, he was intubated with 20 mg etomidate and 40 mg rocuronium. He was hypotensive to the ___ and started on levophed gtt. Right femoral CVL was placed for IV access. He was noted to have dark brown output (400 cc in total) from his NGT. He received 2 U FFP and vitamin K as well as profilin (activated factor 9). He received vancomycin 1 g X 1 and zosyn. On arrival to the ICU, the patient he was intubated and sedated with markedly distended abdomen. .
